### F425: Mobile release generator accepted invalid minimum supported version policy

- Area: Mobile App / release manifest generation and mandatory update policy
- Environment: `frontend/scripts/generate-mobile-version.js`, generated `mobile-version.json`, generated `mobileRelease.ts`, and native App update prompt policy
- Steps: Run the mobile release metadata generator with `MOBILE_MIN_SUPPORTED_VERSION_CODE` set to a negative number, non-integer, `NaN`, or a value greater than the selected release `versionCode`.
- Expected: Minimum supported version metadata should be a non-negative safe integer and should not exceed the release version code it is published with. Invalid policy should fail generation before a manifest can be served to App clients.
- Actual: The generator copied `Number(process.env.MOBILE_MIN_SUPPORTED_VERSION_CODE || 0)` directly into the manifest. Misconfigured values could emit `NaN`, negative values, fractional values, or a minimum-supported version above the current release version, making native update policy misleading or self-contradictory.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 10:44 UTC found `minSupportedVersionCode` was converted with `Number(...)` inside the manifest object and had no validation before writing `mobile-version.json` or `mobileRelease.ts`.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 10:44 UTC static source review confirmed `generate-mobile-version.js` now parses `minSupportedVersionCode` once, rejects non-safe/non-integer/negative values, and rejects values greater than the release `versionCode` before writing generated mobile release metadata. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/APK signing/artifact inspection/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: Mobile release manifests can no longer publish invalid or impossible minimum-supported-version policy from a bad environment variable.

### F424: Mobile release manifest generator allowed version metadata to drift from the selected APK

- Area: Mobile App / release manifest generation and APK update metadata integrity
- Environment: `frontend/scripts/generate-mobile-version.js`, generated `frontend/public/downloads/mobile-version.json`, and generated `frontend/src/generated/mobileRelease.ts`
- Steps: Run the mobile release metadata generator with `MOBILE_VERSION_NAME` or `MOBILE_VERSION_CODE` overriding the values derived from the selected latest `shoptest-x.y.z.apk`.
- Expected: Generated mobile release metadata should describe the actual APK file whose URL, size, and SHA-256 are written to the manifest. Version name and version code should match the selected versioned APK so App update comparisons and operator checks remain trustworthy.
- Actual: The generator accepted arbitrary `MOBILE_VERSION_NAME` and numeric `MOBILE_VERSION_CODE` values while still using `apkUrl`, `fileName`, `sizeBytes`, and `sha256` from the latest versioned APK file. A misconfigured environment could publish a manifest where the displayed/update version did not match the downloadable APK.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 10:41 UTC found `versionName` and `versionCode` were read from optional environment overrides after selecting `latestApk`, but there was no validation tying those fields back to `latestApk.versionName` or the `major * 10000 + minor * 100 + patch` version-code convention.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 10:41 UTC static source review confirmed `generate-mobile-version.js` now rejects manifests when `MOBILE_VERSION_NAME` differs from the selected APK version, when `MOBILE_VERSION_CODE` is not a positive safe integer, or when the version code does not match the selected semantic version. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/APK signing/artifact inspection/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: Mobile release metadata generation now fails closed instead of emitting a manifest whose version identity does not match the APK file it points to.

### F423: Mobile update manifest accepted mismatched app/platform metadata

- Area: Mobile App / update manifest ownership and storefront Android download gate
- Environment: `frontend/src/utils/mobileUpdate.ts`, fetched `mobile-version.json`, Navbar Android download link, and native App update prompt
- Steps: Serve a stale or wrong-environment mobile manifest with a valid-looking version/signing payload but a different `appId` or non-Android `platform`, then let the storefront or native App fetch it.
- Expected: The App/download runtime should only trust mobile release manifests for the current ShopTest Android app ID and Android platform before comparing versions or exposing a download/update action.
- Actual: Manifest normalization only required a valid positive `versionCode`. A wrong-app or wrong-platform manifest with `releaseSigned: true` and acceptable certificate metadata could still flow into the Navbar download URL or native update prompt.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 10:36 UTC found `normalizeReleaseManifest(...)` defaulted platform to `android`, copied `appId`, and returned the manifest without checking either field against `CURRENT_MOBILE_RELEASE.appId`.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 10:36 UTC static source review confirmed `mobileUpdate.ts` now rejects fetched manifests unless `platform` is Android and `appId` matches the current generated mobile release app ID (`com.shoptest.mobile` fallback). No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/APK signing/artifact inspection/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: Storefront and native App mobile release handling now fails closed on stale or wrong-app manifests before showing APK downloads or update prompts.

### F422: Mobile update runtime gate accepted malformed release certificate fingerprints

- Area: Mobile App / release manifest download gate and Android update prompt
- Environment: `frontend/src/utils/mobileUpdate.ts`, fetched `mobile-version.json`, Navbar Android download link, and native App update prompt
- Steps: Serve a mobile release manifest with `releaseSigned: true` but a malformed non-empty `certificateSha256` such as `release`, `1234`, or any value that is not a normalized 64-character SHA-256 fingerprint, then let the storefront/App fetch the manifest.
- Expected: The runtime download/update gate should enforce the same certificate fingerprint requirement as the F420 release metadata generator: release-signed manifests need a 64-character SHA-256 fingerprint and must not use the known Android Debug certificate.
- Actual: `isMobileReleaseDownloadAllowed(...)` only required a non-empty certificate fingerprint and rejected the known debug fingerprint. A malformed non-debug value could still satisfy the runtime gate if a remote or stale manifest claimed `releaseSigned: true`.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 10:33 UTC found `isMobileReleaseDownloadAllowed(...)` normalized `certificateSha256`, checked only non-empty/debug equality, then returned `release.releaseSigned === true`.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 10:33 UTC static source review confirmed `mobileUpdate.ts` now requires a normalized 64-character uppercase hex SHA-256 fingerprint and rejects the Android Debug fingerprint before allowing storefront download links or native update downloads. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/APK signing/artifact inspection/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: Runtime App/mobile release download gating now matches the generator-side release certificate contract and fails closed on malformed certificate metadata.

### F421: Admin dashboard product images bypassed the shared product image resolver

- Area: Frontend / Admin Dashboard, operations first viewport image reliability
- Environment: `frontend/src/pages/AdminDashboard.tsx`, top products table and low-stock product list
- Steps: Return dashboard `topProducts.imageUrl` or `lowStockList.imageUrl` with an unsafe or stale product image value, such as a raw-backslash URL, encoded `%5c` path, or missing asset, then open the admin dashboard.
- Expected: Admin dashboard product thumbnails should use the same shared product image resolver and fallback behavior as storefront/App product surfaces and other admin product image views.
- Actual: The dashboard rendered Ant Design `Avatar` components with `src={value}` and `src={item.imageUrl}` directly from backend dashboard data, bypassing `resolveProductImage(...)` and the shared F414/F419 image URL safety/fallback path.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 10:26 UTC found direct dashboard product image URL assignment in the top-products table and low-stock list while neighboring product/admin surfaces use `resolveProductImage(...)`.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 10:26 UTC static source review confirmed both dashboard product image surfaces now pass backend image values through `resolveProductImage(...)` before assigning them to `Avatar.src`. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: The admin operations dashboard now shares product image URL hardening and fallback behavior with the rest of the App/storefront/admin product image surfaces.

### F420: Mobile release manifest generator accepted invalid release-signing metadata

- Area: Mobile App / release manifest generation and APK download/update chain
- Environment: `frontend/scripts/generate-mobile-version.js`, `npm run build` prebuild, generated `frontend/public/downloads/mobile-version.json`, generated `frontend/src/generated/mobileRelease.ts`, and the F334 Android release-signing gate
- Steps: Run the mobile release metadata generator with `MOBILE_RELEASE_SIGNED=true` but leave `MOBILE_RELEASE_CERTIFICATE_SHA256` empty, malformed, or set to the known Android Debug certificate fingerprint.
- Expected: Release metadata generation should fail before writing a manifest that claims a production APK is release-signed unless a managed non-debug SHA-256 certificate fingerprint is supplied in a normalized, auditable form.
- Actual: The generator copied `MOBILE_RELEASE_SIGNED=true` and the raw certificate fingerprint environment value directly into the manifest. Runtime download/update code still blocked empty or debug fingerprints, but the release pipeline itself could emit misleading `releaseSigned: true` metadata and depend on the client-side gate to catch a bad publishing configuration.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 10:24 UTC found `generate-mobile-version.js` set `releaseSigned` from the environment and wrote a trimmed `MOBILE_RELEASE_CERTIFICATE_SHA256` string without requiring a fingerprint, checking SHA-256 shape, normalizing separators/case, or rejecting the known Android Debug fingerprint.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 10:24 UTC static source review confirmed the generator now normalizes certificate fingerprints to uppercase hex, fails fast when `MOBILE_RELEASE_SIGNED=true` lacks a 64-character SHA-256 fingerprint, and refuses the known Android Debug fingerprint before any manifest or generated TypeScript output is written. `node --check frontend/scripts/generate-mobile-version.js`, current mobile manifest JSON parsing, and issue-file `git diff --check` passed; no build, automated tests, UI smoke, screenshot, APK signing, artifact inspection, or commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: The mobile release metadata step now enforces the same non-debug certificate requirement that the storefront/App download and update gate expects, reducing the chance of a misconfigured debug/unsigned APK being labeled as release-signed.

### F419: Admin review product thumbnails bypassed the shared image safety resolver

- Area: Frontend / Admin Review Management, Mobile App shared image hardening
- Environment: `frontend/src/pages/ReviewManagement.tsx`, admin review moderation table product column
- Steps: Return an admin review row whose `productImageUrl` or nested `product.imageUrl` contains an unsafe or broken product image value, such as `https:\\evil.example\image.png`, `https://cdn.example.com/%5cadmin.png`, or a stale missing asset, then open the review management page.
- Expected: Admin review thumbnails should use the same shared product image resolver and fallback behavior as product, order, wishlist, support, and storefront image surfaces. Unsafe or broken image URLs should be rejected or replaced with the commercial product placeholder rather than passed directly to the browser.
- Actual: The review management product column read `record.productImageUrl || record.product?.imageUrl` and passed it directly into `<img src={productImageUrl}>`, bypassing `resolveProductImage(...)` and the F414 shared URL-shape hardening.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 10:22 UTC found the direct admin review thumbnail `src` assignment in `ReviewManagement.tsx` while neighboring product/order/admin image surfaces already used `resolveProductImage(...)` plus a fallback.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 10:22 UTC static source review confirmed `ReviewManagement.tsx` now resolves admin review product thumbnail URLs through `resolveProductImage(...)` before rendering and falls back to `productImageFallback` on image load failure. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: Admin review moderation thumbnails now share the same product image safety and fallback path as customer-facing App/storefront product image rendering.

### F418: Runtime API and support WebSocket URL config accepted obfuscated unsafe URL characters

- Area: Frontend / runtime configuration, API base URL, support WebSocket endpoint, Mobile App runtime config
- Environment: `frontend/src/utils/runtimeConfig.ts`, `window.__SHOP_RUNTIME_CONFIG__`, legacy `window.__SHOP_API_BASE_URL__`, `REACT_APP_API_BASE_URL`, and `REACT_APP_SUPPORT_WEBSOCKET_URL`
- Steps: Configure `apiBaseUrl` or `supportWebSocketUrl` with an ambiguous URL shape such as `https:\\api.example.com\root`, `https://api.example.com/%5cadmin`, `wss:\\support.example.com\ws`, or `wss://support.example.com/ws%00`, then load the storefront/App.
- Expected: Runtime API/WebSocket endpoint configuration should reject the same ambiguous raw-backslash and encoded NUL/backslash shapes as payment, announcement, media, mobile-update, and redirect URL surfaces. Invalid runtime config should fall back to the next safe configured/default endpoint.
- Actual: Runtime config rejected protocol-relative URLs, unsafe protocols, credentials, query strings, and hashes, but did not reject raw backslashes or encoded `%00`/`%5c` before URL parsing. Browser URL parsing can normalize backslash-shaped HTTP(S)/WS(S) values into ordinary-looking endpoints, creating parser ambiguity for the API and support socket base.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 10:21 UTC found `normalizeApiBaseUrl(...)` and `normalizeSupportWebSocketUrl(...)` only called `cleanString(...)`, then accepted relative paths or parsed absolute URLs without raw/encoded unsafe-character checks.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 10:21 UTC static source review confirmed `runtimeConfig.ts` now rejects runtime API and support WebSocket URL values containing raw backslashes, `%00`, or `%5c` before relative-path or absolute URL handling. `runtimeConfig.test.ts` was extended with API and WebSocket cases for raw-backslash, encoded-backslash, and encoded-NUL endpoint values, but Jest was not run per instruction.
- Resolution: Storefront/App runtime endpoint configuration now shares the stricter URL-shape gate used across other public commerce URL surfaces.

### F417: Navbar announcement ticker trusted stale backend placeholder content

- Area: Frontend / Storefront and Mobile App announcement banner quality
- Environment: `frontend/src/components/Navbar.tsx`, public announcement ticker fed by `GET /announcements/active`
- Steps: Return active announcement data containing QA/test placeholder or gibberish copy, such as title `TEST fast` and content `sadsadsafffffqewqe1231413214132141231`, from a stale backend instance or legacy database row; then open any storefront/App route.
- Expected: Customer-visible announcement banners should not render obvious test, placeholder, or gibberish content even if an old backend instance or stale database row returns it. The frontend should have a final display guard in addition to backend validation/cleanup.
- Actual: Backend source already filters and deactivates placeholder active announcements, but the Navbar trusted the API response directly. TEST_ISSUES F406 recorded a live regression where stale running backend data still returned the gibberish announcement and the ticker repeated it on every storefront route.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 10:08 UTC found `Navbar.tsx` set `announcements` directly from `announcementApi.getActive(4)` and repeated one or two returned announcements to fill the ticker. The backend service source has quality guards, but a stale deployed backend or old data response could still reach the visible first viewport.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 10:08 UTC static source review confirmed `Navbar.tsx` now filters fetched announcements with a frontend commercial-copy guard before storing them. The guard rejects blank text, obvious test/placeholder tokens, repeated-letter gibberish, and long alphanumeric tokens with suspicious digit/vowel patterns. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: The storefront/App announcement ticker now has a client-side last-resort quality guard for stale or legacy announcement API responses.

### F416: Product detail gallery did not apply the shared image fallback after URL hardening

- Area: Frontend / Mobile App product detail image reliability
- Environment: `frontend/src/pages/ProductDetail.tsx`, mobile product detail gallery, desktop main image, thumbnails, cart snapshot image, and image preview modal
- Steps: Load a product whose `imageUrl` or gallery `images` value is blank after trimming, unsupported, or rejected by the shared asset URL hardening such as a raw-backslash or encoded `%5c` image URL.
- Expected: Product detail should use the same shared image resolver as other storefront product surfaces and fall back to the product placeholder/backup image when a product image URL is invalid, so the mobile gallery, main image, thumbnails, cart snapshot, and preview modal do not receive an empty or unsafe `src`.
- Actual: ProductDetail assembled `productImages` and selected image strings directly from product payload fields, then passed them into `getOptimizedImageUrl(...)` and `buildResponsiveImageSrcSet(...)`. After F414 correctly rejects unsafe URL shapes, invalid detail images could become empty strings in the gallery path instead of consistently falling back.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 09:53 UTC found `normalizeProductImages(...)` and `resolveProductPrimaryImage(...)` returned raw product image strings, while other product surfaces use `resolveProductImage(...)` / `resolveApiAssetUrl(...)` before rendering.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 09:53 UTC static source review confirmed `ProductDetail.tsx` now resolves product detail image candidates through `resolveApiAssetUrl(..., fallbackProductImage)`, deduplicates the resolved safe URLs, and uses the same fallback for the selected primary image and cart snapshot image. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: Mobile and desktop product detail galleries now keep a stable fallback image when upstream product image URLs are invalid or rejected by the shared URL safety guard.

### F415: Login redirect helper accepted encoded unsafe relative paths

- Area: Frontend / Mobile App and storefront authentication redirects
- Environment: `frontend/src/utils/authRedirect.ts`, login/register-required flows from Home, ProductList, Wishlist, Notifications, Pet Gallery, ProductReview, AdminLayout, and native App WebView routes that call `buildLoginUrlFromWindow()` or consume `redirect=...` after login
- Steps: Open a protected storefront/App action with a crafted relative-looking redirect value containing encoded unsafe characters such as `/%5cevil.example/path`, `/products%00hidden`, or a raw backslash path, then complete login.
- Expected: Post-login redirects should only allow ordinary app-relative paths. Relative-looking paths with raw control characters, raw backslashes, encoded NUL, or encoded backslash should be rejected the same way absolute URLs and protocol-relative URLs are rejected.
- Actual: The redirect normalizer rejected non-relative values and `//host` protocol-relative values, but accepted raw backslashes and encoded unsafe URL characters inside an otherwise slash-prefixed path. Those shapes can create browser/proxy/parser ambiguity in native WebView and mobile browser auth flows.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static App/frontend review at 2026-06-01 09:41 UTC found `normalizeRelativePath(...)` only checked `startsWith('/')` and `startsWith('//')`, with no raw/encoded unsafe-character rejection.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 09:41 UTC static source review confirmed `authRedirect.ts` now rejects redirect targets containing control characters, raw backslashes, `%00`, or `%5c`, in addition to the existing absolute/protocol-relative/login-loop rejection. `authRedirect.test.ts` now documents encoded-backslash, raw-backslash, and encoded-NUL cases for both login URL building and post-login target consumption. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: Mobile App and storefront login redirects now fail closed on unsafe relative-looking redirect paths.

### F414: Shared storefront image resolver accepted obfuscated unsafe URL characters

- Area: Frontend / Mobile App and storefront image URL safety
- Environment: `frontend/src/utils/mediaAssets.ts`, shared product/category/brand/cart/order/gallery image rendering across mobile App and web storefront
- Steps: Return or enter a product, category, brand, cart snapshot, or gallery image URL with an obfuscated shape such as `https:\\evil.example\image.png`, `https://cdn.example.com/%5cadmin.png`, or `https://cdn.example.com/image%00.png`, then render a page that resolves it through `resolveApiAssetUrl(...)`, `buildResponsiveImageSrcSet(...)`, or `getOptimizedImageUrl(...)`.
- Expected: Shared public image helpers should reject the same ambiguous URL shapes as payment, announcement, update-manifest, and rich-media links: raw control characters, raw backslashes, protocol-relative URLs, encoded NUL/backslash payloads, URL credentials, and non-HTTP(S) schemes. Invalid image URLs should fall back instead of being normalized by the browser URL parser.
- Actual: `resolveApiAssetUrl(...)` rejected non-image data URLs, protocol-relative URLs, credentials, and non-HTTP(S) schemes, but did not reject raw backslashes or encoded `%00`/`%5c` before constructing `new URL(...)`. Browser URL parsing can normalize backslash-shaped HTTP URLs into ordinary-looking remote URLs, and the responsive/optimized image helpers also parsed resize-aware URLs without the stricter encoded-character guard.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 09:42 UTC found `mediaAssets.ts` accepted `http(s)` asset URLs after only credential checks, and `new URL('https:\\\\evil.example\\x')` normalizes to `https://evil.example/x` in the runtime parser.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 09:42 UTC static source review confirmed `mediaAssets.ts` now rejects raw control characters, raw backslashes, protocol-relative URLs, and encoded `%00`/`%5c` before resolving shared image assets, responsive srcsets, or optimized image URLs. `mediaAssets.test.ts` was extended with focused regression expectations, but Jest was not run per instruction.
- Resolution: Storefront and App image URL rendering now uses the same strict unsafe-character gate as other public commerce URL surfaces.

### F413: Product rich media accepted protocol-relative URLs in editor and payload normalization

- Area: Admin product rich content / storefront product detail media safety
- Environment: `frontend/src/components/ProductRichDetail.tsx`, `frontend/src/components/ProductRichDetailEditor.tsx`, and `frontend/src/api/index.ts` product create/update payload normalization
- Steps: Add a product rich image or video block with a media URL such as `//evil.example/media.mp4`, or an encoded unsafe URL shape, then preview/save the product and open the storefront product detail page.
- Expected: Rich media blocks should use the same safe URL shape as other public commerce links: site-relative upload paths or explicit HTTP(S) URLs without credentials, protocol-relative forms, raw backslashes/control characters, or encoded NUL/backslash payloads. Invalid media URLs should be visible to admins before save and should not be submitted as usable rich blocks.
- Actual: The storefront renderer rejected raw backslashes, control characters, credentials, `%00`, and `%5c`, but `isHttpMediaUrl(...)` resolved protocol-relative URLs against `window.location.origin`, allowing `//host/path` as embeddable media. The product create/update payload normalizer only trimmed media URL strings, so invalid media blocks could be sent to the backend even though storefront rendering later dropped or treated them differently; the editor showed invalid warnings for images but not videos.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 09:30 UTC found `isHttpMediaUrl(...)` used `new URL(trimmed, window.location.origin)` without an explicit `trimmed.startsWith('//')` rejection, and `normalizeProductDetailContent(...)` copied rich media URLs after text normalization only.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 09:30 UTC static source review confirmed `ProductRichDetail.tsx` now rejects protocol-relative rich media URLs, `ProductRichDetailEditor.tsx` shows the invalid URL warning for both image and video blocks and only previews valid media, and `normalizeProductDetailContent(...)` drops image/video blocks unless their URL is a safe site-relative path or explicit HTTP(S) URL without credentials, encoded `%00`/`%5c`, raw backslashes, or protocol-relative form. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: Product rich media URL safety is now consistent between admin editing, API payload normalization, and storefront rendering.

### F412: Announcement link validation accepted encoded unsafe URL characters

- Area: Storefront announcements / public banner link safety
- Environment: `frontend/src/utils/announcementLinks.ts`, `src/main/java/com/example/shop/service/SiteAnnouncementService.java`, active public announcement ticker links, and admin announcement link publishing
- Steps: Save or receive an announcement `linkUrl` containing encoded unsafe URL characters such as `%00` or `%5c`, then render the public storefront announcement ticker.
- Expected: Public announcement links should accept only safe relative paths or HTTP(S) URLs with no credentials, control characters, backslashes, or encoded NUL/backslash payloads. The backend publishing guard and frontend render guard should use the same allowlist shape.
- Actual: The guards rejected raw control characters, raw backslashes, protocol-relative URLs, URL credentials, and non-HTTP(S) schemes, but did not reject encoded `%00` or `%5c`. Those URL shapes can create parser/proxy/browser ambiguity if an announcement link is published or returned from older data.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static source review at 2026-06-01 09:26 UTC found `normalizeAnnouncementLink(...)` and `SiteAnnouncementService.isSafeLinkUrl(...)` both checked raw `\\` and control characters but not encoded NUL/backslash sequences.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 09:26 UTC static source review confirmed `announcementLinks.ts` now rejects links whose lower-cased value contains `%00` or `%5c`, and `SiteAnnouncementService.isSafeLinkUrl(...)` applies the same encoded-character rejection before accepting admin-saved or public announcement links. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: Public announcement links now share stricter encoded-character URL validation on both backend and frontend.

### F411: Native App scroll helpers miss the actual content scroll host

- Area: Mobile App / native WebView scroll behavior
- Environment: `frontend/src/utils/nativeScroll.ts`, native App shell using `.shop-app-shell` and `.ant-layout-content`, route scroll reset, Home lazy loading, ProductList pagination reset, and ProductDetail scroll warmup
- Steps: Run the storefront inside the native App/WebView shell where CSS makes `.ant-layout-content` the element that actually scrolls, then navigate between routes, scroll Home near the bottom, or open ProductDetail after scrolling.
- Expected: App scroll utilities should detect and listen to the active native content scroll container so route changes reset to top, bottom detection fires reliably, and lazy/warmup work is triggered from real scroll events.
- Actual: The utility only treated `.shop-app-shell` as the native scroll host. When `.ant-layout-content` is the real scrollable container, `getAppScrollMetrics()` can read stale window/shell metrics, `addAppScrollListener()` can miss content-layer scroll events, and `scrollAppToTop()` can leave the visible App content scrolled.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Parallel App worker static review at 2026-06-01 09:17 UTC found native App CSS can make `.ant-layout-content` the effective scroll container while `nativeScroll.ts` prioritized only `.shop-app-shell`. Current callers include `App.tsx`, `Home.tsx`, `ProductList.tsx`, and `ProductDetail.tsx`.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 09:17 UTC static source review confirmed `nativeScroll.ts` now builds native scroll candidates from `.ant-layout-content` plus `.shop-app-shell`, selects the first actually scrollable candidate for metrics, resets all candidates plus window/body on `scrollAppToTop(...)`, and registers scroll listeners on both window and native candidates. `git diff --check -- frontend/src/utils/nativeScroll.ts` passed in the parallel App worker. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: Native App scrolling utilities now follow the real content scroll host instead of assuming the shell element always scrolls.

### F410: Mobile cart card list lacked accessible list and item selection semantics

- Area: Frontend / Mobile App and mobile web cart accessibility
- Environment: `frontend/src/pages/Cart.tsx`, mobile cart card layout rendered instead of the desktop table
- Steps: Open the cart on a mobile/App viewport with multiple cart items and inspect the mobile card list with assistive technology or DOM accessibility semantics.
- Expected: The mobile cart cards should be exposed as a cart item list, and each product selection checkbox should identify the product it selects.
- Actual: The mobile layout visually replaced the table with cards, but the card container and items did not expose list/listitem semantics, and the selection checkbox lacked a product-specific accessible label.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Parallel App worker static review at 2026-06-01 09:00 UTC found the mobile card layout in `Cart.tsx` did not provide list semantics or item-specific checkbox labeling.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 09:00 UTC static source review confirmed `Cart.tsx` now gives `.cart-page__mobileList` `role="list"` with the cart title as its label, gives each mobile cart card `role="listitem"`, and labels each product checkbox with the cart selection action plus `item.productName`. `git diff --check -- frontend/src/pages/Cart.tsx` passed in the parallel App worker. No Maven/Jest/build/UI smoke/screenshot/commit was run per instruction.
- Resolution: Mobile cart cards now expose a navigable item list and product-specific selection controls for assistive technology.

### F409: Mobile update URL resolver accepts unsafe manifest-provided URLs

- Area: Mobile App / update manifest and APK download URL handling
- Environment: `frontend/src/utils/mobileUpdate.ts`, native App update prompt, Navbar Android download entry, runtime-configured mobile manifest URL, and `frontend/public/downloads/mobile-version.json`
- Steps: Provide a mobile release manifest or runtime config with an unsafe `apkUrl`, `legacyApkUrl`, or manifest URL such as protocol-relative `//host/path`, URL credentials, encoded NUL/backslash payloads, a non-HTTP(S) scheme, or remote plaintext `http://` origin; then open the native App update flow or storefront Android download entry.
- Expected: Commercial App update/download code should only open a trusted HTTPS URL, a local-development HTTP URL, or a safe relative asset resolved from a trusted origin. Malformed or attacker-controlled URL shapes should be rejected before reaching `window.open`, the Capacitor Browser plugin, or a visible download link.
- Actual: The resolver previously normalized strings by trimming control characters and then passed more URL shapes through origin resolution. That left the update/download path too permissive for manifest-provided values that should never be accepted in a production mobile update surface.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Parallel App worker static review at 2026-06-01 06:20 UTC inspected the mobile update chain in `mobileUpdate.ts`, `App.tsx`, `Navbar.tsx`, runtime config, and the current public mobile manifest. The review identified the unsafe URL-shape gap as distinct from F334's debug-signing release issue.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 06:22 UTC static source review confirmed `normalizeMobileUrlValue(...)`, `parseSafeMobileHttpUrl(...)`, and `resolveMobileAssetUrl(...)` now reject control characters, backslashes, `%00`, `%5c`, protocol-relative URLs, URL credentials, non-HTTP(S) schemes, and non-localhost plaintext HTTP remote origins while preserving HTTPS, localhost HTTP, and safe relative-path resolution. No build, automated test, UI smoke, screenshot, or commit was run per instruction.
- Expected fix direction: Keep the URL allowlist strict and add regression tests around release-manifest URL normalization when tests are allowed. F334 separately requires a release-signed APK and publishing workflow.

### F405: Native App top navigation icon actions are below commercial touch-target size

- Area: Mobile App UI / Android WebView-like top navigation
- Environment: `/home/guhao/shoptest` authoritative source; CRA storefront at `http://127.0.0.1:3000`; Playwright Chromium Android WebView-like profile (`390x844`, DPR 3, Android WebView UA) with injected native Capacitor runtime. API responses were route-fulfilled only to keep the App UI deterministic. No screenshots, recordings, traces, or image captures were produced.
- Steps: Open App/WebView-like storefront routes including `/`, `/products`, `/products/1`, `/cart`, `/checkout`, `/coupons`, and `/track-order`; inspect visible top-nav action bounding boxes.
- Expected: Core App header actions such as language/currency, More, and Cart should expose stable one-handed tap targets of at least 44px, or equivalent padded hit areas, without shrinking the actual clickable element to 36px.
- Actual: The visible top-nav controls repeatedly measure `36x36` in native App mode. The no-screenshot DOM probe measured `.shop-nav__mobile-locale` at `36x36`, `.shop-nav__secondary-action.shop-nav__more-trigger` at `36x36`, and the cart button (`aria-label="Cart"`) at `36x36` across multiple storefront routes. These are core App actions in the fixed header and are smaller than the 44px commercial mobile target used for the newly fixed product-card actions.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 13:55 UTC development pass added a native-App header hit-area guard in `frontend/src/mobile-app.css`. Native App top-nav language/currency, More, cart, and other icon-only header actions now reserve 44px width/height/min-size with a compact 1-2px action gap so the fixed header still fits at 390px and narrow App widths.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 13:52 UTC no-screenshot Android WebView-like DOM/layout regression confirmed `bodyClass="shop-mobile-app shop-mobile-app--android"`, `clientWidth=390`, `scrollWidth=390`, and no CRA overlay, while the top-nav action rectangles remained `36x36` at `left=260`, `left=301`, and `left=342`. Static source review found mobile/App CSS explicitly forcing these controls to 36px: `frontend/src/components/Navbar.css` "Final mobile nav icon tap-target pass" sets `.shop-nav__mobile-locale`, `.shop-nav__secondary-action`, and `.shop-nav__actions button[aria-label]` to `width/min-width/height/min-height: 36px`; `frontend/src/mobile-app.css` repeats native-App overrides at the header action selectors with `36px !important`.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 13:55 UTC static source review confirmed the final native-App override in `mobile-app.css` sets `.shop-nav__mobile-locale`, `.shop-nav__secondary-action`, `.shop-nav__actions button[aria-label]`, `.shop-nav__actions a[aria-label]`, and `.shop-nav__actions .shop-nav__admin` to 44px width/min-width/max-width/height/min-height in `body.shop-mobile-app`, with the App action cluster capped to 148px and 142px on narrow screens. No automated tests, builds, screenshots, or commits were run per instruction.
- Retest: 2026-06-01 08:07 UTC parallel App worker static source review found `Navbar.css` loads after `mobile-app.css`, so a later mobile navbar rule could still shrink native-App header actions. Added a final `body.shop-mobile-app .shop-nav .shop-nav__actions > ...` guard at the end of `frontend/src/components/Navbar.css` that pins language/currency, More, cart, action links, and admin icon controls to actual 44px width/height/min/max dimensions and preserves icon/badge centering. `git diff --check -- frontend/src/components/Navbar.css` passed. No build, Maven, Jest, UI smoke, screenshot, or commit was run per instruction.
- Expected fix direction: Raise the clickable box for native App top-nav icon actions to at least 44px while preserving header fit, or add an equivalent transparent hit-area wrapper. Re-run no-screenshot App DOM checks on `/`, `/products`, `/cart`, `/checkout`, `/coupons`, and `/track-order`, including narrow `360px` width.

### F404: Product list defaults to a zero-price filter and hides purchasable catalog items

- Area: Frontend / ProductList default catalog browsing and add-to-cart chain
- Environment: `/home/guhao/shoptest` authoritative source; CRA storefront at `http://127.0.0.1:3000`; local backend at `http://127.0.0.1:8081`; no-screenshot Playwright DOM/network smoke with local `/api` route fulfillment to exercise live backend data. No screenshots, recordings, traces, or image captures were produced.
- Steps: Open `/products` without any query string in desktop web, mobile web, or Android WebView-like App profile, with live backend product data available.
- Expected: The default product list should request the unfiltered first product page, show normal purchasable products, and expose an enabled primary add-to-cart/quick-add path.
- Actual: The page treats missing `minPrice` and `maxPrice` URL params as `0`, marks the price filter as active, and requests `/api/products?minPrice=0&maxPrice=0&sort=default&page=0&size=12`. In the live-data smoke this rendered only one product card with `Notify me` instead of a primary add/quick-add button on all three profiles, so the product-list add-to-cart chain could not proceed and `/cart` / `/checkout` stayed empty.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 13:55 UTC development pass corrected `ProductList.parsePriceParam(...)` so missing or blank `minPrice`/`maxPrice` query parameters return `undefined` before numeric conversion. Default `/products` loads therefore keep `priceFilterTouched=false` and stop sending `minPrice=0&maxPrice=0` unless the user explicitly supplies price parameters.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 13:42 UTC no-screenshot Playwright live-data smoke saw proxied requests from `/api/products?minPrice=0&maxPrice=0&sort=default&page=0&size=12` to backend `/products?minPrice=0&maxPrice=0&sort=default&page=0&size=12`. Desktop, mobile web, and Android WebView-like App all rendered `productCardCount=1`, the first card actions were `Quick preview`, `Notify me`, `Favorite`, and `Compare`, and no `.product-list__actionButton.ant-btn-primary` was available to click. Static source review found `parsePriceParam(value: string | null)` in `frontend/src/pages/ProductList.tsx` uses `Number(value)` and returns `0` for `null`; the initial search-param effect therefore sets `requestedMinPrice=0`, `requestedMaxPrice=0`, `requestedPriceFilterTouched=true`, and sends the zero-price filter to `productApi.getPage(...)` even when the URL has no price params.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 13:55 UTC static source review confirmed `parsePriceParam(null)` and `parsePriceParam('')` now return `undefined`; the search-param effect derives `requestedPriceFilterTouched=false` when both price params are absent, and `fetchProducts(...)` receives `minPrice: undefined` and `maxPrice: undefined` for default `/products`. No automated tests, builds, screenshots, or commits were run per instruction.
- Expected fix direction: Make `parsePriceParam(null)` return `undefined` before numeric conversion, keep `priceFilterTouched=false` when price params are absent, and retest `/products` default load plus product-list quick-add/cart/checkout on desktop, mobile web, and Android WebView-like App. Add regression coverage for missing, blank, zero, and positive price query params.

### F403: Native App home hero clips primary copy and CTA off the left edge

- Area: Mobile App UI / Android WebView-like home page
- Environment: `/home/guhao/shoptest` source is authoritative; `/root/shoptest` is not the active repo and only contains stale/auxiliary docs plus a frontend symlink. CRA dev server at `http://127.0.0.1:3000`; Playwright Chromium DOM/layout smoke with Android WebView user agent and injected Capacitor native runtime (`isNativePlatform() === true`). No screenshots, recordings, traces, or image captures were produced.
- Steps: Open `/` in an Android WebView-like viewport (`390x844`) with native App classes enabled (`body.shop-mobile-app shop-mobile-app--android`), wait for network idle plus 3.5 seconds so transitions settle, then inspect DOM layout rectangles for the home hero.
- Expected: The first viewport hero title, supporting copy, and primary CTA should fit inside the App viewport and be fully tappable/readable.
- Actual: The hero section itself has `left=-12`, `right=402`, `width=414` with `overflow-x:hidden`. The key content is shifted further left: `.shopee-hero h1` reports `left=-174`, `right=297`, `width=471`; `.shopee-hero p` reports `left=-174`, `right=297`, `width=471`; `.shopee-hero__actions` reports `left=-174`, `right=361`, `width=535`; and the primary hero button reports `left=-174`, `right=200`, `width=374`. Because the hero clips overflow, the App first screen can hide a large part of the headline and CTA hit area.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 13:20 UTC development pass added a native-App home hero viewport guard in `frontend/src/mobile-app.css`. The App-specific override removes the negative/full-bleed hero section sizing in native mode, forces the hero grid/main/content block to stay within the inner viewport, resets the late mobile `align-items:flex-end` interaction that pushed the content block left, and makes hero CTA groups single-column 44px-minimum buttons in App mode.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 13:04 UTC no-screenshot Playwright DOM/layout smoke. `document.body.className` was `shop-mobile-app shop-mobile-app--android`, `document.documentElement.clientWidth` was `390`, `scrollWidth` remained `390`, and the visible body text started with `ShopMX Home Products Coupons Cart My account...`, but the measured hero title and CTA rectangles were partially off-screen to the left after the page had stabilized. Related CSS candidates include repeated native App home overrides in `frontend/src/mobile-app.css` around the `.shopee-hero`, `.shopee-container`, `.shopee-hero__main`, and `.shopee-hero__actions` blocks.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 13:20 UTC no-screenshot Playwright DOM/layout retest against `http://127.0.0.1:3000/` with Android WebView-like user agent and injected native Capacitor runtime confirmed `bodyClass="shop-mobile-app shop-mobile-app--android"`, `clientWidth=390`, `scrollWidth=390`, and no CRA overlay iframe. After a 3.5s settle, `.shopee-hero` measured `left=12`, `right=378`, `width=366`; `.shopee-hero h1`, `.shopee-hero p`, `.shopee-hero__actions`, and the primary hero button all measured `left=29`, `right=361`, `width=332`, so the title, copy, and CTA are fully inside the App viewport. Additional no-screenshot desktop web (`1366x900`) and mobile web (`390x844`, no App class) DOM probes confirmed `scrollWidth` equals the viewport width and hero title/actions/button rectangles remain in viewport. No screenshots, recordings, traces, builds, or automated test suites were run in this development pass.
- Expected fix direction: Constrain App hero content and CTA grid to the inner viewport width, remove negative/over-wide hero child positioning in native App mode, and re-run no-screenshot DOM layout checks for `/` in desktop, mobile web, and Android WebView-like profiles.

### F402: App product-card quick actions are below commercial mobile touch-target size

- Area: Mobile App / Android WebView-like storefront UI; Home product cards and quick actions
- Environment: Current `/home/guhao/shoptest` source, Android WebView-like Playwright probe at 390x844 with `window.Capacitor.isNativePlatform() === true`; no screenshots or recordings generated.
- Steps: Open the App/WebView-like home route, inspect visible product-card quick actions such as View all, Add to cart, and Favorite.
- Expected: Frequent commerce actions should expose stable touch targets suitable for one-handed mobile use, ideally at least 44px high/wide or with an equivalent padded hit area, without relying on sub-32px controls.
- Actual: App-specific CSS forces product-card quick action buttons to `31px` by `31px`. The no-screenshot WebView-like DOM probe measured repeated visible View all/Add to cart/Favorite quick actions at `31x31`, and source confirms `body.shop-mobile-app .shopee-product__quickActions button { width: 31px; height: 31px; }` in `frontend/src/mobile-app.css` plus a mobile override in `frontend/src/pages/Home.css`. These controls sit on dense product cards and are below commercial mobile tap-target expectations, increasing missed taps on core add-to-cart/favorite actions.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 13:12 UTC development pass raised native App product-card hit targets to commercial mobile size. `mobile-app.css` now gives Home quick actions, ProductList preview triggers, ProductList primary action buttons, and compact wishlist/compare buttons 44px width/height/min-size in App mode, including the narrow native-App media branch. The Home mobile CSS overrides that previously forced 31/32/34/38px quick buttons now resolve to 44px as well.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 12:46 UTC no-screenshot Android WebView-like probe of `http://127.0.0.1:3000/` with Capacitor App classes found `bodyClass="shop-mobile-app shop-mobile-app--android"`, no horizontal overflow, no dev overlay, and repeated quick action button bounding boxes of `31x31` for View all, Add to cart, and Favorite. Static source check found the App override in `frontend/src/mobile-app.css` and mobile Home override setting the same small dimensions.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 13:12 UTC static retest confirmed the App-specific quick-action selectors now set `44px` width/height/min-size for Home and ProductList product-card actions, and the ProductList action grid reserves `44px` compact columns. No build, automated test, or screenshot run was performed in this development pass.
- Expected fix direction: Keep the compact visual treatment if desired, but give each quick action a minimum 40-44px tappable box through width/height, padding, or a larger hit-area wrapper. Recheck Home, ProductList cards, and App WebView-like product grids in English/Chinese/Spanish after the sizing change.

### F401: Frontend build and web UI are blocked by nullable support WebSocket token

- Area: Frontend / SupportManagement.tsx; Desktop web, mobile web, Android WebView shell
- Environment: `frontend/src/pages/SupportManagement.tsx:329`, `npx tsc --noEmit --pretty false`, `npm run build`, CRA dev server at `http://127.0.0.1:3000`
- Steps: Run `npx tsc --noEmit --pretty false` or `npm run build` in `/home/guhao/shoptest/frontend`; or open the storefront in desktop Chrome, mobile Chrome viewport, or an Android WebView-like viewport while the dev server is running.
- Expected: TypeScript and production build gates pass, and desktop/mobile/App WebView screens render without a compile overlay intercepting the UI.
- Actual: `SupportManagement` reads `token` from `localStorage.getItem('token')`, so the value is `string | null`, then passes it directly to `supportWebSocketUrl(token)` and `supportWebSocketProtocols(token)`, both of which require `string`. `npx tsc --noEmit --pretty false` fails with two `TS2345` errors at `SupportManagement.tsx:329`; `npm run build` fails with the same error before producing a deployable frontend. The CRA dev server injects a `webpack-dev-server-client-overlay` iframe on ordinary storefront routes, so desktop web, mobile web, and Android WebView-like sessions see "Compiled with problems" and pointer hits at the top-left resolve to the overlay iframe instead of the page.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 12:18 UTC development pass changed `SupportManagement`'s admin support token helper to return an empty string instead of `null` when no token is stored. The existing `if (!token) return` guard now narrows the WebSocket token to a stable `string` before passing it to `supportWebSocketUrl(...)` and `supportWebSocketProtocols(...)`, matching the helper signatures and the already-fixed customer support socket pattern.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 12:08 UTC `npx tsc --noEmit --pretty false` failed with `TS2345: Argument of type 'string | null' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'` at `SupportManagement.tsx:329:52` and `329:86`. `npm run build` failed with the same `SupportManagement.tsx:329` error. Playwright checks against `http://127.0.0.1:3000/` and `/products` confirmed the `webpack-dev-server-client-overlay` frame on desktop, mobile, and Android WebView-like viewports; screenshots were saved under `artifacts/test-agent-20260531/screenshots/desktop-home-overlay-check.png`, `mobile-home-overlay-check.png`, and `android-webview-home-overlay-check.png`.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 12:18 UTC static retest confirmed `readAdminSupportToken()` now returns `getLocalStorageItem('token') || ''`, so `const token = readAdminSupportToken()` is typed as `string` before the support WebSocket is constructed. `git diff --check -- frontend/src/pages/SupportManagement.tsx` passed. No frontend typecheck, production build, or Playwright smoke was rerun in this development pass.
- Expected fix direction: After the null guard, copy `token` into a narrowed non-null local such as `socketToken`, or otherwise return before WebSocket construction when it is absent, then pass the narrowed string to both WebSocket helper functions. Re-run `npx tsc --noEmit --pretty false`, `npm run build`, and a smoke check of desktop/mobile/App WebView routes to confirm the overlay is gone.

### F400: Frontend production build fails with null token in WebSocket URL

- Area: Frontend / CustomerSupportWidget.tsx
- Environment: `frontend/src/components/CustomerSupportWidget.tsx:390`, `npm run build`
- Steps: Run `npm run build` in `/home/guhao/shoptest/frontend`
- Expected: Frontend production build compiles successfully
- Actual: TypeScript compilation fails with `TS2345: Argument of type 'string | null' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'` at line 390. The `token` variable from `localStorage.getItem('token')` can be `null`, but `supportWebSocketUrl(token)` requires `string`.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 10:41 UTC development pass stabilized the narrowed token value before creating the signed-in support WebSocket. The effect now assigns `const socketToken = token` after the `!token` guard and passes that string to `supportWebSocketUrl(...)` and `supportWebSocketProtocols(...)`, resolving the TypeScript `string | null` build error.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 16:48 UTC `npm run build` failed with `TS2345` at `frontend/src/components/CustomerSupportWidget.tsx:390`
- Retest: 2026-05-31 10:41 UTC static confirmation found `CustomerSupportWidget` still passes `token` from `localStorage.getItem('token')` into `supportWebSocketUrl(token)` and `supportWebSocketProtocols(token)`, while both helpers require `string`; the build failure path is valid.

### F399: Coupon center displays payable-percent coupons as percent-off discounts

- Area: Frontend / CouponCenter discount copy; locale coupon wording
- Environment: Public coupon center, checkout coupon picker/summary copy, and DISCOUNT coupons where `discountPercent` is stored as the payable percent
- Steps: Create or load a DISCOUNT coupon with `discountPercent=90`, then view it in the coupon center or checkout coupon description.
- Expected: The UI should either display payable-percent semantics explicitly (for example, "Pay 90%") or convert to the actual savings percent (for example, "10% off"). Displayed text should match backend order discount and coupon estimated value.
- Actual: Backend coupon calculation treats `discountPercent` as the payable percent: `CouponService.calculateDiscount()` computes `subtotal * (100 - percent) / 100` at lines 311-327. `CouponCenter.describeCoupon()` passes the raw `discountPercent` value to `pages.coupons.discountPayable` at lines 202-209, while the locale strings render that raw value as an off/discount percentage (`"{percent}% off"`, `"{percent}% de descuento"`, `优惠 {percent}%`) at `frontend/src/locales/en.json` line 1016, `es.json` line 1421, and `zh.json` line 1205. A 90-payable-percent coupon can therefore be advertised as 90% off even though backend checkout and `getCouponEstimatedValue()` apply only a 10% savings.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 12:28 UTC static verification confirmed the coupon description paths now convert payable percent to actual savings percent before passing it to the existing off/discount locale strings. `CouponCenter.describeCoupon()`, `Checkout.describeCoupon()`, and the admin coupon rule display all calculate `100 - discountPercent` after clamping the stored payable percent.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 09:49 UTC static review found the coupon-center display path and locale copy still interpret the raw payable-percent field as an off/discount percent. Existing F114 fixed checkout estimation semantics and F220 now confirms `getCouponEstimatedValue()` matches backend payable-percent math; F399 covers the remaining user-facing coupon description mismatch.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 10:25 UTC tracker/source correction confirmed F399 remains OPEN. `CouponCenter.describeCoupon()` still passes raw `discountPercent` to `pages.coupons.discountPayable`, and English/Spanish/Chinese locale text still renders it as off/discount copy. The prior 10:20 FIXED note was misplaced F353 marketing-sort evidence, not a coupon-copy fix.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 12:28 UTC static retest confirmed the current source no longer passes raw payable percent to user-facing off/discount copy. Coupon center, checkout, and admin coupon management now display the actual savings percent while backend coupon calculation still treats `discountPercent` as payable percent.

- Environment: `src/main/java/com/example/shop/repository/PetGalleryPhotoRepository.java:22`, `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` / `./mvnw test`

- Expected: Backend compiles successfully with Java 11

- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 07:56 UTC development pass replaced the Java text block in `PetGalleryPhotoRepository` with Java 11-compatible string concatenation for the admin gallery filter clause.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 14:48 UTC `./mvnw test` failed with compilation error in `PetGalleryPhotoRepository.java:22`
