Plain `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` completed successfully with the project still configured for Java 11 source/target.

### F397: Direct search endpoint ignores storefront refinement filters

- Area: Backend / public search API contract; Frontend / public product API compatibility
- Environment: Anonymous or legacy direct `GET /search` calls with storefront refinement query parameters such as `petSize`, `material`, or `color`
- Steps: Call `GET /search?keyword=harness&petSize=small&material=nylon&color=red&page=0&size=20`, or migrate a product-list/refinement URL to the public search endpoint.
- Expected: The public search endpoint should honor the same supported product-list refinements as `/products`, or reject unsupported refinement parameters explicitly. Search results should not silently ignore user-selected pet size, material, or color constraints.
- Actual: `SearchController.searchProducts()` binds keyword, category, discount, featured, price, page, size, and sort, but it has no `petSize`, `material`, or `color` request parameters and never sets `ProductListQuery.petSizes`, `materials`, or `colors`. Those fields exist and are used by `ProductServiceImpl.matchesCatalogRefinements(...)`, and `/products` does bind and pass the same refinement parameters. Direct `/search` callers therefore receive unrefined results even though the public product query model supports the filters and the active storefront sends them to `/products`.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 07:38 UTC development pass added `petSize`, `material`, and `color` request parameters to `SearchController.searchProducts(...)` and passes them into `ProductListQuery`, matching the `/products` public list binder.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 07:38 UTC static retest confirmed `SearchController.searchProducts(...)` now binds `List<String> petSize`, `material`, and `color`, calls `query.setPetSizes(...)`, `query.setMaterials(...)`, and `query.setColors(...)`, and therefore reaches the existing `ProductServiceImpl.matchesCatalogRefinements(...)` filter path. `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` completed successfully.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 07:34 UTC static review found `SearchController.searchProducts()` lines 21-47 only copies `q`/`keyword`, `categoryId`, `discount`, `featured`, `minPrice`/`maxPrice`, `page`, `size`, and `sort` into `ProductListQuery`; there are no `petSize`, `material`, or `color` request parameters. `ProductController.getAllProducts()` lines 40-68 binds `List<String> petSize`, `material`, and `color`, then calls `query.setPetSizes(...)`, `setMaterials(...)`, and `setColors(...)`. `ProductListQuery` has the refinement fields at lines 16-18, and `ProductServiceImpl.matchesCatalogRefinements(...)` lines 463-472 applies them. Frontend `productApi.getAll()` and `getPage()` append those same parameters to `/products` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1187-1203 and 1222-1238. Existing F296 covered ProductList not sending refinement filters to the backend and is fixed; F235/F295 cover `/search` response-size/page-size behavior; F396 covers backend full-scan pagination work. F397 covers the remaining direct `/search` contract gap where refinement parameters are ignored entirely.
- Expected fix direction: Share a public product-query binder between `ProductController` and `SearchController`, or add `petSize`, `material`, and `color` parameters to `SearchController` and pass them into `ProductListQuery`. Add controller/service regression coverage proving `/search` and `/products` return consistently filtered results for pet size, material, and color.

### F396: Public product list and search pages still full-scan catalogs before pagination

- Area: Backend / public product list and search APIs; Frontend / Home and ProductList catalog browsing
- Environment: Anonymous `GET /products?page=...&size=...`, direct `GET /search?page=...&size=...`, Home bounded discovery load, ProductList pagination/search/category/refinement browsing, and stores with large public catalogs or many approved reviews
- Steps: Open the home page, browse `/products`, change ProductList pages or filters, or call `/search` on a large catalog after the F343/F235 response-size fixes.
- Expected: Public list/search endpoints should push filtering, sorting, pagination, and counts into bounded repository/database queries. A request for a 12-, 20-, or 24-item page should not load and enrich every active product and category first.
- Actual: The response is bounded, but the backend page builder still performs full in-memory catalog work on cache misses. `ProductController.getAllProducts()` returns the paginated response when `page` or `size` is supplied, and `SearchController.searchProducts()` delegates to the same public product query path, but `ProductServiceImpl.findPublicProductsFilteredSorted()` loads all categories with `categoryRepository.findAll()`, loads all products with `productRepository.findAll()`, filters every product in Java, enriches review stats for every matched product, sorts the full matched list, and only then slices the requested page. `enrichReviewStats()` builds review-summary input ids from the whole matched list before pagination, so common storefront page views can still do full-product and broad review-summary work even though the JSON response contains a small page.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 07:32 UTC development pass changed public paginated product/search reads to use a JPA `Specification` plus `PageRequest` before review-stat enrichment. The page path now applies active/public status, category descendants, keyword/category keyword matches, featured/discount, price, refinement, and sort constraints at repository/page-query level, then enriches review stats only for the returned page rows. `/search?page=...` also reaches this bounded path through `findPublicProducts(...)`.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 07:13 UTC static review found `ProductController.getAllProducts()` accepts `page`/`size` and returns `ProductPublicPageResponse` at lines 40-80, while `SearchController.searchProducts()` accepts `page`/`size` and returns a list from `productService.findPublicProducts(query)` at lines 21-47. Both paths reach `ProductServiceImpl.findPublicProductPage()` or `findPublicProductsUncached()`, which call `findPublicProductsFilteredSorted(...)` before `applyProductPagination(...)` at lines 197-210. Inside `findPublicProductsFilteredSorted(...)`, lines 220-228 load every category, call `productRepository.findAll()`, filter in Java, and call `enrichReviewStats(...)`; lines 230-231 sort the whole list before pagination. `enrichReviewStats(...)` lines 2712-2728 collects all matched product ids and queries review summaries for that full set before the page slice. Active frontend callers request small pages through `Home.tsx` lines 464-467, `ProductList.tsx` lines 798-843, and `productApi.getAll()`/`getPage()` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1188-1249. Existing F343 covered unbounded `/products` response downloads, F235/F295 covered `/search` response size and size clamping, F349/F352/F353 cover product-list pagination and sort correctness, F387 covers featured products, and F395 covers recommendation candidate endpoints; F396 covers the remaining full-catalog backend work behind the now-bounded public list/search responses.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 07:32 UTC static retest confirmed `findPublicProductPage(...)` delegates to `findPublicProductPageUncached(...)`, which calls `productRepository.findAll(publicProductSpecification(...), pageRequest)` instead of `findPublicProductsFilteredSorted(...)`. `findPublicProductsUncached(...)` now delegates to the same page path whenever `page` or `size` is present, covering `/search?page=...` as well. Category keyword lookup uses bounded `CategoryRepository.findIdsByKeyword(..., PageRequest.of(0, 40))`, and page review enrichment now receives only `page.getContent()`. `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` completed successfully.
- Expected fix direction: Add repository-level public catalog page and count queries that apply active/public status, category descendants, keyword, featured/discount, price, refinement, and sort constraints in SQL/JPA before fetching rows. Enrich review stats only for the returned page ids, keep totals from a matching count query, and add regression tests or repository spies proving paginated `/products` and `/search` do not call `productRepository.findAll()`/global category loaders on ordinary page requests.

### F395: Public recommendation endpoints score full product catalogs before returning small candidate lists

- Area: Backend / public product recommendation APIs; Frontend / add-on assistant, pet finder, personalized recommendations
- Environment: Anonymous `/products/add-on-candidates`, anonymous `/products/finder-candidates`, signed-in `/products/personalized-recommendations`, carts/checkouts with add-on assistant, PetFinder, Home/ProductList/personalized assistant, and stores with large active catalogs
- Steps: Load the cart or checkout add-on assistant, open PetFinder, or load personalized recommendations for a customer with pet profiles on a store with many active products and categories.
- Expected: Recommendation candidate APIs should bound database work as well as response size, using repository queries with price/category/status/stock filters, pageable candidates, or precomputed recommendation inputs. A small response such as 3, 12, 36, or 60 items should not require loading and scoring the entire product and category tables on each uncached key.
- Actual: The endpoints cap the returned list but still build it from full in-memory catalog scans. Add-on candidates call `productRepository.findAll()`, filter/score/sort every product, then limit to at most 8. Finder candidates load all categories and all products, enrich review stats for every public sellable product, score/sort them, then limit to at most 60. Personalized recommendations load all categories and all products for each user cache miss, score them against pet profiles, and only then limit to 12. These paths can make ordinary cart, checkout, pet finder, home, and personalized-assistant views perform full-catalog reads and ranking work despite tiny response windows.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 07:25 UTC development pass changed the public recommendation cache-miss paths to use bounded repository candidate windows before scoring. Add-on recommendations now call `ProductRepository.findPublicAddOnCandidateWindow(...)`; finder and personalized recommendations use keyword/public sellable candidate windows plus a bounded category lookup for only the candidate products and their parents. Review stats are enriched only for the bounded candidate/result windows.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 07:02 UTC static review found `ProductController.getPersonalizedRecommendations()`, `getAddOnCandidates()`, and `getFinderCandidates()` expose `/products/personalized-recommendations`, `/products/add-on-candidates`, and `/products/finder-candidates` at lines 96-117. `ProductServiceImpl.findAddOnCandidatesUncached()` lines 724-746 calls `productRepository.findAll()`, filters `isReadyAddOnCandidate`, scores, sorts, and only then applies `.limit(normalizedLimit)` where callers clamp to 8. `findFinderCandidatesUncached()` lines 792-808 loads every category through `categoryRepository.findAll()`, loads every product through `productRepository.findAll()`, enriches review stats for the full public sellable set, then scores/sorts and limits to 60. `findPersonalizedRecommendationsUncached()` lines 842-864 loads all categories and all products, scores against all of the user's pet profiles, sorts, and limits to 12. The repository has pageable query helpers for some specific cases such as featured/related products, but no bounded candidate query is used here. Frontend callers include `AddOnAssistant.tsx` lines 54-64, `PetFinder.tsx` lines 61-71, and `PetPersonalizedAssistant.tsx` lines 60-79; the API wrappers call the three endpoints at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1330-1355 and 1258-1263. Existing F343 covered unbounded public `/products` responses, F387 covered `/products/featured`, F388 covered `/categories`, F352/F353 cover product-list pagination/sort correctness, and F395 covers the remaining full-catalog backend work hidden behind bounded recommendation response sizes.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 07:08 UTC tracker/source correction re-read current `ProductServiceImpl` and confirmed F395 remains OPEN: add-on candidates still call `productRepository.findAll()` before scoring and limiting at lines 736-746, finder candidates still call both `categoryRepository.findAll()` and `productRepository.findAll()` before enriching/scoring/limiting at lines 792-808, and personalized recommendations still call both full loaders before scoring and limiting at lines 842-864. The misplaced 07:04 admin-permission fix evidence belonged to F275/F274, not this recommendation issue.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 07:25 UTC static retest confirmed `findAddOnCandidatesUncached(...)` no longer calls `productRepository.findAll()` and instead requests a capped `findPublicAddOnCandidateWindow(...)`; `findFinderCandidatesUncached(...)` and `findPersonalizedRecommendationsUncached(...)` now call `boundedRecommendationCandidates(...)`, which uses `findPublicKeywordCandidateWindow(...)` and `findPublicSellableCandidateWindow(...)` with `PageRequest` caps. The category lookup for scoring is now `loadCategoryLookupForProducts(...)`, which calls `categoryRepository.findAllById(...)` only for candidate category ids and parent ids, not `categoryRepository.findAll()`. `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` completed successfully.
- Expected fix direction: Add bounded repository-level candidate queries for these recommendation paths, such as active/sellable products within price bands for add-ons, pageable keyword/category candidate retrieval for finder, and category/species/size-filtered candidate windows for personalization. Apply review-stat enrichment only after the candidate set is bounded, keep configured response caps, and add regression coverage proving these endpoints do not call full-table product/category loaders on cache misses.

### F394: Backend main compile fails after support session limit wiring only changed the controller

- Area: Backend / support session API compile gate; customer support session history boundedness fix
- Environment: `src/main/java/com/example/shop/controller/SupportController.java:66`, `./mvnw -DskipTests compile`
- Steps: Run `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` in `/home/guhao/shoptest` after the support session list endpoint was changed to accept a `limit` request parameter.
- Expected: Backend main sources should compile, and the `/support/sessions` boundedness fix should be implemented end-to-end through controller, service, mapper, and frontend callers.
- Actual: Backend main compilation fails because `SupportController.getMySessions()` calls `supportService.getUserSessions(currentUserId(), limit)`, but `SupportService` still only defines `getUserSessions(Long userId)`. The mapper still exposes only `findByUserId(Long userId)` with no `LIMIT`, and `supportApi.getSessions()` / `CustomerSupportWidget` still call `/support/sessions` without passing a bounded limit. The attempted F392 fix is therefore incomplete and the backend cannot build.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 06:54 UTC development pass completed the customer support session limit contract end-to-end. `SupportService` now exposes a capped two-argument `getUserSessions(userId, limit)`, `SupportSessionMapper.findByUserId(...)` accepts the normalized limit, the MyBatis query applies `LIMIT #{limit}`, and `supportApi.getSessions(...)` plus `CustomerSupportWidget` pass a bounded recent-session window.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 06:54 UTC `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` completed successfully after the missing service/mapper method was added, so the F394 compile gate is no longer failing. `npm run build` also completed successfully, confirming the frontend limit parameter wiring compiles.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 06:50 UTC `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` failed during main compilation with `SupportController.java:[66,49] method getUserSessions in class com.example.shop.service.SupportService cannot be applied to given types; required: java.lang.Long; found: java.lang.Long,java.lang.Integer`. Static review found `SupportController.getMySessions()` lines 64-67 accepts `Integer limit` and calls the missing two-argument service method, while `SupportService.getUserSessions()` lines 72-74 still delegates to `supportSessionMapper.findByUserId(userId)`, `SupportSessionMapper.java` line 17 declares only `findByUserId(Long userId)`, and `SupportSessionMapper.xml` lines 85-94 still orders every normal customer session with no cap. Frontend `supportApi.getSessions()` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` line 2251 still sends `/support/sessions` with no params, and `CustomerSupportWidget.tsx` lines 358-361 and 487-490 still call it from initial load and polling.
- Expected fix direction: Complete the F392 limit contract end-to-end or revert the controller signature until the service/mapper are ready. Add a capped service method, a limited mapper query or cursor/page contract, and frontend callers that pass the intended window size. Re-run `./mvnw -DskipTests compile`, then retest F392 boundedness.

### F393: Customer support session endpoints let broad admin roles read or close any session

- Area: Backend / customer support route authorization; admin/support RBAC boundary
- Environment: Authenticated non-user custom admin roles without `support`, `support:read-state`, or `support:close`; direct calls to `/support/sessions/{sessionId}/messages`, `/support/sessions/{sessionId}/read`, and `/support/sessions/{sessionId}/close`
- Steps: Assign a custom admin role such as alerts-only, catalog-only, logs-only, or another role with no support permissions; sign in as that operator; then call the customer support session message, read, or close endpoints with another customer's or guest-order support session id.
- Expected: Customer `/support/**` routes should be self-only for normal signed-in customer sessions, and guest support routes should require guest order credentials. Cross-customer/admin support reads and lifecycle mutations should go through `/admin/support/**` and enforce the dedicated support action permissions added for F375.
- Actual: The customer support session authorization helper returns true for any principal whose authorities include `ROLE_ADMIN` before checking session ownership or default-session context. Because custom non-`USER` role codes receive broad `ROLE_ADMIN`, a role with no support page or support action permission can call the customer support paths directly to read a bounded window of any support transcript, mark a session read, or close another customer's or guest-order support session. This bypasses the fixed `/admin/support/**` action-permission model and performs the customer close path without the admin audit logging used by the guarded admin close endpoint.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 06:49 UTC development pass removed the broad-admin shortcut from customer support session authorization. `canAccessSession()` now loads the session and requires the current principal's user id to match the session user id and the session to be a default customer session.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 06:45 UTC static review found `SupportController.getMyMessages()` lines 69-77, `markMyMessagesRead()` lines 80-86, and `closeMySession()` lines 104-109 all relied on `canAccessSession(sessionId)`. At that time the helper returned true immediately when `currentRole(user)` was `ADMIN`, before loading the session or checking `session.userId` and `isDefaultUserSession(...)`; `UserDetailsImpl.build()` granted `ROLE_ADMIN` to any non-empty, non-`USER` role code. Existing F375 fixed the `/admin/support/**` reply/read/close/assign/reopen authorization path, F392 covers unbounded customer session history, and F384 covered cart/address/notification/coupon/wishlist customer-resource admin overrides. F393 covers the remaining storefront support-session admin override.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 06:49 UTC static retest confirmed `SupportController.getMyMessages()`, `markMyMessagesRead()`, and `closeMySession()` still rely on `canAccessSession(sessionId)`, but `canAccessSession()` lines 345-350 now returns true only when the session exists, `user.getId().equals(session.getUserId())`, and `supportService.isDefaultUserSession(session)` is true. The prior `currentRole(...)` helper is no longer present in `SupportController`, while `/admin/support/**` lifecycle actions still route through `requireSupportActionPermission(...)` for the dedicated support action permissions. Runtime/API validation is blocked by the separate F394 backend compile failure.
- Expected fix direction: Remove the broad-admin shortcut from customer `/support/sessions/**` authorization. Keep signed-in customer session access self-only and null/blank-context only, require guest order credentials for guest-context sessions, and route all operator support reads or lifecycle actions through `/admin/support/**` with the existing support action permissions and audit logging. Add regression coverage proving custom admin roles without support permissions cannot use customer support URLs to read, mark, or close arbitrary sessions.

### F392: Customer support session history loads every normal session without pagination

- Area: Backend / customer support session list API; Frontend / CustomerSupportWidget session history
- Environment: Signed-in customers with many closed or reopened support conversations, `/support/sessions`, CustomerSupportWidget initial load and fallback polling
- Steps: Accumulate many normal signed-in customer support sessions for one account, then open the storefront support widget or leave it open on an active signed-in support session.
- Expected: Customer support session history should be bounded or cursor/page based, with a small recent-session window and explicit older-history loading. Polling should refresh only the active session summary or request a bounded delta, not reload the entire account support-session history.
- Actual: `GET /support/sessions` returns every normal support session for the signed-in user, ordered by `updated_at DESC`, with no `limit`, `page`, cursor, or total metadata. CustomerSupportWidget calls that full-list endpoint on initial widget load and again in the signed-in fallback polling loop alongside bounded message polling. Long-lived customer accounts can therefore make ordinary support widget use repeatedly download and sort the full support-session history even after F377 bounded per-conversation messages.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 06:54 UTC development pass added a bounded `/support/sessions` contract for signed-in customer session history. The controller accepts `limit`, the service clamps it to a default of 12 and maximum of 30, the mapper applies the SQL limit to default customer-context sessions, and the customer support widget requests only the configured recent-session window during initial load, polling, and session switching refreshes.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 06:39 UTC static review found `SupportController.getMySessions()` lines 64-67 returns `toCustomerSessions(supportService.getUserSessions(currentUserId()))` with no paging parameters. `SupportService.getUserSessions()` lines 72-74 delegates directly to `supportSessionMapper.findByUserId(userId)`, and `SupportSessionMapper.xml` lines 85-94 selects every null/blank-context session for the user ordered by `ss.updated_at DESC` without `LIMIT`, `OFFSET`, or cursor predicates. The frontend wrapper `supportApi.getSessions()` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 2247-2249 exposes only `/support/sessions`, while `CustomerSupportWidget.tsx` calls it on initial signed-in load at lines 351-360 and again during signed-in fallback polling at lines 481-490, then sorts the full returned array at lines 492-493. Existing F358 covers the admin support queue, and F377 covers per-conversation message history; F392 covers the remaining customer account session-history list.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 06:50 UTC backend compile retest found the attempted `limit` wiring is incomplete and does not build, tracked separately as F394. Static review also found the frontend still calls `supportApi.getSessions()` without a limit and the mapper still has no capped customer-session query, so F392 remains OPEN.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 06:54 UTC `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` and `npm run build` both completed successfully after the service, mapper, API helper, and widget callers were wired end-to-end. Static retest confirmed `SupportSessionMapper.xml` now limits `/support/sessions` results and `CustomerSupportWidget` no longer calls `supportApi.getSessions()` without a bounded limit.
- Expected fix direction: Add a bounded customer support session list contract, such as `limit` plus `beforeId`/`beforeUpdatedAt` or `page`/`size` with a server-side max. Update CustomerSupportWidget to request a small recent-session window, merge active-session updates without full-list polling, and load older conversations only on explicit history navigation. Add regression coverage proving `/support/sessions` cannot return an unbounded account history.

### F391: Backend main compile fails in category product-count batching lambda

- Area: Backend / category service compile gate; public category product-count fix
- Environment: `src/main/java/com/example/shop/service/impl/CategoryServiceImpl.java:170`, `./mvnw -DskipTests compile`
- Steps: Run `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` in `/home/guhao/shoptest`.
- Expected: Backend main sources compile so the category API and storefront can be built and regression-tested.
- Actual: Maven fails during main compile with `local variables referenced from a lambda expression must be final or effectively final` at `CategoryServiceImpl.java:170`. The category product-count batching implementation initializes `rootsByParentId` inside a `rootIds.forEach(...)` lambda and later reassigns `rootsByParentId` inside the descendant-expansion loop, so the variable is not effectively final and the backend cannot compile.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 06:08 UTC development pass replaced the `rootIds.forEach(...)` initialization with a plain `for` loop, so `rootsByParentId` is no longer captured by a lambda before being reassigned during descendant expansion.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 06:02 UTC `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` failed during `maven-compiler-plugin:compile` with `/home/guhao/shoptest/src/main/java/com/example/shop/service/impl/CategoryServiceImpl.java:[170,13] local variables referenced from a lambda expression must be final or effectively final`. Static review found `rootsByParentId` declared at line 167, used inside `rootIds.forEach(...)` at lines 168-171, and reassigned at line 195. This also invalidates the earlier static-only F388 fixed status until the category implementation compiles.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 06:08 UTC `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` completed successfully after the lambda capture was removed.
- Expected fix direction: Avoid capturing a reassigned local variable in the initialization lambda, such as replacing the `rootIds.forEach(...)` with a plain loop or using a separate final initialization map before the iterative reassignment. Re-run backend compile and then retest F388 behavior after the compile gate is green.

### F390: Frontend production build fails with MutationObserver type mismatch in mobileContrastGuard

- Area: Frontend / mobileContrastGuard.ts
- Environment: `frontend/src/utils/mobileContrastGuard.ts:1840`, `npm run build`
- Steps: Run `npm run build` in `/home/guhao/shoptest/frontend`
- Expected: Frontend production build compiles successfully
- Actual: TypeScript compilation fails with `TS2345: Argument of type '(delayMs?: number) => void' is not assignable to parameter of type 'MutationCallback'` at line 1840. The `scheduleMobileContrastScan` function signature doesn't match `MutationObserver` callback type.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 05:59 UTC development pass wrapped the `MutationObserver` body callback so it calls `scheduleMobileContrastScan()` without receiving observer arguments, restoring the expected `MutationCallback` type while keeping deferred mobile contrast rescans.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 12:28 UTC `npm run build` failed with `TS2345` at `frontend/src/utils/mobileContrastGuard.ts:1840`
- Retest: 2026-05-31 05:55 UTC `npm run build` still failed with the same `TS2345` at `frontend/src/utils/mobileContrastGuard.ts:1840`: `new MutationObserver(scheduleMobileContrastScan)` passes a `(delayMs?: number) => void` function where the DOM API expects a `MutationCallback` receiving mutation records and the observer. Status remains OPEN.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 05:59 UTC static retest confirmed the body observer now uses `new MutationObserver(() => scheduleMobileContrastScan())`, so mutation records are no longer passed into the delay parameter. Full frontend build was not rerun in this development pass.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 06:02 UTC `npm run build` in `/home/guhao/shoptest/frontend` completed successfully, confirming the production TypeScript gate no longer fails at `mobileContrastGuard.ts:1840`. Status remains FIXED.

- Area: Backend / ProductServiceImpl.java
- Environment: `src/main/java/com/example/shop/service/impl/ProductServiceImpl.java:350`, `./mvnw test`
- Steps: Run `./mvnw test` in `/home/guhao/shoptest`

- Actual: Maven fails at main compile (not testCompile) with `cannot find symbol: method normalizeFeaturedProductLimit(int)` in `ProductServiceImpl.java:350`. The method is called but not defined.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 05:54 UTC current source defines `normalizeFeaturedProductLimit(...)` and the backend main compile gate succeeds, so the transient featured-product limit compile failure is no longer present.

- Evidence: 2026-05-31 05:54 UTC static retest found `ProductServiceImpl.findPublicFeaturedProducts(int)` calls `normalizeFeaturedProductLimit(limit)` at lines 349-351, while `normalizeFeaturedProductLimit(int limit)` is now present with default/max clamping. `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` completed successfully, confirming backend main compilation no longer fails on the missing method.

### F388: Public category list performs unbounded recursive product-count work

- Area: Backend / public category API boundedness and query efficiency; Frontend / home and product-list startup
- Environment: Anonymous `GET /categories` with no `parentId` or `level`, home page startup, product list/category filter loading, and catalogs with many categories or broad category trees
- Steps: Create a large category tree with active products assigned across multiple levels, then load the home page, product list, or call `/categories` directly without filters.
- Expected: Public category discovery should return a bounded or explicitly scoped category set, such as top-level categories plus lazy-loaded children, and compute product counts in batch. A single anonymous request should not require loading every category and recursively counting descendants one category at a time.
- Actual: `GET /categories` with no filter returns every category and enriches every row with `productCount`. For each returned category, the service recursively walks descendants through repeated `findByParentId(...)` calls and then runs a product count over that category-id set. Home and product-list startup call the all-category endpoint, so a large taxonomy can turn ordinary anonymous page loads into full category-table responses plus N+1/recursive database work before the UI displays a small navigation/filter set.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 06:08 UTC development pass changed anonymous `/categories` without filters to return top-level categories instead of the full taxonomy, added explicit `level` handling, switched home and product-list startup to `categoryApi.getTopLevel()`, moved product-management category loading to `/admin/categories`, and replaced per-category recursive product counts with batched child/category-id product-count aggregation. The follow-up F391 compile error in that batching implementation was fixed in the same pass.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 05:47 UTC static review found `CategoryController.getAll()` lines 34-45 returns `categoryService.findAll()` whenever `parentId` is absent and `level` is not `1`, then maps every row through `CategoryPublicResponse`. `CategoryServiceImpl.findAll()` lines 24-27 calls `withProductCounts(categoryRepository.findAll())`, and `withProductCounts()` lines 130-135 calls `countPublicProducts(category.getId())` for each category. `countPublicProducts()` lines 138-147 calls `findSelfAndDescendantIds(categoryId)` and then `productRepository.countByCategoryIdInAndStatusIgnoreCase(...)`; `findSelfAndDescendantIds()` lines 43-47 delegates to recursive `collectDescendantIds()`, which calls `categoryRepository.findByParentId(id)` for every visited node at lines 110-113. The repository has only unpaged `findAll()`, `findByParentIdIsNull()`, and `findByParentId(...)` methods at `CategoryRepository` lines 10-13. The public DTO includes `productCount` and localized content at `CategoryPublicResponse` lines 7-30. The frontend calls `categoryApi.getAll()` on home startup at `Home.tsx` lines 463-466 and in the product list category cache path at `ProductList.tsx` lines 645-655 through `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1636-1643, while top-level and children helpers already exist but are not used for those all-category startup reads. Existing public category issues covered DTO minimization and by-id route availability; F388 covers the remaining unbounded recursive work in the public category list.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 05:55 UTC static retest found F388 remains OPEN. `CategoryController.getAll()` lines 34-44 still returns `categoryService.findAll()` for unfiltered `/categories`; `CategoryServiceImpl.findAll()` lines 24-27 still wraps `categoryRepository.findAll()` in `withProductCounts(...)`; and `withProductCounts()`/`countPublicProducts()` lines 130-146 still compute counts one category at a time through recursive descendant collection. `Home.tsx` line 467 and `ProductList.tsx` lines 307-321 still call/cache `categoryApi.getAll()`. `CategoryRepository.findByParentIdIn(...)` now exists, but this public full-list/count path does not use it.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 05:59 UTC static retest confirmed unfiltered `CategoryController.getAll()` now returns `categoryService.findTopLevel()`, explicit `level` requests call `findByLevel(level)`, `Home.tsx` and `ProductList.tsx` call `categoryApi.getTopLevel()`, `ProductManagement.tsx` uses `adminApi.getCategories()`, and `CategoryServiceImpl.withProductCounts(...)` now uses batched descendant expansion plus `ProductRepository.countPublicProductsByCategoryIds(...)` instead of per-category recursive count queries. Full backend/frontend builds were not rerun in this development pass.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 06:02 UTC backend compile retest invalidated the static-only fixed status. `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` now fails in the F388 category-count batching implementation with `local variables referenced from a lambda expression must be final or effectively final` at `CategoryServiceImpl.java:170`, tracked separately as F391. F388 remains OPEN until the category implementation compiles and the bounded top-level/batched-count behavior can be regression-tested from a buildable backend.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 06:08 UTC `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` completed successfully after F391 was fixed. Static retest confirmed the bounded category behavior remains in place: unfiltered public `/categories` returns top-level categories, explicit `level` requests use `findByLevel(level)`, frontend startup paths call `categoryApi.getTopLevel()`, and public product counts are aggregated through batched descendant/category-id queries.
- Expected fix direction: Avoid full-tree category loading on anonymous startup paths. Use top-level `/categories?level=1` plus lazy child loading where possible, add a bounded/paginated all-category contract only for supported callers, and compute public product counts in one batched query grouped by category/descendant instead of per-category recursion. Add regression coverage for large category trees and ensure home/product-list do not require full taxonomy loading to render initial UI.

### F387: Featured products endpoint returns every featured product without a server-side cap

- Area: Backend / public product API boundedness; Frontend / home page product loading
- Environment: Anonymous `GET /products/featured`, home page startup, and catalogs where many products are flagged `isFeatured=true`
- Steps: Mark a large number of active products as featured, then load the home page or call `/products/featured` directly.
- Expected: Public featured-product APIs should return only the small carousel/homepage window the storefront can display, or expose explicit bounded pagination with a documented maximum. Home page startup should not download every featured product when it only renders the first 12.
- Actual: `/products/featured` returns every public featured product. The service queries all featured rows, filters/maps them into full public product DTOs, and the home page then slices the response to 12 after downloading and merging it into the local product snapshot. A large featured set can therefore make an anonymous home-page request return large JSON payloads containing product images, detail content, variants, option groups, localized content, bundle, warranty, shipping, and review fields that are not needed for the visible featured rail.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 05:55 UTC development pass added a bounded featured-product contract with a default limit, server-side maximum, repository `Pageable`, and a home-page call that passes the visible featured limit.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 05:39 UTC static review found `ProductController.getFeaturedProducts()` returns `toPublicProducts(productService.findPublicFeaturedProducts())` at lines 90-92 with no `limit`, `page`, or `size` parameter. `ProductServiceImpl.findPublicFeaturedProducts()` lines 342-346 calls `productRepository.findByIsFeaturedTrueOrderByIdAsc()`, filters public catalog products, sorts by id, and collects the entire stream without a cap; the repository method at `ProductRepository` line 16 has no `Pageable`. `ProductPublicResponse` includes heavy storefront fields including images, specifications, i18n, detailContent, variants, optionGroups, localizedContent, bundle, warranty, and shipping at lines 10-44 and maps them at lines 46-84. The frontend calls `productApi.getFeatured()` on home startup at `Home.tsx` lines 463-466, merges the full featured response into the bounded catalog snapshot at lines 468-470, and only then slices visible featured products to 12 at line 471. `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1316-1319 exports `getFeatured()` as an anonymous GET to `/products/featured` with no pagination parameters. Existing F343 covered full-catalog `/products` loading and was fixed by bounding `getAll`; F235/F295 cover `/search` response size; F387 covers the remaining unbounded public featured-products endpoint.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 05:55 UTC static retest confirmed `ProductController.getFeaturedProducts()` lines 90-93 accepts `limit` with default `12` and calls `productService.findPublicFeaturedProducts(limit)`. `ProductServiceImpl` defines `DEFAULT_FEATURED_PRODUCT_LIMIT = 12` and `MAX_FEATURED_PRODUCT_LIMIT = 36` at lines 67-68, routes the no-arg service method through the default at lines 343-345, normalizes the requested limit at lines 349-351 and 624-628, and queries `productRepository.findPublicFeaturedProducts(PageRequest.of(0, normalizedLimit))` before a defensive `.limit(normalizedLimit)` at lines 351-355. `ProductRepository.findPublicFeaturedProducts(Pageable pageable)` lines 17-23 returns only active, storefront-valid featured rows ordered by id. The frontend wrapper normalizes the optional limit to 12-36 and sends it as `/products/featured?limit=...` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1316-1324, and `Home.tsx` line 466 calls `productApi.getFeatured(HOME_FEATURED_LIMIT)`. `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` succeeded; the remaining frontend production build failure is tracked separately as F390.
- Expected fix direction: Add a service-side featured limit or paginated `/products/featured` contract with a low default and maximum, pass that bound from `Home.tsx`, and avoid merging non-visible featured rows into the home product snapshot. Consider a lightweight featured-card DTO if the rail does not need full product detail/variant content.

### F386: Order-context logistics tracking still trusts broad admin role

- Area: Backend / logistics tracking RBAC and provider credential use; Frontend / shared logistics tracking helper
- Environment: `GET /logistics/track?trackingNumber=...&orderId=...`, configured Kuaidi100 or generic logistics provider, and custom admin roles without `orders` or `orders:fulfillment`
- Steps: Assign a custom admin role such as alerts-only, catalog-only, logs-only, or another non-user role without the `orders` page and without `orders:fulfillment`; sign in as that operator; then call `/logistics/track` with a known customer `orderId` plus its tracking number and carrier.
- Expected: Order-context logistics tracking should be available to the order owner or verified guest credentials, and any admin/operator lookup for another customer's order should require the same order page/fulfillment authority used by `/admin/orders` and no-order operational tracking. A custom admin role that cannot view or fulfill orders should not be able to query carrier tracking for another customer's shipment through store provider credentials.
- Actual: `LogisticsService.resolveVisibleOrder()` still authorizes any authenticated broad admin for order-context tracking by calling `SecurityUtils.assertSelfOrAdmin(authentication, order.getUserId())`. Because custom non-`USER` role codes receive broad `ROLE_ADMIN`, a custom admin role with no `orders` or `orders:fulfillment` permission can bypass the fixed no-order check by supplying an `orderId` and can trigger Kuaidi100 or generic provider lookup for that shipment. This leaks shipment status/events for known order ids and consumes provider quota outside the order-management permission boundary.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 05:55 UTC development pass removed the order-context broad-admin bypass. Cross-customer admin logistics tracking now calls the same operational tracking permission guard used by no-order lookups, while owner and verified guest order access still return before the admin branch.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 05:31 UTC static review found `SecurityConfig` still permits `GET /logistics/track` at line 79, and `LogisticsController.track()` lines 21-30 forwards optional `orderId`, `guestEmail`, `orderNo`, and `Authentication` directly into `LogisticsService.track(...)`. The no-order branch was fixed: `LogisticsService.resolveVisibleOrder()` lines 93-96 now calls `requireOperationalTrackingPermission(...)`, and lines 115-128 require an authenticated admin with `orders:fulfillment`. However, the order-context branch loads the order at lines 101-104, accepts guest credentials at lines 105-107, then lets any signed-in broad admin pass `SecurityUtils.assertSelfOrAdmin(authentication, order.getUserId())` at lines 108-110 with no `AdminRoleService.canAccess("/admin/orders")` or `orders:fulfillment` check. `SecurityUtils.assertSelfOrAdmin(...)` returns for any `ROLE_ADMIN` at lines 30-38, and `UserDetailsImpl.build()` grants `ROLE_ADMIN` to any non-empty custom role code at lines 39-58. After that, `trackInternal(...)` uses the configured Kuaidi100/provider paths at `LogisticsService` lines 77-83 and 143-204. `frontend/src/api/index.ts` still exports `logisticsApi.track()` with an optional `orderId` and authenticated request config at lines 2200-2231. Existing F383 covered no-order lookups, F380 covers legacy order read/action routes, F101 covers anonymous registered-order access, and F256/F257/F260 cover provider validation/response handling; F386 covers the remaining order-context admin permission bypass.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 05:55 UTC static retest confirmed `LogisticsService.resolveVisibleOrder()` lines 94-119 now returns the order for verified guest credentials or the owning user, but the cross-customer admin branch at lines 114-117 calls `requireOperationalTrackingPermission(authentication)` before returning. That helper at lines 122-135 requires an authenticated admin plus `adminRoleService.canAccess(user.getId(), "/admin/orders")` and `AdminRoleService.ORDER_FULFILLMENT_PERMISSION`, otherwise it throws 403. The prior `SecurityUtils.assertSelfOrAdmin(...)` shortcut is no longer used in the order-context branch.
- Expected fix direction: For order-context `/logistics/track` calls, keep owner and verified guest access, but require cross-customer admin callers to have `/admin/orders` plus `orders:fulfillment` or a dedicated `logistics:track` permission before provider lookup. Audit cross-customer tracking lookups and update the shared frontend helper/admin widgets so roles without that permission cannot initiate carrier queries.

### F385: Pet profile per-user limit is bypassable by concurrent creates

- Area: Backend / pet profile account limits and birthday-coupon input integrity
- Environment: Concurrent `POST /pet-profiles` requests for the same signed-in customer near `pet-profile.max-per-user`, plus pet birthday coupon automation that reads all pet profile rows
- Steps: Configure or use the default `pet-profile.max-per-user`, create an account with one slot remaining, then submit two or more valid pet-profile create requests for that same account at the same time.
- Expected: The per-user pet-profile limit should be enforced atomically. At most one concurrent create should fill the remaining slot, and later requests should fail with a controlled limit response. The database or service transaction should guarantee that a user cannot exceed the configured maximum even under parallel requests.
- Actual: `PetProfileService.save()` checks the current count by loading all existing profiles and comparing `size() >= maxProfilesPerUser()` before inserting a new row. There is no locking read, per-user slot table, database constraint, or post-insert enforcement, and the schema has only an index on `user_id`. Concurrent create requests can all observe the same below-limit count and insert, leaving more pet profiles than the configured maximum. Those extra rows then participate in personalized recommendations and birthday-coupon scans/reissues, creating inconsistent customer benefits and operational data.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 05:19 UTC development pass added a transactional user-row lock before per-user pet-profile create counting, switched the create limit check to a locked `countByUserId(...)`, and returns HTTP 409 for limit races through `PetProfileController`, so same-user concurrent creates serialize before insert.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 05:08 UTC static review found `PetProfileController.create()` lines 31-39 accepts authenticated profile creation and delegates directly to `petProfileService.save(userDetails.getId(), request, null)`. `PetProfileService.save()` lines 24-53 enforces the create limit with `petProfileMapper.findByUserId(userId).size() >= maxProfilesPerUser()` at lines 32-33 and then inserts at lines 45-47; the method never locks the user's existing rows or rechecks after insert. `maxProfilesPerUser()` lines 133-135 reads `pet-profile.max-per-user` and clamps it to 1-50, and the shipped default is `PET_PROFILE_MAX_PER_USER:10` at `application.properties` line 144. `PetProfileMapper.findByUserId()` selects rows ordered by created time at `PetProfileMapper.xml` lines 17-19, while the insert at lines 44-47 is independent of the count check. The `pet_profiles` table in both `schema.sql` lines 200-212 and `V2__pet_tables.sql` lines 2-14 defines only an index on `user_id`, not a per-user count invariant. `PetBirthdayCouponService.grantBirthdayCoupons()` scans all birthday pet profiles at lines 81-113 and `reissueBirthdayCoupons()` scans all matching user pet profiles at lines 116-131, so over-limit profiles remain active inputs to coupon automation subject only to the separate birthday-benefit cap. Existing F252 covers unsafe principal casts in the same controller, F266 covers address-book count/default races, and F370/F355 cover coupon action authorization; F385 covers the missing atomicity of the pet-profile per-user limit itself.
- Expected fix direction: Enforce the per-user pet-profile limit under a transaction-safe invariant, such as locking the user's profile rows or a per-user counter before checking and inserting, and return a controlled 409/400 when the cap is reached. Add a database-backed guard where possible, add concurrent create regression coverage, and make birthday-coupon jobs ignore or flag impossible over-limit states until existing data is cleaned up.

### F384: Customer account resource routes trust broad admin role instead of explicit support or user permissions

- Area: Backend / storefront account-resource RBAC and customer data privacy
- Environment: Direct customer-resource calls such as `GET /cart?userId=...`, `POST /cart/add`, `DELETE /cart/clear?userId=...`, `GET /addresses?userId=...`, `POST /addresses`, `PUT /addresses/{id}`, `DELETE /addresses/{id}`, `GET /notifications?userId=...`, `PUT /notifications/read-all?userId=...`, `DELETE /notifications/{id}`, `GET /coupons/user/{userId}`, `GET /coupons/user/{userId}/available`, `POST /coupons/{couponId}/claim?userId=...`, `POST /coupons/quote`, `GET /wishlist?userId=...`, and `POST /wishlist/toggle`, using a custom admin role that lacks `users`, `support`, `orders`, `coupons`, or `notifications` permissions
- Steps: Assign a non-user custom admin role such as alerts-only, catalog-only, logs-only, or another role with no customer-support/user/order authority; sign in as that operator; then call the customer account-resource endpoints with another customer's `userId`, address id, cart item id, notification id, coupon id, or wishlist product id.
- Expected: Storefront customer routes should authorize only the resource owner. Any cross-customer admin/support operation should go through an admin or support endpoint that enforces an explicit permission, scopes the fields returned for that workflow, and records the operational action. A custom admin role with no customer-management authority should not read or mutate another customer's address book, cart, notification state, coupon wallet, or wishlist through customer URLs.
- Actual: The customer-resource controllers still use `SecurityUtils.assertSelfOrAdmin(...)` or `SecurityUtils.isAdmin(...)` as a cross-user override on non-admin URLs. Because custom non-`USER` role codes receive broad `ROLE_ADMIN`, any custom admin role can impersonate cross-user ownership checks on these routes. The operator can read customer contact/address data, shopping intent, notification content/read state, coupon wallet and checkout-quote state, and wishlist state; several routes can also add/delete/update addresses, cart lines, notification read/delete state, coupon claims, and wishlist entries for customers without any `users`, `support`, `orders`, `coupons`, or `notifications` permission.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 05:19 UTC development pass removed the broad-admin override from storefront customer-resource ownership checks. `/cart`, `/addresses`, `/notifications`, `/coupons`, and `/wishlist` cross-user legacy parameters now require the authenticated user's own id through `SecurityUtils.assertSelf(...)`, address creation always binds to the current principal, and cart bulk removal uses self-only ownership validation.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:59 UTC static review found `WebMvcConfig` registers `AdminPermissionInterceptor` only for `/admin/**`, `/products/**`, `/brands/**`, and `/categories/**` at lines 15-22, while `SecurityConfig` leaves the customer resource routes under plain `anyRequest().authenticated()` at lines 117-118. `UserDetailsImpl.build()` grants `ROLE_ADMIN` to any non-empty, non-`USER` role code at lines 52-57, and `SecurityUtils.assertSelfOrAdmin(...)` returns immediately for any user with that broad role at lines 30-38. `NotificationController` cross-user list/count/read-all/delete paths call that helper at lines 28-31, 39-42, 61-64, and 74-81 before `NotificationService` reads or mutates notification rows at lines 24-33 and 94-106. `UserAddressController` cross-user reads and mutations use the same broad-admin override at lines 33-55 and 84-129, while `addAddress()` lets broad admins submit an arbitrary `userId` at lines 64-82; `UserAddressResponse` includes recipient name, phone, and address at lines 20-25, and `UserAddressService` writes address/default state at lines 30-79. `CartController` permits cross-user cart reads, add/update/remove/clear through `assertSelfOrAdmin(...)` at lines 23-57, 77-125, and 133-147, and `CartService` reads/writes cart lines at lines 30-131. `CouponController` lets broad admins claim public coupons for another user, read that user's wallet/available coupons, and run checkout quotes at lines 36-87; `CouponService` returns wallet DTOs at lines 100-115 and inserts claims at lines 170-197, while `OrderService.quoteCheckout()` validates the selected customer cart and returns quote amounts at lines 242-250. `WishlistController` uses the same broad-admin override for cross-user wishlist read/check/count/toggle/remove at lines 27-60 and 71-89, and `WishlistService` reads and mutates wishlist rows at lines 25-60. Existing F231 covers the broad role-code authority source, F380-F383 cover order/payment/logistics compatibility surfaces, F365 covers admin notification broadcast authorization, F370 covers admin coupon-management actions, F120/F139/F136 cover DTO/cache minimization, F265/F266 cover wishlist/address validation/concurrency, and F233/F236 cover notification scale; F384 covers the remaining cross-customer access and mutation bypass across customer account-resource routes.
- Expected fix direction: Remove the admin override from storefront `/cart`, `/addresses`, `/notifications`, `/coupons`, and `/wishlist` customer routes so they are self-only, or split cross-customer workflows into `/admin` or `/admin/support` routes guarded by explicit permissions such as `users`, `support`, `orders`, `coupons:grant`, and `notifications:manage`. Return purpose-built admin/support DTOs, audit cross-customer reads and mutations, and retire legacy userId-based storefront helpers that invite direct cross-user calls.

### F383: No-order logistics tracking trusts broad admin role instead of explicit order or logistics permission

- Area: Backend / logistics tracking RBAC; Frontend / exported logistics tracking helper
- Environment: `GET /logistics/track` with `trackingNumber` and no `orderId`, custom admin roles without `orders`, `orders:fulfillment`, or logistics-carrier/admin shipping permissions, and configured real logistics providers
- Steps: Assign a custom admin role such as alerts-only, catalog-only, notification-only, or another non-user role without order/logistics permissions; sign in as that operator; then call `/logistics/track?trackingNumber=...&carrier=...` without an order context.
- Expected: Logistics tracking should require a verified order context for customers/guests, or an explicit admin order/logistics permission before allowing no-order provider lookups. A role that cannot see orders or perform fulfillment should not be able to query arbitrary tracking numbers through the store's logistics provider credentials.
- Actual: The endpoint is publicly routed and the service permits no-order tracking whenever the authenticated principal has broad `ROLE_ADMIN`. Because custom non-user role codes receive `ROLE_ADMIN`, any custom admin role can query arbitrary tracking numbers without the `orders` page, `orders:fulfillment`, or a dedicated logistics tracking permission. When a real provider is configured, this can disclose shipment status/events for tracking numbers unrelated to orders the operator is authorized to inspect and can consume provider quota outside a fulfillment workflow.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 05:19 UTC development pass changed no-order logistics tracking to require an authenticated admin with `orders:fulfillment`, while keeping order-context tracking available to the owner or verified guest. The exported frontend logistics helper no longer sends no-order lookups through anonymous GET config, so operational tracking uses the authenticated permission path.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:49 UTC static review found `SecurityConfig` permits `GET /logistics/track` at line 79. `LogisticsController.track()` lines 21-30 forwards optional `orderId`, `guestEmail`, `orderNo`, and `Authentication` to `LogisticsService.track(...)`. In `LogisticsService.resolveVisibleOrder()`, a missing `orderId` is allowed when `authentication.getPrincipal()` is `UserDetailsImpl` and `SecurityUtils.isAdmin(user)` is true at lines 90-96; otherwise the method throws. There is no `AdminRoleService` check for `orders`, `orders:fulfillment`, `logistics-carriers`, or any dedicated tracking permission before `trackInternal(...)` calls Kuaidi100 or another configured provider at lines 72-80 and 142-191. `frontend/src/api/index.ts` still exports `logisticsApi.track()` with optional `orderId` at lines 2218-2250 and uses `optionalAnonymousGetConfig(...)` for no-order calls, so a signed-in custom admin can send the same helper request with credentials. Existing F101 fixed anonymous registered-order logistics access and explicitly left no-order lookups limited to signed-in admins; F256/F257/F260 cover provider URL/response/tracking-number validation; F369 covers logistics-carrier configuration writes. F383 covers the remaining least-privilege gap for arbitrary no-order tracking lookups by broad admin roles.
- Expected fix direction: Require an order context for all storefront tracking calls, and move no-order provider lookups behind an admin route that enforces an explicit permission such as `orders:fulfillment` or `logistics:track`. If no-order lookup is retained for operations, expose the permission in role management, audit the lookup with tracking number/carrier metadata, and hide or block frontend calls when the operator lacks that permission.

### F382: General payment history and sync routes bypass the admin order payment permission

- Area: Backend / legacy payment API RBAC and side effects; Frontend / exported payment API compatibility helpers
- Environment: `GET /payments/order/{orderId}`, `GET /payment/order/{orderId}`, `GET /payments/order/{orderId}/latest`, `GET /payment/order/{orderId}/latest`, `POST /payments/{id}/sync`, `POST /payment/{id}/sync`, and custom admin roles that should not have `orders` or `orders:payment`
- Steps: Assign an admin role such as support-only, alerts-only, catalog-only, or another custom non-user role without `orders` and `orders:payment`; sign in as that operator; then call the general payment history/latest or sync routes for another customer's order/payment.
- Expected: Admin payment history and payment sync should use `/admin/orders/**` and enforce the `orders` page plus `orders:payment` action permission before returning operational payment rows or invoking provider sync/expiry. Customer/general payment routes should behave as customer/guest recovery APIs and should not grant cross-order admin payment access or full `Payment` entity responses based only on broad `ROLE_ADMIN`.
- Actual: The general payment controller still has an admin branch: any authenticated principal with broad `ROLE_ADMIN` passes `assertSelfOrAdmin(...)` for any order, can read full `Payment` entities for arbitrary orders through the general history/latest endpoints, and can call `POST /payments/{id}/sync` to run provider sync/expiry without `orders` or `orders:payment`. This bypasses the newer `/admin/orders/payments/{paymentId}/sync` action guard and keeps exported frontend helpers that can accidentally reconnect admin workflows to the general payment surface.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 05:19 UTC development pass changed the general payment routes to return `PaymentResponse` only and require explicit admin order-payment authority for cross-order admin access. Owner and verified guest payment recovery remain available, while non-owner admin access now requires `/admin/orders` access plus `orders:payment`.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:43 UTC static review found `PaymentController.syncPayment()` lines 133-143 calls `assertCanOperatePayment(...)` and then `paymentService.syncPayment(id)` without any `AdminRoleService` or `orders:payment` guard. `findByOrderId()` and `findLatestByOrderId()` at lines 201-225 call `assertCanSeeOrder(...)` and then return `paymentResponses(...)`; `paymentResponse()`/`paymentResponses()` at lines 300-308 return raw `Payment` entities whenever `isAdmin(authentication)` is true. The shared authorization path `assertCanOperateOrder()` lines 285-291 delegates authenticated callers to `SecurityUtils.assertSelfOrAdmin(...)`, and `SecurityUtils.assertSelfOrAdmin(...)` returns immediately for any broad admin role. `PaymentService.findByOrderId()` lines 276-294, `syncPayment()` lines 297-311, and `findLatestByOrderId()` lines 318-329 can call `syncProviderPaymentState(...)` and `expirePayment(...)`; `expirePayment()` lines 381-385 can cancel the order, and `syncProviderPaymentState()` lines 764-796 can mark Stripe payments paid/expired and move orders to `PENDING_SHIPMENT`. The safer admin sync path exists separately: `AdminController.syncOrderPayment()` is already covered by F347's `orders:payment` fix, but the general route remains callable. Frontend `paymentApi.sync()`, `getByOrder()`, and `getLatestByOrder()` remain exported at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1578-1591. Existing F341 covers payment GET endpoints being state-mutating for customer/guest reads, F350 covers the `/admin/orders/{id}/payments` GET side effects for `orders` read roles, F347 covers now-fixed `/admin/orders/**` mutation guards, and F152 covered moving current admin pages away from general payment helpers; F382 covers the remaining direct admin-branch permission bypass on the general payment routes.
- Expected fix direction: Split customer/guest payment routes from admin payment operations. General `/payments/**` should authorize only the order owner or verified guest and return `PaymentResponse` only; cross-order admin reads and sync should go through `/admin/orders/**` with the `orders` page and `orders:payment` action permission. Remove or quarantine exported frontend helpers that could be reused for admin payment history/sync.

### F381: Legacy public catalog write routes bypass catalog action permissions

- Area: Backend / legacy catalog API RBAC; Frontend / exported catalog API compatibility helpers
- Environment: `POST /products`, `PUT /products/{id}`, `DELETE /products/{id}`, `POST /brands`, `PUT /brands/{id}`, `DELETE /brands/{id}`, `POST /categories`, `PUT /categories/{id}`, `DELETE /categories/{id}`, and custom catalog roles that have page visibility without `products:write`, `products:delete`, `brands:write`, `brands:delete`, `categories:write`, or `categories:delete`
- Steps: Assign an admin role only the `products`, `brands`, or `categories` page permission but not the matching action permissions; sign in as that operator; then call the legacy public resource write routes directly, or call the still-exported `productApi`, `brandApi`, or `categoryApi` create/update/delete helpers.
- Expected: Legacy compatibility write routes should be removed, disabled, or enforce the same action permissions as the current `/admin/products/**`, `/admin/brands/**`, and `/admin/categories/**` routes. Read-only catalog roles should not be able to create, update, publish/deactivate, reorder, or delete catalog records by bypassing the admin route namespace.
- Actual: The newer admin catalog routes now require dedicated action permissions, but the legacy public resource controllers still perform product, brand, and category create/update/delete after only page-level catalog access plus broad `ROLE_ADMIN` checks. A role with catalog page visibility but no write/delete action authority can directly mutate storefront catalog, brand, and taxonomy data through the old URLs. The frontend still exports the legacy mutation helpers, so future code can accidentally reintroduce the bypass even though current management pages use `adminApi`.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 05:19 UTC development pass added action-permission checks to the legacy public catalog write routes. `/products` create/update/delete now require `products:write` or `products:delete`; `/brands` requires `brands:write` or `brands:delete`; `/categories` requires `categories:write` or `categories:delete`, with failed attempts audited before returning 403.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:37 UTC static review found `WebMvcConfig` still registers `AdminPermissionInterceptor` for `/products/**`, `/brands/**`, and `/categories/**` at lines 15-22, but `AdminRoleService.WRITE_PATH_PERMISSIONS` maps those non-admin paths only to the page permissions `products`, `brands`, and `categories` at lines 158-162. `canAccess()` uses `permissionForPath(...)` at lines 261-266, and non-`/admin` paths flow through `permissionForWritePath(...)` at lines 302-325; no action permission is selected. `SecurityConfig` separately gates public catalog writes with only `hasRole("ADMIN")` at lines 95-109. `ProductController` legacy create/update/delete at lines 142-157, 165-183, and 234-245; `BrandController` legacy create/update/delete at lines 36-50, 58-76, and 90-104; and `CategoryController` legacy create/update/delete at lines 48-62, 70-88, and 102-112 all call the services and audit writes without `requireAdminActionPermission(...)`. By contrast, the current admin paths in `AdminController` require `PRODUCTS_WRITE_PERMISSION`/`PRODUCTS_DELETE_PERMISSION` at lines 148-210, `BRANDS_WRITE_PERMISSION`/`BRANDS_DELETE_PERMISSION` at lines 229-290, and `CATEGORIES_WRITE_PERMISSION`/`CATEGORIES_DELETE_PERMISSION` at lines 310-370. The frontend still exports legacy mutation helpers at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1381-1383, 1665-1673, and 1685-1693, while the active admin helpers use `/admin/*` at lines 1922-1953. Existing F150 covered moving back-office UI writes to admin paths, F368/F369 cover the now-guarded admin mutation surface, and F381 covers the remaining legacy public-path bypass of those action permissions.
- Expected fix direction: Delete the legacy public catalog write endpoints and helpers if no supported client still needs them, or make those compatibility routes call the same catalog action guard as `/admin/*` before any service write. Keep public GET catalog routes on narrowed storefront DTOs, and remove or clearly quarantine the legacy frontend mutation helpers so admin UI code cannot accidentally bypass the `/admin/*` permission contract.

### F380: Legacy order admin compatibility routes bypass the admin order permission model

- Area: Backend / legacy order API RBAC; Frontend / exported order API compatibility helpers
- Environment: Direct `/orders`, `/orders/user/{userId}`, `/orders/{id}`, `/orders/{id}/items`, `PUT /orders/{id}`, `DELETE /orders/{id}`, `POST /orders/{id}/items`, `PUT /orders/{id}/pay`, `PUT /orders/{id}/ship`, and custom admin roles that do not have the `orders` page permission or the newer `orders:*` action permissions
- Steps: Create or assign a custom admin role such as an alert-only, support-only, or other non-user role without `orders`, `orders:payment`, or `orders:fulfillment`; sign in as that operator; then call legacy `/orders` endpoints directly to list/read orders, add an order item, confirm payment, ship an order, update a raw order payload, or delete an order.
- Expected: Admin order reads and operational writes should use the `/admin/orders/**` permission boundary or enforce the same page/action permissions on any legacy compatibility path. A custom admin role without `orders` should not read full order entities, and a role without `orders:payment`, `orders:fulfillment`, or another explicit order action permission should not mutate payment, shipment, item, or raw order state through non-admin URLs.
- Actual: The legacy `/orders/**` admin compatibility paths sit outside the admin permission interceptor and rely only on broad `ROLE_ADMIN` checks or `SecurityUtils.isAdmin(...)` response branching. Any custom role code receives `ROLE_ADMIN`, so a role that lacks the `orders` page can still retrieve full order/order-item entities through `/orders`, `/orders/user/{userId}`, `/orders/{id}`, and `/orders/{id}/items`, while a role with no order action authorities can call legacy update/delete/add-item/pay/ship paths. This bypasses the newer `/admin/orders/**` least-privilege guards and keeps exported frontend helpers available for future accidental reuse.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 05:19 UTC development pass narrowed legacy `/orders/**` admin compatibility behavior. Cross-order reads now require `/admin/orders` access; legacy raw update/delete/add-item admin mutations are disabled; pay/ship and admin status-style actions require the matching `orders:*` action permission; and legacy order creation/checkout no longer lets broad admins create orders for other users.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:18 UTC static review found `WebMvcConfig` registers `AdminPermissionInterceptor` only for `/admin/**`, `/products/**`, `/brands/**`, and `/categories/**` at lines 15-22, so `/orders/**` never reaches `AdminRoleService.canAccess(...)`. `SecurityConfig` has explicit public guest/order-tracking exceptions but otherwise leaves `/orders/**` under `anyRequest().authenticated()` at lines 77-84 and 117-118. `UserDetailsImpl.build()` grants `ROLE_ADMIN` to any non-empty, non-`USER` role code at lines 52-57, and `SecurityUtils.assertAdmin()` checks only that broad role at lines 40-45. In `OrderController`, admin branches for `createOrder()`, `getUserOrders()`, `getOrder()`, `getAllOrders()`, and `getOrderItems()` return full `Order`/`OrderItem` entities based on `SecurityUtils.isAdmin(...)` at lines 39-51, 102-107, 110-115, 142-148, and 151-157 without checking the `orders` page permission. The legacy mutation paths `updateOrder()`, `deleteOrder()`, `addOrderItem()`, `payOrder()`, and `shipOrder()` call only `SecurityUtils.assertAdmin(authentication)` at lines 126-139, 169-176, and 222-240 before invoking `OrderService.updateOrder()`, `deleteOrder()`, `confirmPayment()`, `shipOrder()`, or `OrderItemService.addOrderItem()`; those service methods perform raw update/delete/payment/shipping/item writes at `OrderService` lines 665-674, 701-720, and 1054-1075 plus `OrderItemService` lines 60-64. The frontend still exports legacy helpers for update/delete/pay/ship/get-items/add-item at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1490-1538 even though the active admin order page has moved to `/admin/orders/**`. Existing F347 covers the newer `/admin/orders/**` mutation guards, F350 covers the side-effecting admin payment history GET, F351 covers OrderManagement UI action visibility, and F357 covers unbounded `/admin/orders`; F380 covers the remaining direct legacy `/orders/**` permission bypass.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:21 UTC static retest found the current source still matches the bypass: `WebMvcConfig` still omits `/orders/**` from the admin permission interceptor, `OrderController` still returns admin/full-entity branches from `SecurityUtils.isAdmin(...)` and still uses only `SecurityUtils.assertAdmin(...)` on raw update/delete/add-item/pay/ship paths, and `frontend/src/api/index.ts` still exports the legacy update/delete/pay/ship/get-items/add-item helpers. F380 remains OPEN.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:32 UTC static retest corrected the tracker status after re-reading current source. `src/main/java/com/example/shop/config/WebMvcConfig.java` lines 15-22 still registers `AdminPermissionInterceptor` for `/admin/**`, `/products/**`, `/brands/**`, and `/categories/**` only; `src/main/java/com/example/shop/controller/OrderController.java` lines 39-51, 102-115, and 142-157 still returns admin/full-entity responses through broad `SecurityUtils.isAdmin(...)`, while lines 126-139, 169-176, and 222-240 still guard update/delete/add-item/pay/ship with only `SecurityUtils.assertAdmin(...)`. `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1490-1538 still exports the legacy update/delete/pay/ship/get-items/add-item helpers. F380 remains OPEN; aggregate totals remain 530 issues, 396 FIXED, 4 WONTFIX, 130 OPEN because the confirmed 04:25 status transition was F378 to FIXED.
- Expected fix direction: Remove or narrow the admin behavior from customer `/orders/**` endpoints and make admin workflows use `/admin/orders/**` only, or add the same `AdminRoleService` page/action guards to every legacy admin branch before returning full entities or mutating order state. Deprecate or delete the exported legacy admin-capable `orderApi` helpers so future admin UI code cannot bypass the `/admin/orders/**` contract.

### F379: CSV product import preview bypasses the products import action permission

- Area: Backend / admin RBAC for product import preview; Frontend / ProductManagement import workflow consistency
- Environment: `POST /admin/products/import/preview`, ProductManagement CSV import, and custom catalog roles granted the `products` page permission without `products:import`
- Steps: Assign an admin role the `products` page permission but not `products:import`, sign in as that operator, then call `POST /admin/products/import/preview` directly with a `.csv` file. Compare this with the ProductManagement UI, the CSV apply endpoint, and URL import preview endpoint.
- Expected: The CSV import preview step should require the same explicit product-import authority as the visible import workflow and the apply step, or a separate explicit preview/import-preparation permission. Read-only product catalog roles should not be able to upload catalog import files into the server-side parser by calling the preview endpoint directly.
- Actual: `POST /admin/products/import/preview` inherits only the broad `products` page permission and calls the import parser without an action-permission guard. A products-read role can therefore upload and parse CSV import files, trigger import validation/category/product lookups, and create import-preview audit entries even though ProductManagement hides the import control and direct apply/URL import endpoints require `products:import`.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:01 UTC static review found `AdminRoleService` defines `PRODUCTS_IMPORT_PERMISSION = "products:import"` at line 41 and exposes it in `ADMIN_ACTION_PERMISSIONS` at line 91. `AdminController.previewImportProducts()` lines 1482-1496 accepts the multipart CSV, calls `productService.previewImportCsv(file)`, and records `PRODUCT_IMPORT_PREVIEW` without `requireAdminActionPermission(...)`. The adjacent apply endpoint `importProducts()` requires `PRODUCTS_IMPORT_PERMISSION` before parsing/writing at lines 1499-1505, and URL import preview also requires `PRODUCTS_IMPORT_PERMISSION` at lines 1534-1542. `ProductServiceImpl.processCsvImport()` parses and validates preview uploads at lines 863-985, including category lookup and existing product/variant checks at lines 878-880 and 939-954, so the direct preview endpoint still performs import workflow work. On the frontend, `ProductManagement.tsx` computes `canImportProducts` from `PRODUCTS_IMPORT_PERMISSION` at lines 553-556 and blocks the CSV import handler before calling preview at lines 1397-1413, while `adminApi.previewImportProducts()` still exposes the direct `POST /admin/products/import/preview` wrapper at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1976-1979. Existing F368 fixed product create/update/delete/status and CSV apply action authorization; F379 covers the remaining backend preview authorization bypass.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:19 UTC static retest confirmed `AdminController.previewImportProducts()` now calls `requireAdminActionPermission(authentication, AdminRoleService.PRODUCTS_IMPORT_PERMISSION, "PRODUCT_IMPORT_PREVIEW", ...)` before checking the multipart body or invoking `productService.previewImportCsv(file)`, so direct CSV preview requests require the same `products:import` action permission as CSV apply and URL import preview. Denied preview attempts now flow through the shared admin action guard and audit failure path. ProductManagement's existing `canImportProducts` frontend gate remains defense in depth.
- Resolution: CSV product import preview now requires the dedicated `products:import` backend action permission before parsing uploaded import files.
- Expected fix direction: Require `products:import` before `previewImportProducts()` parses the upload, or add a deliberately separate import-preview permission and expose it in role management. Record denied preview attempts consistently through the audit helper, and keep ProductManagement's existing `canImportProducts` UI gate as a defense-in-depth layer rather than the only preview restriction.

### F378: Alert acknowledgement, resolve, and self-check actions only require alerts page access

- Area: Backend / admin RBAC for alert operations; Frontend / AlertManagement action visibility
- Environment: `/admin/alerts`, `POST /admin/alerts/self-check`, `POST /admin/alerts/{id}/acknowledge`, `POST /admin/alerts/{id}/resolve`, `POST /admin/alerts/batch/acknowledge`, `POST /admin/alerts/batch/resolve`, and custom monitoring roles granted alert-center visibility without operational alert action authority
- Steps: Assign an admin role the `alerts` page permission but not `alerts:purge` or any operational alert action permission, sign in as that operator, open `/admin/alerts`, then run Self Check, acknowledge/resolve a row, or use the batch acknowledge/resolve controls; or call the same POST endpoints directly.
- Expected: Viewing the alert center should be separable from changing alert state or manually generating self-check alerts. Self-check, acknowledgement, resolve, and batch state changes should require explicit action permissions such as `alerts:self-check`, `alerts:acknowledge`, and `alerts:resolve`, and the UI should hide or block those controls for read-only alert roles.
- Actual: The admin permission model still exposes only `alerts:purge` as an alert action permission, so every non-purge alert mutation under `/admin/alerts/**` inherits the plain `alerts` page permission. A role that should only inspect alert history can run self-checks, acknowledge open alerts, resolve alerts, and batch-change alert state without any separate operational authority. AlertManagement also renders and invokes those controls without checking current permissions, while only the purge controls are permission-gated.
- Status: FIXED
