- Evidence: 2026-05-31 03:44 UTC static review found `AdminRoleService` defines only `ALERTS_PURGE_PERMISSION = "alerts:purge"` for alert actions at line 61, includes only that alert action in `ADMIN_ACTION_PERMISSIONS` at line 111, and maps `/alerts` to the page permission at line 141. `AdminAlertController.runSelfCheck()` lines 57-69, `acknowledge()` lines 71-87, `resolve()` lines 89-105, `acknowledgeBatch()` lines 107-123, and `resolveBatch()` lines 125-141 call `SystemAlertService` and audit the action without `requireAdminActionPermission(...)`; only `purgeResolved()` calls the `alerts:purge` guard at lines 143-149, with the shared guard at lines 162-168. The services mutate or create alert state through `SystemAlertService.acknowledge()`, `resolve()`, `acknowledgeBatch()`, and `resolveBatch()` at lines 129-146, while self-check can record new system alerts through the self-check flow. On the frontend, `AlertManagement.tsx` imports only `ALERTS_PURGE_PERMISSION` at line 9 and computes only `canPurgeResolved` at line 42; `runSelfCheck()`, `acknowledge()`, `resolve()`, `batchAcknowledge()`, and `batchResolve()` at lines 88-176 have no permission guards, and the self-check, row acknowledge/resolve, and batch buttons render at lines 247-256, 274-276, and 335-349 without action permission checks. The API wrappers expose the same POST calls at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1737-1746. Existing F362 covers resolved-alert purge and is fixed with `alerts:purge`; F191 covers a single-alert status-transition regression; F160/F192 cover alert list/filter display issues. F378 covers the remaining non-purge alert action authorization gap.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:21 UTC static retest found a partial fix only. `AdminRoleService` then defined and listed `alerts:self-check`, `alerts:acknowledge`, and `alerts:resolve`, but `AdminAlertController.runSelfCheck()`, single acknowledge/resolve, and batch acknowledge/resolve still did not call `requireAdminActionPermission(...)`; only purge used an action guard. `frontend/src/utils/roles.ts` still exported only `ALERTS_PURGE_PERMISSION`, and `AlertManagement.tsx` still computed only `canPurgeResolved`, with self-check, acknowledge, resolve, and batch controls plus handlers ungated. F378 remained OPEN at that checkpoint until the new permissions were enforced and surfaced in the UI.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:25 UTC static retest confirmed the alert action permission chain is now complete. `AdminRoleService` defines and exposes `alerts:self-check`, `alerts:acknowledge`, and `alerts:resolve` beside `alerts:purge`; `AdminAlertController` requires the matching permission before self-check, single acknowledge, single resolve, batch acknowledge, and batch resolve. `frontend/src/utils/roles.ts` exports and labels the new keys, and `AlertManagement.tsx` now computes separate action gates from `/admin/me/permissions`, blocks direct handlers without permission, hides self-check plus row/batch acknowledge/resolve controls when absent, and keeps resolved-alert purge scoped to `alerts:purge`.
- Resolution: Alert self-check, acknowledgement, and resolve operations now require dedicated backend action permissions with matching AlertManagement visibility and handler guards.
- Expected fix direction: Add explicit alert action permissions for self-check, acknowledge, and resolve or intentionally require super-admin for those operations. Enforce the permissions in `AdminAlertController` before single and batch mutations, expose the keys in role management, and gate AlertManagement handlers plus buttons from `/admin/me/permissions`. Keep alert list and summary reads on the `alerts` page permission and keep `alerts:purge` scoped to destructive retention cleanup.

### F377: Support message history endpoints return entire conversations without pagination

- Area: Backend / support message history APIs; Frontend / admin and customer support transcript rendering
- Environment: Long-running support conversations, `/support/sessions/{id}/messages`, `/support/guest/sessions/{id}/messages`, `/admin/support/sessions/{id}/messages`, CustomerSupportWidget, and SupportManagement
- Steps: Accumulate a long support thread with many customer/admin messages, then open it as a signed-in customer, guest order customer, or admin operator; leave the support widget/admin page open on that thread.
- Expected: Support transcripts should use a bounded page/cursor contract such as newest messages first with `limit`, `beforeId`/`beforeTime`, and `hasMore`, and clients should request older messages incrementally. Polling or backup refresh should request only changes since the last message, not the full transcript.
- Actual: Every support message-history endpoint returns the full message list for the session, ordered oldest-first, with no `limit`, `page`, cursor, or total/has-more metadata. The customer widget and admin page store and render the entire returned array; both customer and admin polling paths reload the full active transcript repeatedly. Large or high-churn support threads can therefore produce oversized responses, repeated full-table/session scans, and expensive client rendering during ordinary support use.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 06:23 UTC development pass bounded support transcript reads without changing the array response contract. Customer, guest, and admin message endpoints now accept `limit` and `afterId`, default to a capped recent-message window, and use incremental `id > afterId` queries for polling. `SupportService.sendMessage(...)` now reloads only the inserted message instead of scanning the whole session. CustomerSupportWidget and SupportManagement request the recent window on initial/session loads, poll only for newer messages through refs that avoid stale interval closures, and merge messages into a bounded local window.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 03:25 UTC static review found `SupportController.getMyMessages()` returns `toCustomerMessages(supportService.getMessages(sessionId))` at lines 67-74, `getGuestMessages()` returns the same full service result at lines 120-128, and `getSupportMessages()` returns `supportService.getMessages(sessionId)` at lines 171-174; none accept paging or cursor parameters. `SupportService.getMessages()` lines 99-100 delegates directly to `supportMessageMapper.findBySessionId(sessionId)`, and `SupportMessageMapper.xml` lines 16-22 selects all `support_messages` rows for the session ordered by `created_at ASC, id ASC` without `LIMIT` or cursor predicates. Frontend wrappers `supportApi.getMessages()`, `supportApi.getGuestMessages()`, and `adminSupportApi.getMessages()` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 2257, 2266-2268, and 2285 expose only `sessionId` plus guest credentials. `CustomerSupportWidget.tsx` loads and stores the full array for signed-in customers at lines 333-335, guests at lines 434-436, its polling loop reloads full messages at lines 451-476, and session switching reloads full messages at lines 730-734. `SupportManagement.tsx` loads the full admin transcript at lines 156-161, reloads the full active transcript in the 10-second polling fallback at lines 228-240, and renders `messages` directly as an Ant Design `List` at lines 512-555. Existing F358 covers the unbounded admin support session queue, F187 covers summary/list scope mismatch, F189 covers WebSocket filter leakage, and F375 covers support action authorization; F377 covers per-conversation message history boundedness.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 06:19 UTC static retest confirmed `SupportController` passes optional `limit`/`afterId` through all customer, guest, and admin message-history paths; `SupportService.getMessages(sessionId, limit, afterId)` clamps the window to 1-120 and uses `findRecentBySessionId(...)` or `findBySessionIdAfterId(...)`; `SupportMessageMapper.xml` contains bounded recent and after-id queries; `supportApi`, `adminSupportApi`, CustomerSupportWidget, and SupportManagement now request bounded windows and incremental polling. `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` completed successfully.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 06:21 UTC static/build retest found F377 remains OPEN, narrowed to the customer/guest widget polling path. Backend message reads now accept `limit` and `afterId`: `SupportController.getMyMessages()` lines 69-77, guest reads lines 124-134, and admin reads lines 177-181 pass those parameters into `SupportService.getMessages(...)`; `SupportService.getMessages(...)` lines 106-112 uses `findBySessionIdAfterId(...)` when `afterId` is present and `findRecentBySessionId(...)` otherwise; and `SupportMessageMapper.xml` lines 24-45 has bounded recent and after-id queries. Admin `SupportManagement.tsx` lines 193-195 maintains `messagesRef`, and its fallback poll uses `newestSupportMessageId(messagesRef.current)` at lines 295-306. However, `CustomerSupportWidget.tsx` still computes customer/guest polling `afterId` from the React state variable `messages` inside the interval at lines 468-483 while the effect dependencies at line 509 omit `messages`; the production build emits `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` for this dependency gap. The interval therefore keeps using the message array captured when the timer was installed, so customer/guest fallback polling can repeatedly request the initial/latest 80-message window or an obsolete cursor instead of reliably requesting only messages after the newest rendered message. `npm run build` as `guhao` succeeds but reports that warning; `./mvnw test` still fails at known testCompile errors before backend tests can exercise this path.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 06:23 UTC build retest confirmed F377 is now fixed. `CustomerSupportWidget.tsx` now mirrors `messages` into `messagesRef` and the polling interval computes `afterId` from `messagesRef.current`, matching the admin polling pattern and removing the stale closure. `npm run build` completed successfully with no React hook warnings after this change; `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` had already completed successfully for the backend changes.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 06:29 UTC manual retest reconfirmed F377 remains FIXED in the current worktree. `CustomerSupportWidget.tsx` lines 119 and 229-231 maintain `messagesRef`, and the polling interval uses `newestSupportMessageId(messagesRef.current)` at lines 473-488. `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` succeeded and `npm run build` as `guhao` succeeded with no React hook warnings.
- Expected fix direction: Add bounded support transcript APIs for customer, guest, and admin paths with a shared page/cursor response, cap page size, and support incremental refresh by newest id/timestamp. Update CustomerSupportWidget and SupportManagement to render a bounded current window, load older messages on demand, and poll only for deltas or rely on WebSocket events.

### F376: Support order detail links ignore the separate orders permission

- Area: Frontend / SupportManagement cross-page permission gating; Backend / admin order-detail permission contract
- Environment: `/admin/support`, support messages containing order context cards, `/admin/orders/{id}`, `/admin/orders/{id}/items`, and custom support roles granted `support` without the `orders` page permission
- Steps: Create or assign an admin role with `support` but not `orders`, sign in as that operator, open `/admin/support`, select a conversation with an order-context message, and click View Order.
- Expected: SupportManagement should either provide a support-scoped order context endpoint authorized by the `support` permission, or fetch current admin permissions and hide/disable the full order detail link unless the operator also has `orders`. A support-only queue role should not see a primary action that can only fail at the backend.
- Actual: The support page is visible from the `support` permission alone and renders View Order for any decoded order card, but the click calls `adminApi.getOrder()` and `adminApi.getOrderItems()`, which target `/admin/orders/{id}` and `/admin/orders/{id}/items`. Those routes are protected by the separate `orders` page permission, so support-only roles are invited into an order-detail modal that receives backend 403 failures instead of a permission-aware support workflow.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 03:20 UTC static review found `AdminLayout.tsx` builds the Support menu item from `canSee('support')` at lines 62-71, while `App.tsx` routes `/admin/support` to `SupportManagement` at lines 634-649. `AdminRoleService.PATH_PERMISSIONS` maps `/support` to `support` and `/orders` to `orders` at lines 107-126, and `AdminPermissionInterceptor.preHandle()` lines 24-35 enforces only `adminRoleService.canAccess(...)` for the request path. `AdminController.getOrder()` and `getOrderItems()` serve `/admin/orders/{id}` and `/admin/orders/{id}/items` at lines 1117-1128. In the frontend, `SupportManagement.tsx` imports no role/permission helper at lines 1-15, `openOrderDetail()` calls `adminApi.getOrder(orderId)` and `adminApi.getOrderItems(orderId)` at lines 321-334, and every decoded order card renders the View Order button at lines 528-541 without checking `orders`. The API helpers target `/admin/orders/${id}` and `/admin/orders/${id}/items` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1875 and 1903. Existing F126/F134 resolved earlier admin-page use of customer/general order APIs, F350 covers side effects in the admin payment history GET, F351 covers OrderManagement mutation controls, and F375 covers support conversation action authorization; F376 covers the remaining cross-permission SupportManagement order-detail workflow.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 03:41 UTC QA retest re-read the current source and confirmed `SupportManagement.tsx` now stores current role and permissions at lines 52-53, loads `/admin/me/permissions` at lines 76-93, computes `canViewOrders` from the separate `orders` permission at line 67, blocks direct order-detail attempts without that permission at lines 381-385, and renders the View Order link only when `canViewOrders` is true at lines 619-623. The backend permission mapping still keeps `/support` and `/orders` separate in `AdminRoleService.PATH_PERMISSIONS` at lines 130 and 144, so the support-only role no longer sees a full-order-detail action that can only fail with a backend 403.
- Resolution: SupportManagement now hides and blocks full order-detail links unless the operator also has the `orders` page permission.
- Expected fix direction: Decide whether support operators need a narrowed support-order context endpoint under `/admin/support/**` or full `orders` read permission. If full order detail still requires `orders`, load current admin permissions in SupportManagement, hide/disable View Order and the order-detail modal trigger without `orders`, and add regression coverage for a `support`-only role.

### F375: Support conversation actions only require support page access

- Area: Backend / admin RBAC for support conversation actions; Frontend / SupportManagement action visibility
- Environment: `/admin/support`, `/admin/support/sessions/{id}/messages`, `/admin/support/sessions/{id}/read`, `/admin/support/sessions/{id}/close`, `/admin/support/sessions/{id}/assign`, `/admin/support/sessions/{id}/reopen`, and custom roles that should inspect support queues without replying to or changing ownership/lifecycle state
- Steps: Assign an admin role only the `support` page permission, sign in as that operator, open a support conversation, send a reply, assign it to yourself, close or reopen it, or mark messages read by opening the thread or calling the read endpoint directly.
- Expected: Replying to customers, claiming conversations, closing/reopening sessions, and changing admin unread state should require dedicated support action permissions such as `support:reply`, `support:assign`, `support:close`, `support:reopen`, or `support:read-state`, separate from read-only queue visibility. The support UI should hide or disable those controls for roles that can only inspect queue state.
- Actual: Every admin support REST action is routed under `/admin/support/**`, so the interceptor authorizes it with only the `support` page permission. The handlers call support services that send admin messages, auto-assign sessions, close/reopen sessions, assign ownership, and mark messages read without an action-permission guard. SupportManagement renders and invokes the reply composer, assign, close, reopen, and read-marking flows without fetching current admin permissions. A support-read role can therefore participate in customer conversations and alter queue ownership/lifecycle/read state.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 03:14 UTC static review found `AdminRoleService` action permissions list order/product/catalog/user/coupon/notification/announcement/alert/IP/log-debug/traffic/config/pet-gallery keys at lines 28-63 and 68-104 but no `support:reply`, `support:assign`, `support:close`, `support:reopen`, or `support:read-state` keys; `/support` maps to the `support` page permission at line 126, and `CUSTOMER_SERVICE` is seeded with `support` at lines 170-171. `AdminPermissionInterceptor.preHandle()` lines 24-35 enforces only `adminRoleService.canAccess(...)`, which is the path/page permission check. In `SupportController`, `GET /admin/support/sessions/{sessionId}/messages` marks messages read at lines 171-174, `PUT /read` marks read at lines 177-180, `POST /messages` sends an admin reply at lines 183-201, `PUT /close` closes a session at lines 204-218, `PUT /assign` assigns ownership at lines 220-234, and `PUT /reopen` reopens/assigns at lines 236-249; none call `adminRoleService.hasPermission(...)` or a support action guard. `SupportService.sendAdminMessage()` auto-assigns and sends at lines 128-138, `closeSession()` mutates status at lines 242-251, `assignSession()` mutates ownership at lines 254-261, and `reopenSession()` mutates status/ownership at lines 264-274. On the frontend, `SupportManagement.tsx` imports no permission helpers at lines 1-15, sends via WebSocket or `adminSupportApi.sendMessage()` at lines 257-287, closes at lines 289-302, assigns at lines 361-373, reopens at lines 375-388, and renders assign/reopen, close, and reply controls at lines 497-509 and 599-618 without permission gates. `adminSupportApi` exposes the matching REST calls at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 2285-2291. Existing F355 covers the separate support birthday-coupon grant route, F190 covers unaudited WebSocket support actions, F358 covers unbounded support queue loading, and F189 covers WebSocket filter leakage; F375 covers missing least-privilege authorization for ordinary support conversation actions themselves.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 03:41 UTC QA retest found `AdminRoleService` now defines `support:reply`, `support:assign`, `support:close`, `support:reopen`, and `support:read-state` at lines 33-37 and exposes them in `ADMIN_ACTION_PERMISSIONS` at lines 83-87, while `frontend/src/utils/roles.ts` exports the same constants at lines 61-65 and labels them at lines 172-176. `SupportController` now requires the matching action permission before admin read-state, reply, close, assign, and reopen mutations at lines 182-184, 193-194, 215-216, 233-234, and 251-252, and the shared helper rejects missing permissions through `adminRoleService.hasPermission(...)` at lines 313-324. `SupportWebSocketHandler` also guards admin READ, CLOSE, and SEND paths with `support:read-state`, `support:close`, and `support:reply` at lines 101-124 and rejects missing action authority at lines 230-237. Frontend `SupportManagement.tsx` computes separate support action gates at lines 61-65, only marks messages read when `canUpdateSupportReadState` is true at lines 199-203, 248-249, and 284-287, blocks direct send/close/assign/reopen/quick-reply handlers at lines 308-310, 343-345, 427-430, 445-448, and 463-466, and renders assign/reopen, close, workflow quick replies, and the composer only when the matching permission is present at lines 573-588 and 639-704.
- Resolution: Admin support reply, assignment, close/reopen, and read-state mutations now require dedicated backend/WebSocket support action permissions and matching SupportManagement visibility plus handler guards.
- Expected fix direction: Add dedicated support action permissions, expose them in role management, enforce them before REST and WebSocket support reply/assign/close/reopen/read-state mutations, decide intentionally which default roles receive them, and gate SupportManagement handlers and controls from current admin permissions. Keep read-only support queue access on the `support` page permission.

### F374: Bulk admin exports and log downloads only require page permissions

- Area: Backend / admin RBAC for bulk exports and runtime log download; Frontend / export-download action visibility
- Environment: `/admin/users/export`, `/admin/orders/export`, `/admin/audit-logs/export`, `/admin/logs/download`, UserManagement, OrderManagement, SecurityAuditLogManagement, LogManagement, and custom roles that should browse operational pages without bulk data extraction authority
- Steps: Assign an admin role the `users`, `orders`, `audit-logs`, or `logs` page permission without any dedicated export/download authority, then use the corresponding Export/Download button or call the matching endpoint directly.
- Expected: Bulk extraction of user PII, order/customer fulfillment data, security-audit evidence, or runtime logs should require a dedicated export/download action permission or super-admin approval separate from page browsing. The UI should hide or disable export/download buttons unless the operator has that explicit authority.
- Actual: The backend has no export/download action permissions and authorizes these routes only through the page-level path mapping. Page-visible roles can therefore bulk export users with email/phone/role/status, orders with addresses/payment/tracking/return/items data, audit logs with actor/IP/user-agent/metadata fields, or runtime log files without separate export authority. The frontend renders the matching buttons without checking any export/download permission.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 03:01 UTC static review found `AdminRoleService` action permissions include order/product/catalog/user/coupon/notification/announcement/alert/IP/log-debug/traffic/config/pet-gallery actions at lines 28-63 but no `users:export`, `orders:export`, `audit-logs:export`, or `logs:download` keys. `AdminController.exportUsers()` lines 811-846 writes CSV fields `id`, `username`, `email`, `phone`, `role`, `roleCode`, `status`, and `createdAt` without an action guard; `exportOrders()` lines 1753-1811 writes order id/no, `userId`, amount, status, shipping address, payment method, tracking/return/refund fields, and item summaries without an action guard; and `exportAuditLogs()` lines 1865-1912 writes audit id/time/action/result/actor/resource/IP/user-agent/message/metadata without an action guard. `AdminLogManagementController.download()` lines 108-131 returns runtime log bytes after only parsing filters, while the controller's only action guard is for `LOGS_DEBUG_PERMISSION` at lines 49-80. On the frontend, `UserManagement.tsx` calls `adminApi.exportUsers()` at lines 167-181 and renders Export at line 448; `OrderManagement.tsx` calls `adminApi.exportOrders()` at lines 516-537 and renders Export at lines 904-906; `SecurityAuditLogManagement.tsx` calls `adminApi.exportAuditLogs()` at lines 1246-1260 and renders Export at lines 1510-1511; and `LogManagement.tsx` calls `adminApi.downloadLogs()` at lines 84-112 and renders Download Logs at lines 126-127 without export/download permission checks. Existing F356 covers unbounded user-list/export size, F357 covers the legacy unbounded order-list endpoint, F361 covers destructive audit-log purge, F366 covers runtime debug-level mutation, and F351 covers order mutation controls; F374 covers missing least-privilege authorization for bulk extraction itself.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 03:28 UTC development retest found `AdminRoleService` now defines and exposes `orders:export`, `users:export`, `logs:download`, and `audit-logs:export` through the shared action-permission list; `AdminController.exportUsers()`, `exportOrders()`, and `exportAuditLogs()` require those dedicated permissions before generating CSV payloads; and `AdminLogManagementController.preview()` plus `download()` require `logs:download` before returning runtime log content. Frontend `UserManagement.tsx`, `OrderManagement.tsx`, `SecurityAuditLogManagement.tsx`, and `LogManagement.tsx` now fetch `/admin/me/permissions`, hide the matching Export/Download buttons without the dedicated permission, and keep handler-level `adminLayout.noPermission` guards as a direct-call fallback.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 03:28 UTC QA retest re-read the current source and confirmed `AdminRoleService` exposes `orders:export`, `users:export`, `logs:download`, and `audit-logs:export` at lines 32, 51, 66, and 67 and includes them in `ADMIN_ACTION_PERMISSIONS` at lines 82, 101, 116, and 117. `AdminController.exportUsers()` requires `USERS_EXPORT_PERMISSION` at lines 811-823, `exportOrders()` requires `ORDER_EXPORT_PERMISSION` at lines 1756-1768, and `exportAuditLogs()` requires `AUDIT_LOGS_EXPORT_PERMISSION` at lines 1871-1883. `AdminLogManagementController.preview()` and `download()` require `LOGS_DOWNLOAD_PERMISSION` at lines 82-96 and 109-123. Frontend `roles.ts` exposes the same permission constants at lines 31-37 and 51 and labels them at lines 155-161 and 175; `UserManagement.tsx` computes `canExportUsers` at lines 54-58, blocks direct export attempts at lines 169-176, and renders Export only when permitted at lines 453-456; `OrderManagement.tsx` computes `canExportOrders` at lines 93-99, blocks direct export attempts at lines 538-545, and renders the order export button only when permitted at lines 930-934; `SecurityAuditLogManagement.tsx` computes `canExportAuditLogs` at lines 1040-1052, blocks direct export at lines 1268-1275, and renders the audit export button only when permitted at lines 1536-1540; `LogManagement.tsx` computes `canDownloadLogs` at lines 27-30, blocks direct download at lines 85-97, and renders Download Logs only when permitted at lines 131-135.
- Resolution: Bulk user export, order export, audit-log export, and runtime log preview/download now require dedicated backend action permissions and matching frontend action visibility gates.
- Expected fix direction: Add explicit export/download action permissions such as `users:export`, `orders:export`, `audit-logs:export`, and `logs:download`, expose them in role management, enforce them before generating CSV/log payloads, and gate each frontend export/download button and handler from current admin permissions. Keep existing caps/streaming work separate from the authority check.

### F373: UserManagement row actions do not honor users action permissions

- Area: Frontend / UserManagement action visibility; Backend / admin user-action permission integration
- Environment: `/admin/users`, `users:write`, `users:status`, `users:delete`, and custom roles that should inspect accounts without changing or removing customer identities
- Steps: Assign an admin role only the `users` page permission, sign in as that operator, open UserManagement, and inspect the ordinary-customer edit, ban/unban, and delete controls.
- Expected: UserManagement should fetch current admin permissions and hide or disable customer profile edit, status toggle, and delete controls unless the operator has the matching `users:write`, `users:status`, or `users:delete` action permission. Read-only user-list roles should not see actionable controls that only fail at the backend.
- Actual: The backend now defines and enforces `users:write`, `users:status`, and `users:delete`, and the frontend permission helper now exposes those permission keys, but UserManagement still renders edit, ban/unban, and delete controls without checking current admin permissions. A role with only `users` visibility therefore still sees mutation controls for ordinary customer rows and hits backend 403s instead of a permission-aware UI.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 02:39 UTC static review found `AdminRoleService` now defines `USERS_WRITE_PERMISSION`, `USERS_STATUS_PERMISSION`, and `USERS_DELETE_PERMISSION` at lines 42-44 and includes them in `ADMIN_ACTION_PERMISSIONS` at lines 83-85. `AdminController.updateUser()` requires `USERS_STATUS_PERMISSION` before status changes at lines 982-985 and `USERS_WRITE_PERMISSION` before ordinary profile/contact changes at lines 1022-1026, while `deleteUser()` requires `USERS_DELETE_PERMISSION` at lines 1068-1070. Frontend `roles.ts` now exports `USERS_WRITE_PERMISSION`, `USERS_STATUS_PERMISSION`, and `USERS_DELETE_PERMISSION` at lines 45-47 and includes them in `ADMIN_ACTION_PERMISSIONS` at lines 83-85. However, `UserManagement.tsx` still imports role helpers but no permission helper at lines 1-10, sets only `canManageRoles` from super-admin role at line 43, calls `adminApi.updateUser()` for status at lines 161-175 and profile edits at lines 187-206, calls `adminApi.deleteUser()` at lines 214-222, and renders edit/status/delete controls at lines 318-358 with only self, guest, and privileged-target disabling. Existing F207 covers validation/normalization bypass in contact edits, F209-F211 cover self/privileged-account safeguards, F215/F338 cover guest status handling, and F356 covers unbounded user listing; F373 covers the remaining frontend action-permission gating gap for the newly guarded user mutations.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 02:53 UTC retest found `UserManagement.tsx` now imports `USERS_WRITE_PERMISSION`, `USERS_STATUS_PERMISSION`, `USERS_DELETE_PERMISSION`, and `hasAdminPermission` at lines 7-16; stores current admin permissions at lines 48-56; loads `/admin/me/permissions` at lines 130-139; blocks unauthorized status changes at lines 185-194, profile edits at lines 205-224, and deletes at lines 250-254; and disables row edit/status/delete controls from the matching permission booleans at lines 368-397. Backend `AdminController.updateUser()` and `deleteUser()` still require the matching `users:*` action permissions at lines 982-985, 1022-1026, and 1068-1070.
- Resolution: User profile/contact edits, status changes, and deletes now have both backend `users:*` action-permission enforcement and UserManagement frontend permission gating.
- Expected fix direction: Load `/admin/me/permissions` in UserManagement, gate the visible profile/status/delete controls and handlers by `users:write`, `users:status`, and `users:delete`, and add frontend regression coverage proving a `users` read-only role can list/search accounts without seeing mutation controls.

### F372: Pet-gallery admin delete only requires pet-gallery page access

- Environment: `/admin/pet-gallery`, `DELETE /admin/pet-gallery/{id}`, and custom roles that should inspect community gallery uploads without deleting user content

The UI should hide or disable remove controls for roles that can only inspect gallery state.

PetGalleryManagement renders the remove button for every listed row without checking a dedicated delete permission, so any role granted pet-gallery visibility can delete community gallery content.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 02:24 UTC static review found `AdminRoleService` action permissions cover only order, alert purge, traffic-control, and config-center operations at lines 28-50, while `PATH_PERMISSIONS` maps `/pet-gallery` to `pet-gallery` at line 73 and `canAccess()` lines 178-183 authorizes by that path permission via `permissionForPath()` lines 219-231. `AdminController.getGalleryPhotos()` lines 2355-2357 is the read path, but `deleteGalleryPhoto()` lines 2360-2374 calls `petGalleryService.adminDeletePhoto(id)` and records `PET_GALLERY_PHOTO_DELETE` without an action-permission guard. Frontend `PetGalleryManagement.tsx` imports no role/permission helper at lines 1-10, calls `adminApi.deletePetGalleryPhoto()` in `handleDelete()` at lines 110-121, and renders the row remove `Popconfirm` and danger button at lines 197-215 without permission gating. `adminApi.deletePetGalleryPhoto()` calls `DELETE /admin/pet-gallery/{id}` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` line 1777. Existing F154 fixed the missing `pet-gallery` page mapping, and F359 covers the unbounded admin list; F372 covers destructive delete authorization within the now-mapped page.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 02:51 UTC retest found `AdminRoleService` now defines and exposes `pet-gallery:delete` through the shared action-permission list, while `AdminController.deleteGalleryPhoto()` requires `PET_GALLERY_DELETE_PERMISSION` before calling `petGalleryService.adminDeletePhoto(id)` at lines 2441-2447. Frontend `PetGalleryManagement.tsx` imports `PET_GALLERY_DELETE_PERMISSION` and `hasAdminPermission` at line 10, stores current role and permissions at lines 54-56, loads `/admin/me/permissions` at lines 84-94, blocks unauthorized delete attempts in `handleDelete()` at lines 126-130, and only appends the delete action column and remove `Popconfirm` when `canDeletePhotos` is true at lines 220-239.
- Resolution: Pet-gallery admin delete now requires the dedicated `pet-gallery:delete` backend action permission, and PetGalleryManagement hides and blocks remove controls for roles without that permission.
- Expected fix direction: Add a dedicated pet-gallery delete/moderation action permission, expose it in role management, enforce it before `adminDeletePhoto(...)`, gate PetGalleryManagement remove controls from current admin permissions, and add regression coverage proving a `pet-gallery` read-only role can list photos but cannot delete them.

On the frontend, `adminApi.publishConfigCenter()` and `adminApi.applyConfigCenter()` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1717-1722 post to those endpoints; `ConfigCenter.tsx` imports no permission helper at lines 1-8, invokes publish/apply at lines 59-113, and renders Apply Only plus Publish & Sync buttons behind only `Popconfirm` wrappers at lines 126-151. - Evidence: 2026-05-31 02:12 UTC retest found `AdminRoleService` now defines `config-center:apply` and `config-center:publish` at lines 35-36 and includes them in `ADMIN_ACTION_PERMISSIONS` at lines 49-50. `AdminConfigCenterController.publish()` lines 56-83 requires `CONFIG_CENTER_PUBLISH_PERMISSION`, and also requires `CONFIG_CENTER_APPLY_PERMISSION` when `applyRuntime` is true; `apply()` lines 85-109 requires `CONFIG_CENTER_APPLY_PERMISSION`; the shared guard at lines 111-117 throws 403 when missing. Frontend `ConfigCenter.tsx` imports the two permission constants and role helpers at lines 8-13, loads `/admin/me/permissions` at lines 72-75, guards handlers at lines 87-100 and 122-136, and renders Apply/Publish buttons only behind the matching permissions at lines 185-191.
- Resolution: Backend publish/apply routes now enforce dedicated config-center action permissions, and ConfigCenter gates the mutation controls from current admin permissions.
- Expected fix direction: Add explicit config-center action permissions for runtime apply and Nacos publish, or require super-admin for both write endpoints; expose those permissions in role management, gate ConfigCenter buttons from current admin permissions, and add backend regression coverage proving a `config-center` read-only role can fetch snapshots/health but cannot apply or publish configuration.

### F363: Traffic-control reset and clear actions only require page access

- Area: Backend / admin RBAC for traffic-control operations; Frontend / TrafficControl action visibility
- Environment: `/admin/traffic-control`, `POST /admin/traffic-control/rate-limit/clear`, `POST /admin/traffic-control/circuit-breakers/reset`, and custom roles that should monitor traffic controls without disabling protections
- Steps: Assign an admin role the `traffic-control` page permission, sign in as that operator, open `/admin/traffic-control`, and confirm either Clear Rate Limit or Reset All Circuits; or call the matching POST endpoints directly.
- Expected: Clearing rate-limit counters and resetting circuit breakers should require a dedicated operational action permission such as `traffic-control:manage` or super-admin authority, separate from read-only traffic-control status access. The UI should hide or disable those actions for roles that can only inspect traffic health.
- Actual: The admin permission model maps every `/admin/traffic-control/**` route to the plain `traffic-control` page permission, and the clear/reset endpoints mutate protection state without an action-permission guard or super-admin check. A role granted traffic-control visibility can therefore clear abuse-throttling counters or reset all circuit state, temporarily weakening protection and masking downstream failure signals.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 01:47 UTC static review found `AdminRoleService` action permissions still include only the four order actions at lines 28-40, while `PATH_PERMISSIONS` maps `/traffic-control` to `traffic-control` at line 66 and `canAccess()` lines 168-173 authorizes by that path permission. `AdminTrafficControlController.status()` lines 32-38 is the read path, but `clearRateLimit()` lines 41-53 calls `rateLimitService.clear()` and `resetCircuit()` lines 55-71 calls `circuitBreakerService.reset(name)` with no action-permission or super-admin guard. `RateLimitService.clear()` lines 90-94 clears all rate-limit buckets and accepted/rejected counters, while `CircuitBreakerService.reset()` lines 54-60 clears all circuit state when no name is supplied; circuit open/failure state is what blocks repeated downstream failures at lines 137-147. On the frontend, `adminApi.clearRateLimitCounters()` and `adminApi.resetCircuitBreaker()` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1731-1733 post to those endpoints; `TrafficControl.tsx` imports no permission helper at lines 1-9, invokes reset/clear at lines 44-68, and renders per-circuit reset plus global clear/reset buttons behind only `Popconfirm` wrappers at lines 113-168. Existing F303 covered missing confirmation dialogs and is now fixed; F363 covers backend authorization and frontend permission gating for the same high-impact operations.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 02:12 UTC retest found `AdminRoleService` now defines `traffic-control:rate-limit-clear` and `traffic-control:circuit-reset` at lines 33-34 and includes them in `ADMIN_ACTION_PERMISSIONS` at lines 47-48. `AdminTrafficControlController.clearRateLimit()` lines 49-62 requires `TRAFFIC_CONTROL_RATE_LIMIT_CLEAR_PERMISSION`; `resetCircuit()` lines 64-81 requires `TRAFFIC_CONTROL_CIRCUIT_RESET_PERMISSION`; the shared guard at lines 83-89 throws 403 when missing. Frontend `TrafficControl.tsx` imports both permission constants and role helpers at lines 9-14, loads `/admin/me/permissions` at lines 54-70, guards mutation handlers at lines 72-104, conditionally adds the row reset column at lines 147-170, and renders global clear/reset controls only when the matching permission is present at lines 190-215.
- Resolution: Backend traffic-control mutation routes now enforce dedicated action permissions, and TrafficControl gates clear/reset controls from current admin permissions.
- Expected fix direction: Add explicit traffic-control action permissions for clearing rate limits and resetting circuit breakers, or require super-admin for those operations; expose the permissions in role management, gate the TrafficControl buttons from current admin permissions, and add backend regression coverage proving a `traffic-control` read-only role can fetch status but cannot mutate protection state.

### F362: Resolved-alert purge only requires the alerts page permission

- Area: Backend / admin RBAC for system-alert retention; Frontend / AlertManagement purge action visibility
- Environment: `/admin/alerts`, `POST /admin/alerts/purge-resolved`, and custom monitoring or support roles granted alert-center access without authority to delete operational alert history
- Steps: Assign an admin role the `alerts` page permission, sign in as that operator, open the alert center, set a retention window, and confirm the purge action; or call `POST /admin/alerts/purge-resolved?retentionDays=1` directly.
- Expected: Deleting resolved alert history should require super-admin authority or a dedicated destructive action permission such as `alerts:purge`, separate from alert viewing and ordinary acknowledgement/resolve workflows. The alert-center UI should hide or disable purge controls for roles that lack that authority.
- Actual: The admin permission model maps every `/admin/alerts/**` route to the plain `alerts` page permission, and the resolved-alert purge handler performs a delete without an action-permission guard or super-admin check. Any role that can open the alert center can therefore remove resolved operational/security alert history by retention cutoff.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 01:45 UTC static review found `AdminRoleService` action permissions still include only order actions at lines 28-40, while `PATH_PERMISSIONS` maps `/alerts` to `alerts` at line 59 and `canAccess()` lines 168-173 authorizes by that single path permission. The frontend role permission list likewise exposes `alerts` but no `alerts:purge` action permission at `frontend/src/utils/roles.ts` lines 25-53. `AdminAlertController.purgeResolved()` lines 136-152 handles `POST /admin/alerts/purge-resolved`, calls `systemAlertService.purgeResolved(retentionDays)`, and records `ALERT_PURGE_RESOLVED` success/failure without an action-permission or super-admin check. `SystemAlertService.purgeResolved()` lines 153-159 executes `DELETE FROM system_alerts WHERE status = ? AND resolved_at IS NOT NULL AND resolved_at < ?`. On the frontend, `adminApi.purgeResolvedAlerts()` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1750-1753 posts to that endpoint, `AlertManagement.tsx` lines 156-167 invokes it, and lines 325-343 render the retention input and purge button with only a `Popconfirm`; imports at lines 1-9 include no permission helper. This is distinct from F160 alert count/list mismatch, F191 single acknowledge state regression, and F192 alert search default filter narrowing; F362 covers destructive alert-history purge authorization.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 02:12 UTC retest found `AdminRoleService` now defines `alerts:purge` at line 32 and includes it in `ADMIN_ACTION_PERMISSIONS` at line 46. `AdminAlertController.purgeResolved()` lines 143-160 now calls `requireAdminActionPermission(authentication, AdminRoleService.ALERTS_PURGE_PERMISSION)` before `systemAlertService.purgeResolved(...)`, and the shared guard at lines 162-168 throws 403 when the permission is missing. Frontend `AlertManagement.tsx` imports `ALERTS_PURGE_PERMISSION`, `getEffectiveRole`, and `hasAdminPermission` at line 9, loads `/admin/me/permissions` into state at lines 39-42 and 72-75, and renders the purge controls only inside `canPurgeResolved` at lines 351-372.
- Resolution: Resolved-alert purge now requires the dedicated `alerts:purge` action permission, and AlertManagement hides the purge controls from roles without that permission.
- Expected fix direction: Add an explicit `alerts:purge` permission or require super-admin for `/admin/alerts/purge-resolved`, expose the permission in role management, gate the AlertManagement purge UI from current admin permissions, and add regression coverage proving an alerts-read/operator role cannot purge resolved alert rows.

### F361: Audit-log purge only requires the audit-log page permission

- Area: Backend / admin RBAC for security audit retention; Frontend / SecurityAuditLogManagement purge action visibility
- Environment: `/admin/audit-logs`, `POST /admin/audit-logs/purge`, and custom admin roles that should be allowed to inspect audit evidence but not delete it

- Actual: The admin permission model maps every `/admin/audit-logs/**` route to the plain `audit-logs` page permission, and the purge handler does not call an action-permission guard or super-admin check before deleting rows. Any role that can view audit logs can therefore permanently remove older security-audit evidence through the retention purge endpoint.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 06:08 UTC development pass added the dedicated `audit-logs:purge` action permission, included it in backend and frontend role-permission allowlists, required it before `AdminController.purgeAuditLogs()` deletes audit rows, and gated `SecurityAuditLogManagement` purge controls and handler with current admin permissions.

On the frontend, `adminApi.purgeAuditLogs()` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1890-1892 posts to `/admin/audit-logs/purge`; `SecurityAuditLogManagement.tsx` lines 1264-1274 invokes it from the page, and lines 1358-1379 render the retention input plus purge button with only a `Popconfirm`, while imports at lines 1-9 include no permission helper. `frontend/src/api/index.test.ts` lines 790-794 only asserts retention-day clamping for the helper. This is distinct from F183 audit summary filter mismatch, F190 unaudited support WebSocket actions, F193 dashboard refunding audit drilldown, and F204 missing audit entries for IP-blacklist login failures; F361 covers destructive audit-log purge authorization.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:21 UTC static retest confirmed F361 remains OPEN. `AdminRoleService` still exposes only `audit-logs:export` for audit-log actions, `AdminController.purgeAuditLogs()` still calls `auditLogService.purge(retentionDays)` without `requireAdminActionPermission(...)` or a super-admin guard, and `SecurityAuditLogManagement.tsx` still renders and invokes purge while importing only `AUDIT_LOGS_EXPORT_PERMISSION` for export gating.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 06:08 UTC static retest confirmed `AdminRoleService` and `frontend/src/utils/roles.ts` now expose `audit-logs:purge`, `AdminController.purgeAuditLogs()` calls `requireAdminActionPermission(...)` before `auditLogService.purge(...)`, role labels include the new permission in English, Chinese, and Spanish, and `SecurityAuditLogManagement.tsx` hides the retention purge control plus blocks the handler when the permission is missing. `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` completed successfully.
- Expected fix direction: Add an explicit `audit-logs:purge` action permission or require super-admin for purge, expose that permission in role management, gate the SecurityAuditLogManagement purge UI from current admin permissions, and add backend regression coverage proving a role with only `audit-logs` read access cannot call the purge endpoint or delete audit rows.

### F360: Admin announcement management loads the full announcement table before local filtering

- Area: Backend / admin announcement list API; Frontend / AnnouncementManagement filtering and table pagination
- Environment: `/admin/announcements`, AnnouncementManagement, stores with many historical, scheduled, expired, active, inactive, or linked announcements
- Steps: Accumulate many site announcements, then open `/admin/announcements` and use the status filter, keyword search, or table pagination.
- Expected: Admin announcement management should use a bounded server-side list contract with page/size/total metadata and server-side status/keyword filters. The table should request only the current result page, while export or archival workflows should be explicit and capped.
- Actual: `GET /admin/announcements` returns the entire announcement table as a raw list. The frontend loads all rows into `announcements`, computes status/search filters and fallback summary counts locally, and paginates only the already-loaded array. Large announcement histories can therefore turn a normal management page load into an unbounded database read, large JSON response, and oversized client state.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 09:02 UTC development pass replaced the raw admin announcement list with a bounded page contract. `/admin/announcements` now accepts page, size, status, and keyword parameters, returns `items`, `total`, `page`, `size`, `totalPages`, `hasNext`, and `hasPrevious`, and `AnnouncementManagement` now drives table data/pagination from the server page instead of filtering a full local list.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 01:37 UTC static review found `SiteAnnouncementController.getAll()` lines 37-40 handles `GET /admin/announcements` and returns `announcementService.findAll()` with no `page`, `size`, `status`, or keyword parameters. `SiteAnnouncementService.findAll()` lines 31-34 delegates to `repository.findAllByOrderBySortOrderAscIdDesc()`, while the same service uses bounded `PageRequest` only for the public active-announcement banner at lines 36-42. On the frontend, `AnnouncementManagement.tsx` lines 44-47 calls `adminApi.getAnnouncements()` and stores the complete response in `announcements`; lines 72-85 compute fallback summary counts from the full local array; lines 99-113 apply status and keyword filters locally; and `adminApi.getAnnouncements()` at `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 2043-2044 calls `/admin/announcements` without filters or pagination. This is distinct from F213, which covers global summary cards disagreeing with the locally filtered table; F360 covers the unbounded list contract and client-side filtering/pagination itself.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 06:58 UTC static retest confirmed F360 remains OPEN. `SiteAnnouncementController.getAll()` lines 43-46 still returns `announcementService.findAll()` with no paging or filters; `SiteAnnouncementService.findAll()` lines 31-33 still delegates to `repository.findAllByOrderBySortOrderAscIdDesc()`; `adminApi.getAnnouncements()` still calls `/admin/announcements` without params; and `AnnouncementManagement.tsx` lines 47-51 still stores the full response before local summary, status, keyword, and table filtering.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 08:10 UTC tracker/source correction confirmed the prior 07:04 `GET /admin/roles` fix evidence was misplaced and does not apply to F360. Current source still has `SiteAnnouncementController.getAll()` returning `announcementService.findAll()` at lines 43-46, `SiteAnnouncementService.findAll()` calling `repository.findAllByOrderBySortOrderAscIdDesc()` at lines 31-33, `adminApi.getAnnouncements()` calling `/admin/announcements` with no params at lines 2144-2145, and `AnnouncementManagement.tsx` loading the raw list into `announcements` before local summary/filter/table pagination at lines 47-51, 89-130, and 288-290. F360 remains OPEN, so the summary counts were corrected to 425 FIXED and 119 OPEN.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 08:17 UTC tracker correction confirmed the concurrent review/question moderation fix evidence belonged to F354, not F360. The announcement path is still unbounded in current source, so F360 remains OPEN.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 08:34 UTC tracker/source correction confirmed the 08:32 payment-history fix evidence belonged to F350, not F360. Current source still has `SiteAnnouncementController.getAll()` returning `announcementService.findAll()` at lines 43-46, `SiteAnnouncementService.findAll()` calling `repository.findAllByOrderBySortOrderAscIdDesc()` at lines 31-33, and `AnnouncementManagement.tsx` loading the raw response into local `announcements`, filtering into `filteredAnnouncements`, and rendering `dataSource={filteredAnnouncements}` at lines 47-51, 116-130, and 288-290. F360 remains OPEN.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 09:02 UTC static retest corrected the stale 08:54 open note after current source changed. `SiteAnnouncementController.getAll()` now returns `SiteAnnouncementAdminPageResponse` and binds `page`, `size`, `status`, and `keyword` at lines 44-50. `SiteAnnouncementService.findAdminPage()` clamps page/size, normalizes filters, and queries `repository.searchAdmin(...)` with a `PageRequest` at lines 42-59. `SiteAnnouncementRepository.searchAdmin(...)` returns `Page<SiteAnnouncement>` and applies status/keyword predicates at lines 14-23. `adminApi.getAnnouncements()` now sends normalized page/filter params and normalizes the page response at lines 2179-2187. `AnnouncementManagement.tsx` calls that API with page/size/status/keyword and stores only `response.data.items` at lines 58-82, reloads page 1 on debounced keyword/status changes at lines 103-110, and renders `dataSource={announcements}` with server `total`/page-size/onChange pagination at lines 297-309. F360 is FIXED.
