### F350: Admin order payment history GET can sync or expire payments without payment-action permission

- Area: Backend / admin order payment read API and order action permissions; Frontend / OrderManagement payment history and refund evidence loading
- Environment: Admin `/admin/orders/{id}/payments` opened from OrderManagement item detail or refund modal by a role with `orders` but no `orders:payment` permission, especially when the order has a pending Stripe payment whose provider session is paid/expired or whose local expiry time has elapsed
- Steps: Assign an operator a role with only the `orders` page permission and no `orders:payment` action permission, open `/admin/orders`, then open item detail or refund evidence for an order with a pending Stripe payment that has become paid/expired at the provider or expired locally.
- Expected: Admin order payment history GET should be side-effect-free and safe for order read/support roles. Provider sync, payment expiry, and order state transitions should require `orders:payment`, an explicit sync/action endpoint, callback/webhook handling, or a scheduled job with audit coverage.
- Actual: `GET /admin/orders/{id}/payments` does not call the new action-permission guard and is authorized only by the page-level `orders` permission. It delegates to `PaymentService.findByOrderId()`, which performs provider sync and expiry while reading. An order-read/support operator without `orders:payment` can therefore mark a pending Stripe payment `PAID` and move the order to `PENDING_SHIPMENT`, or mark a payment `EXPIRED` and cancel the order when no active pending payments remain, simply by opening the order detail/refund modal.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 08:32 UTC development pass split admin payment-history reads from payment state mutation. `GET /admin/orders/{id}/payments` now uses the new read-only `PaymentService.findStoredByOrderId(...)`, while provider sync and expiry remain on explicit mutation paths such as `POST /admin/orders/payments/{paymentId}/sync`, scheduled expiry, callbacks, or other audited service operations.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 00:44 UTC static review found `AdminRoleService` lines 28-40 define `orders:payment` as an action permission and lines 104-107 seed `CUSTOMER_SERVICE` with only page permissions including `orders`. `AdminController.getOrderPayments()` lines 1071-1076 is a GET handler with no `requireAdminActionPermission(...)` call and returns `paymentService.findByOrderId(id)`, while explicit `syncOrderPayment()` lines 1079-1084 requires `ORDER_PAYMENT_PERMISSION`. `AdminPermissionInterceptor` lines 24-35 calls `adminRoleService.canAccess(...)`, and `AdminRoleService` lines 43-49 plus 168-173 maps `/admin/orders/**` to the single page permission `orders`. `PaymentService.findByOrderId()` lines 276-294 calls `syncProviderPaymentState(...)` and `expirePayment(...)`; `expirePayment()` lines 381-385 can cancel the order, and `syncProviderPaymentState()` lines 764-796 can mark the payment paid, update the order to `PENDING_SHIPMENT`, or expire the payment. Frontend `OrderManagement.tsx` lines 371-379 loads this endpoint when opening order detail and lines 396-403 loads it before refund evidence. This is distinct from F341, which tracks customer/guest payment GET endpoints mutating state, and from F347, whose explicit admin order mutation APIs now have server-side action-permission guards.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 07:45 UTC static retest confirmed F350 remains OPEN. `AdminController.getOrderPayments()` lines 1144-1149 still accepts only the order id, performs no `Authentication` or `requireAdminActionPermission(...)` check, and returns `paymentService.findByOrderId(id)`. The explicit admin sync route immediately below remains guarded with `ORDER_PAYMENT_PERMISSION` at lines 1152-1157, so the GET path is still the bypass. `PaymentService.findByOrderId()` lines 276-294 still calls `syncProviderPaymentState(...)` and `expirePayment(...)` while reading; `expirePayment()` lines 381-385 can cancel the order, and `syncProviderPaymentState(...)` lines 764-797 can call Stripe, mark the payment paid, move the order to `PENDING_SHIPMENT`, or expire the payment. `OrderManagement.tsx` still calls the GET payment-history endpoint when opening details at lines 398-400 and refund evidence at lines 425-426.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 08:10 UTC static retest confirmed F350 remains OPEN. `AdminController.getOrderPayments()` still accepts only `@PathVariable Long id`, performs no `Authentication` or action-permission check, and returns `paymentService.findByOrderId(id)` at lines 1157-1162. The explicit sync route below still requires `ORDER_PAYMENT_PERMISSION` at lines 1165-1169, leaving the GET path as the bypass. `PaymentService.findByOrderId()` remains transactional and still calls `syncProviderPaymentState(payment)` and `expirePayment(payment)` during reads at lines 277-294; `expirePayment()` can cancel the order at lines 381-386, and `syncProviderPaymentState(...)` can retrieve a Stripe session, mark the payment paid, move the order to `PENDING_SHIPMENT`, or expire it at lines 764-797. `OrderManagement.tsx` still calls this GET path when opening order details and refund evidence at lines 400 and 425.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 08:32 UTC static/build retest confirmed `AdminController.getOrderPayments()` now verifies the order exists and returns `paymentService.findStoredByOrderId(id)`, not `findByOrderId(id)`. `PaymentService.findStoredByOrderId(...)` is annotated `@Transactional(readOnly = true)` and delegates directly to `paymentRepository.findByOrderId(orderId)` with no provider sync or expiry calls, while the explicit admin sync route remains guarded by `ORDER_PAYMENT_PERMISSION`. `git diff --check` passed for touched files and `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` SUCCEEDED.
- Resolution: Admin payment-history reads are now side-effect-free; state-changing provider sync and expiry are no longer reachable through the order-detail/refund evidence GET path.
- Expected fix direction: Split read-only admin payment history from provider sync/expiry. Have GET return stored rows only, and keep state-changing provider sync behind `POST /admin/orders/payments/{paymentId}/sync` with `orders:payment`, callbacks/webhooks, scheduled expiry, or explicit audited action paths. Add regression coverage proving a role with only `orders` can view payment history but cannot trigger provider sync, expiry, or order status changes.

### F349: Product list pagination cannot reach products beyond the first bounded server window

- Area: Frontend / ProductList pagination after bounded public catalog requests; Backend / public product list response contract
- Environment: `/products` storefront listing when more than `PRODUCT_LIST_FETCH_SIZE` matching public products exist, especially default catalog, search, category, discount, and refinement views
- Steps: Populate more than 96 active public products that match a storefront listing, open `/products`, and use the visible pagination controls.
- Expected: The product list should either fetch the requested backend page as the user changes pages or receive a response contract with total/next-page metadata so all matching products can be reached without downloading the full catalog.
- Actual: `ProductList` always fetches only backend `page=0` with `size=PRODUCT_LIST_FETCH_SIZE` by default, stores that bounded response in `products`, and then paginates only that in-memory subset. The Ant Design pagination total is `sortedProducts.length`, so the UI can show at most the first 96 matching products and has no path to request backend pages 1, 2, etc. Products sorted after the first server window are hidden from browsing/search/category/refinement views even though the backend can return them with higher `page` values.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 00:39 UTC static review found `ProductList.tsx` lines 37-38 defining `PRODUCT_LIST_PAGE_SIZE = 12` and `PRODUCT_LIST_FETCH_SIZE = PRODUCT_LIST_PAGE_SIZE * 8` (96). `fetchProducts()` lines 638-648 sets `page: filters.page ?? 0` and `size: filters.size ?? PRODUCT_LIST_FETCH_SIZE`, then lines 650-676 stores only `res.data` in `products` and resets `currentPage` to 1. Local pagination still computes totals from `sortedProducts.length` at lines 1399-1406 and renders `<Pagination total={sortedProducts.length}>` at lines 2153-2156; there is no `onChange` path that calls `fetchProducts` with a new backend page. Backend `ProductController.getAllProducts()` lines 33-65 returns a raw `List<ProductPublicResponse>` with no total/next-page metadata, while `ProductServiceImpl.applyProductPagination()` lines 567-582 can return later pages only when callers pass higher `page` values. This is distinct from F343, which tracked the previous full-catalog download; F349 tracks the post-bounding regression where catalog rows beyond the first bounded window are unreachable.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 00:54 UTC retest found the current product-list path now uses a paginated public product contract. Backend `ProductController.getAllProducts()` lines 35-72 returns `ProductPublicPageResponse` with `items`, `total`, `page`, `size`, `totalPages`, `hasNext`, and `hasPrevious` whenever `page` or `size` is supplied. Frontend `productApi.getPage()` lines 1258-1270 always appends `page` and `size` and normalizes either page-object or legacy array responses. `ProductList.fetchProducts()` lines 648-653 sends `page`/`size`, lines 679-685 stores only `res.data.items` plus `res.data.total`, and `handleProductPageChange()` lines 796-803 fetches the requested backend page. `productCountForUi` and the rendered pagination now use the backend total for server pagination at lines 1135 and 2202-2204. Remaining collection-mode pagination mismatch is tracked separately as F352.
- Expected fix direction: Add a paginated public product response with `items`, `total`, `page`, `size`, and `totalPages` or `hasNext`, then wire `ProductList` pagination/filter/sort changes to backend page requests. Keep the bounded initial load, but ensure all matching products remain discoverable and that local fallback snapshots do not mask server pagination gaps.

### F348: Runtime log debug defaults are narrowed by a duplicate property key

- Area: Backend / application.properties runtime log management configuration
- Environment: `/admin/logs`, `LogManagementService`, default local/staging/production startup when `ADMIN_LOGS_ALLOWED_LOGGER_PREFIXES` is not explicitly set
- Steps: Start the backend with default properties, open the runtime logs admin page, and try to enable debug for an operational logger such as `org.mybatis`, `org.springframework.web`, or `org.springframework.security`.
- Expected: The default allowed logger prefixes should match the runtime log service defaults and the additional debug logger defaults, so operators can use the log admin page to temporarily enable the documented MyBatis, Spring Web, and Spring Security diagnostics during incidents.
- Actual: `application.properties` defines `admin.logs.allowed-logger-prefixes` twice. The earlier declaration allows `com.example.shop,org.mybatis,org.springframework.web,org.springframework.security`, but the later logging section redeclares the same key with the narrower default `com.example.shop`. Standard properties loading keeps the later value, so default runtime log management rejects the framework loggers and filters the configured `admin.logs.additional-debug-loggers` set down to nothing unless an environment override is supplied.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 00:35 UTC static review found `src/main/resources/application.properties` lines 99-100 setting `admin.logs.allowed-logger-prefixes` to include `org.mybatis`, `org.springframework.web`, and `org.springframework.security`, plus `admin.logs.additional-debug-loggers` with those same framework loggers. The same file later redefines `admin.logs.allowed-logger-prefixes` at line 212 as `${ADMIN_LOGS_ALLOWED_LOGGER_PREFIXES:com.example.shop}`. `LogManagementService.DEFAULT_ALLOWED_LOGGERS` and `DEFAULT_ADDITIONAL_DEBUG_LOGGERS` lines 37-38 include the framework loggers, and `additionalDebugLoggers()` lines 337-344 filters additional loggers through `isAllowedLogger(...)`. The existing `LogManagementServiceTest` setup lines 37-39 has to inject the broader allowed-prefix property explicitly to exercise the expected additional debug behavior, so it does not cover the shipped default property file. `awk` duplicate-key scan over `application.properties` returned `admin.logs.allowed-logger-prefixes` as the only duplicate active key.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 04:43 UTC static retest found the duplicate override has been removed from current source. `src/main/resources/application.properties` now declares `admin.logs.allowed-logger-prefixes` once at line 99 with the intended default `com.example.shop,org.mybatis,org.springframework.web,org.springframework.security`, and the logging section no longer redeclares the same key. `rg` over the property file returns a single active `admin.logs.allowed-logger-prefixes` occurrence, so default runtime log management no longer drops the MyBatis, Spring Web, or Spring Security debug logger prefixes.
- Resolution: The shipped default log-management allowlist now has one active property declaration and preserves the framework logger prefixes expected by `LogManagementService` defaults and additional debug logger configuration.
- Expected fix direction: Keep a single `admin.logs.allowed-logger-prefixes` declaration with the intended default, or make the narrower default explicit across service constants, tests, UI copy, and additional-debug defaults. Add a configuration regression check that fails on duplicate active keys in `application.properties`, especially for operational/security properties.

### F347: Customer-service order permission can execute refunds, payment sync, and shipment mutations

- Area: Backend / AdminRoleService order permissions and AdminController order mutation authorization
- Environment: Default `CUSTOMER_SERVICE` role and any custom admin role granted only the `orders` page permission; `/admin/orders/**` mutation routes including `POST /admin/orders/{id}/refund`, `POST /admin/orders/payments/{paymentId}/sync`, `PUT /admin/orders/{id}/status`, and `POST /admin/orders/batch-ship`
- Steps: Assign an operator the seeded `CUSTOMER_SERVICE` role or a custom role whose permissions contain `orders`, then send direct API requests to refund an order, sync a provider payment, manually move a pending-payment order to `PENDING_SHIPMENT`, ship an order, complete/reject a return, or batch-ship orders.
- Expected: Read/support access to the order page should not automatically grant financial and fulfillment state transitions. Refunds, payment-state sync/confirmation, shipment, cancellation, return completion/rejection, and batch fulfillment should require action-specific permissions, a higher operational role, or explicit super-admin/manager approval.
- Actual: `CUSTOMER_SERVICE` is seeded with the `orders` permission, custom non-`USER` role codes receive `ROLE_ADMIN`, and `AdminPermissionInterceptor` authorizes every `/admin/orders/**` route by checking only that single `orders` page key. The order mutation methods do not perform finer-grained permission checks, so an order-page/support operator can call refund, payment sync, status transition, and batch shipment endpoints directly.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 00:29 UTC static review found `AdminRoleService.ensureSchema()` lines 98-99 seeding `CUSTOMER_SERVICE` with `orders`, and lines 33-39 mapping all `/admin/orders` paths to the same `orders` permission. `UserDetailsImpl.build()` lines 52-57 grants `ROLE_ADMIN` for any non-empty non-`USER` `roleCode`, satisfying the class-level `@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")` on `AdminController` lines 88-91. `AdminPermissionInterceptor.preHandle()` lines 30-35 then calls only `adminRoleService.canAccess(...)`; for `/admin/orders/**`, `canAccess()` lines 155-160 checks membership of `orders` and does not consider HTTP method or action. `AdminController` lines 1076-1090 perform admin payment sync, lines 1098-1140 perform status changes including cancel, confirm paid, return approval/rejection/completion, and shipping, lines 1164-1180 execute refunds, and lines 1188-1237 batch-ship orders without an additional role/permission guard. This is distinct from F275, which tracks unmapped admin paths defaulting open; F347 tracks mapped `/admin/orders/**` paths whose single page permission is too broad for high-impact mutations.
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 00:44 UTC retest found the direct mutation paths now have server-side action permission guards. `AdminRoleService` lines 28-40 defines `orders:status`, `orders:fulfillment`, `orders:payment`, and `orders:refund`; `CUSTOMER_SERVICE` remains seeded only with page permissions at lines 104-107; `syncOrderPayment()`, `updateOrderStatus()`, `refundOrder()`, and `batchShipOrders()` call `requireAdminActionPermission(...)` at `AdminController` lines 1083-1084, 1126-1128, 1177-1178, and 1202-1203. `permissionForOrderStatusAction()` lines 1869-1884 maps manual payment confirmation, shipment/return fulfillment, return refund completion, and generic status changes to action permissions, and `requireAdminActionPermission()` lines 1887-1903 records an audit failure and throws 403 when the operator lacks the required action permission. Residual read-triggered payment mutation through `GET /admin/orders/{id}/payments` is tracked separately as F350.
- Expected fix direction: Split order permissions into at least read/support, fulfillment/status, payment-sync, and refund/financial actions, then enforce those checks server-side on the specific admin methods before calling order/payment services. Seed `CUSTOMER_SERVICE` with only the least-privilege actions it actually needs, update the frontend permission model to match, and add regression tests proving an `orders`-read/support role cannot refund, confirm payment, sync provider state, or batch-ship.

### F346: Payment callbacks are not bound to the stored provider transaction

- Area: Backend / PaymentService callback validation and payment state transition
- Environment: `POST /payments/callback` and `/payment/callback` for non-Stripe callback channels, especially `GENERIC_API` channels that persist a provider `transactionId`/`providerReference` during payment creation
- Steps: Create a generic API payment where the gateway create response stores transaction id `txn-a`, then send a correctly signed callback for the same `orderNo`, channel, amount, and fresh timestamp but with `transactionId=txn-b` and status `SUCCESS`.
- Expected: A provider paid callback should be accepted only when it is bound to the pending payment record. If a pending payment already has a stored transaction id or provider reference, the callback transaction/reference should match before the system marks the payment `PAID` and advances the order.
- Actual: `handleCallback()` finds the payment only by `orderNo` and channel, verifies the signature over the callback-supplied transaction id, checks only amount/status/order state, and then calls `markPaidDetailed()` with the callback transaction/reference. `markPaidDetailed()` updates the row by id/status only, so a signed but stale, misrouted, or provider-bug callback for a different transaction under the same order/channel can overwrite the persisted provider identifiers and move the order to `PENDING_SHIPMENT`.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 00:20 UTC static review found `PaymentService.handleCallback()` lines 166-202 loading the payment through `paymentRepository.findByOrderNoAndChannel(...)`, verifying the request signature, comparing only amount, then passing `request.getTransactionId()` and `request.getProviderReference()` into `markPaidDetailed(...)` without comparing them to the existing payment identifiers. `PaymentService.createGenericApiPayment()` lines 485-498 stores `gateway.transactionId` and `gateway.providerReference` on the pending payment. `PaymentMapper.findByOrderNoAndChannel` lines 33-36 selects the latest row for only `order_no` and `channel`, and `PaymentMapper.markPaidDetailed` lines 126-134 updates by `id` and `status = 'PENDING'` only. The existing `assertMatchingPaidCallback()` helper at lines 748-759 is not invoked by `handleCallback()` before the pending-to-paid transition. This is distinct from F293/F224, which cover Stripe webhook/provider-sync amount/currency validation; F346 tracks the generic callback transaction/reference binding gap.
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-31 00:29 UTC updated `PaymentService.handleCallback()` to call `assertMatchingPaidCallback(...)` before returning an already-paid payment or marking a pending payment paid. The helper now requires the callback transaction id and effective provider reference to match stored identifiers when those identifiers exist, and payment simulation reuses stored identifiers so the guarded path stays consistent. `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` passed.
- Expected fix direction: Resolve callbacks by immutable provider transaction/reference when available, or require exact callback transaction/provider-reference matches against the pending payment before marking it paid. Keep order/channel/amount checks as secondary validation, reject identifier mismatches with audit evidence, and add regression coverage for stale/misrouted signed callbacks.

### F345: Generic API payment responses can send customers to unvalidated checkout URLs

- Area: Backend / PaymentService generic API payment response validation; Frontend / payment URL navigation
- Environment: Payment channels configured with `provider=GENERIC_API`, customer checkout, payment recovery, profile order payments, and public order tracking payment actions
- Steps: Configure a generic API payment channel, then have the provider create endpoint return a successful JSON response with `paymentUrl` or `checkoutUrl` pointing to an unexpected public HTTP/HTTPS host.
- Expected: The backend should validate returned checkout URLs before persisting and exposing them to customers. In production, generic payment URLs should be HTTPS, credential-free, public, and restricted to an allowlisted provider host/pattern or a signed provider contract for that channel. Frontend navigation should not be the first line of defense for payment redirect trust.
- Actual: `createGenericApiPayment()` and `refreshGenericApiPayment()` validate only the configured `createUrl` before calling the gateway, then persist `gateway.paymentUrl` directly. `parseGenericPaymentResponse()` accepts any nonblank `paymentUrl`/`checkoutUrl` and transaction id from a 2xx response without applying `GatewayUrlValidator`, HTTPS enforcement, private-host rejection, or channel-specific host allowlisting. The storefront payment actions then call `navigateToSafeUrl()`, which allows any syntactically valid `http://` or `https://` URL without credentials. A compromised, misconfigured, or integration-test provider response can therefore turn a legitimate checkout or payment recovery action into an arbitrary external redirect.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 00:14 UTC static review found `PaymentService.createGenericApiPayment()` lines 470-498 and `refreshGenericApiPayment()` lines 502-530 validate `createUrl` with `GatewayUrlValidator.requireOutboundHttpUrl(...)` but then store `gateway.paymentUrl` unchanged. `PaymentService.parseGenericPaymentResponse()` lines 559-590 reads `paymentUrl`/`checkoutUrl`, requires only that it and `transactionId` are nonblank, and returns it without URL validation. `PaymentChannelAvailabilityService.isChannelAvailableForCheckout()` lines 40-45 checks only configured `createUrl`/`refundUrl` for generic API channels. Frontend `safeUrl.ts` lines 7-28 accepts any http/https URL with no credentials and sets `window.location.href`; checkout/profile/tracking payment buttons use that helper for `payment.paymentUrl`.
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-31 00:29 UTC added server-side generic checkout URL validation before `paymentUrl` is inserted or updated. Returned URLs now pass `GatewayUrlValidator.requireOutboundHttpUrl(...)`, require HTTPS in production, reject local/private/credentialed URLs through the existing gateway validator, and must match the configured `createUrl`/`checkoutUrl` host or channel metadata allowlist keys such as `payment-url-hosts`, `checkout-url-hosts`, or `allowed-payment-hosts`. `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` passed.
- Expected fix direction: Validate generic API returned payment URLs server-side before insertion/update. Require HTTPS public hosts in production, reject credentials/control characters/private or local hosts, and add per-channel allowed checkout host/pattern configuration or compare against the configured provider origin where appropriate. Keep frontend URL safety as a defense-in-depth fallback, not the trust boundary.

### F344: Guest payment and provider return URLs expose guest email in query strings

- Area: Backend / PaymentService payment URL generation; Frontend / PaymentInstructions guest tracking handoff
- Environment: Guest checkout payment creation for generic redirect, generic API, and Stripe return URLs; `/payment/{orderNo}?guestEmail=...`; `/track-order?email=...` handoff
- Steps: Place a guest order, then create or open a payment link or provider success/cancel return URL.
- Expected: Guest payment recovery and tracking authorization should use an opaque one-time token, short-lived access token, or authenticated session instead of putting the guest email address in URLs. Third-party payment providers, edge logs, browser history, and referrers should not receive guest PII in query parameters.
- Actual: `PaymentService` extracts the guest email from the order's `[Guest]` shipping address and appends it as `guestEmail` to customer payment URLs and contextual provider success/cancel URLs. Stripe/generic providers can receive return URLs containing the address, the browser stores it in history, and `PaymentInstructions` carries the address forward to `/track-order?email=...`. This expands the existing URL-PII exposure into the payment-provider flow, where guest email can appear in provider dashboards, CDN/proxy/access logs, referrers, and shared payment links.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-31 00:05 UTC static review found `PaymentService.buildPaymentUrl()` lines 825-837 deriving `guestEmail`, building `guestQuery`, and appending `&guestEmail=...` to `/payment/{orderNo}` links. `PaymentService.contextualReturnUrl()` lines 1075-1090 forces guest returns to `/track-order`, appends `orderNo`, then appends `guestEmail`; those URLs are used by `buildStripeCheckoutSession()` lines 615-618 and generic API payment payloads at lines 545-546. `PaymentInstructions.tsx` lines 39-45 reads `guestEmail`/`email` from the URL query and navigates to `/track-order?orderNo=...&email=...`, preserving the PII-bearing URL chain. This is distinct from F244, which tracks the direct order-tracking and coupon-claim endpoint query parameters; F344 tracks payment links and provider return URLs that can expose guest email to third parties and payment/referrer logs.
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-31 00:29 UTC removed guest email query construction from `PaymentService.buildPaymentUrl()` and `contextualReturnUrl()`, so customer payment links and provider success/cancel URLs contain only order/payment status identifiers. `PaymentInstructions` strips legacy `guestEmail`/`email` params, recovers same-device guest context from local storage, and no longer forwards email to `/track-order`; checkout and order tracking now use `orderNo` URLs plus stored guest context. Frontend build and APK 1.0.10 artifact checks passed, and live `/downloads/mobile-version.json` points to the email-free 1.0.10 package.
- Expected fix direction: Use an opaque guest payment/recovery token or short-lived order access token in payment and return URLs, store guest email server-side, and have tracking/payment pages redeem the token through a body/header/session-backed read. Ensure provider success/cancel URLs and customer-visible payment links contain only non-PII identifiers.

### F343: Storefront home and product list load the full public catalog before local slicing

- Area: Frontend / Home and ProductList catalog loading; Backend / Public product pagination defaults
- Environment: `Home.tsx`, `ProductList.tsx`, `productApi.getAll()`, and unauthenticated `GET /products` on large catalogs
- Steps: Open the storefront home page or `/products` page when the public catalog contains many products.
- Expected: First-viewport discovery, featured sections, and product-list pages should request bounded result sets with server-side pagination or purpose-built home/featured endpoints. Public product reads should enforce a default page size when callers omit `page` and `size`, and the UI should request additional pages as users scroll or paginate.
- Actual: The home page calls `productApi.getAll()` with no `page` or `size`, then saves and stores the entire returned catalog even though it initially displays only the first discovery batch and 12 featured products. ProductList likewise fetches products without page/size for the visible listing and then slices `sortedProducts` locally for 12-item pages. `productApi.getAll()` appends `page` and `size` only when the caller supplies them, and `ProductServiceImpl.applyProductPagination()` returns the full product list when both are null. A large catalog can therefore make ordinary home/product-list page loads download, localize, cache, and render state for the entire public catalog before showing a small page.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-30 23:57 UTC static review found `frontend/src/pages/Home.tsx` lines 450-459 calling `productApi.getAll()`, saving `productsRes.data`, deriving `featured` with `.slice(0, 12)`, storing all localized products, and setting `visibleCount` to `DISCOVERY_BATCH_SIZE`; lines 631-640 then slice discovery products locally and increment visible count in batches. `frontend/src/pages/ProductList.tsx` lines 638-667 call `productApi.getAll(kw, cid, disc, filters)` and store the full localized response, while lines 1391-1397 calculate total pages and slice `sortedProducts` locally by `pageSize = 12`. `frontend/src/api/index.ts` lines 1167-1185 only append `page`/`size` params when filters provide them. Backend `ProductServiceImpl.java` lines 206-208 builds public results from `productRepository.findAll()`, and lines 567-570 return all products when `page` and `size` are both null. This is distinct from F235, which tracks the direct `/search` no-pagination endpoint; F343 tracks the visible storefront home/product-list `/products` loading path.
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-31 00:29 UTC bounded storefront catalog reads. Public product list requests now default to backend pagination when `page`/`size` are omitted, admin reads keep the old full-list behavior, `/home/products` requests `page=0&size=24`, `Home.tsx` requests bounded discovery plus featured products and merges them, and `ProductList.tsx` requests a bounded page window instead of full-catalog local slicing. `npm run build` passed.
- Expected fix direction: Make home and product-list screens request bounded public product pages or dedicated section endpoints with totals/next-page metadata. Enforce a backend default page size for omitted `page`/`size` on public product reads, and adjust snapshot/fallback behavior so it does not require downloading the entire live catalog on normal page loads.

### F342: Retired versioned Android APKs remain publicly shipped with unsafe artifacts

- Area: Mobile release artifact retention; Static frontend download payload
- Environment: `frontend/public/downloads`, `frontend/build/downloads`, versioned APK URLs such as `/downloads/shoptest-1.0.2.apk`, `/downloads/shoptest-1.0.3.apk`, and `/downloads/shoptest-1.0.4.apk`
- Steps: Run the production frontend build, then inspect the versioned APKs copied into `frontend/build/downloads`.
- Expected: Public static downloads should expose only the current release and any intentionally supported rollback versions, and every public APK should meet the same release hardening baseline: no bundled source maps, no app-wide cleartext traffic, and no debug signing certificate. Retired/vulnerable APKs should be removed from public/build/live static paths and CDN caches.
- Actual: The production build still publishes retired versioned APKs alongside the current `shoptest.apk`/`shoptest-1.0.6.apk`. The 1.0.2 and 1.0.3 APKs still bundle frontend source maps and advertise the main source map, and they still have `"cleartext": true`. The 1.0.2, 1.0.3, and 1.0.4 APKs are all signed with the Android Debug certificate. Anyone with an old versioned URL can still download artifacts that bypass the hardening applied to the current APK.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-30 23:43 UTC artifact review after `npm run build` found `frontend/build/downloads` contains `shoptest-1.0.2.apk` (41,988,486 bytes), `shoptest-1.0.3.apk` (93,880,604 bytes), `shoptest-1.0.4.apk` (234,946,725 bytes), `shoptest-1.0.5.apk`, `shoptest-1.0.6.apk`, and `shoptest.apk`. `unzip -Z1 frontend/public/downloads/shoptest-1.0.2.apk | rg '\.map$' | wc -l` and the same check for 1.0.3 both returned 130; JS map counts for both returned 82, and their bundled `main.*.js` files end with `sourceMappingURL=main.559457b3.js.map`. `unzip -p ... assets/capacitor.config.json | rg 'cleartext'` shows `"cleartext": true` for 1.0.2 and 1.0.3, while current 1.0.5/1.0.6 show false. `keytool -printcert -jarfile` reports owner/issuer `C=US, O=Android, CN=Android Debug` with SHA256 fingerprint `A5:9C:1D:F8:08:78:4A:F8:70:70:5A:C1:FB:13:B0:A1:2E:50:99:AB:3D:14:0A:26:05:2A:88:5A:D6:66:87:F1` for 1.0.2, 1.0.3, and 1.0.4. This is distinct from F334-F336, which track the current public APK's release signing/source-map/cleartext state; F342 tracks retained retired APKs that remain public.
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 23:47 UTC added retired-version cleanup to `/home/guhao/shoptest-mobile/scripts/build-android-apk.js`, so future APK publishes delete versioned `shoptest-*.apk` files other than the current version while keeping `shoptest.apk`. Removed retired APKs 1.0.2, 1.0.3, 1.0.4, and 1.0.5 from `frontend/public/downloads`, `frontend/build/downloads`, and `/var/www/shoptest/downloads`, reran `npm run build` successfully in `frontend`, and synced `frontend/build/` to `/var/www/shoptest/`. Post-fix `find` shows each download directory contains only `mobile-version.json`, `shoptest.apk`, and `shoptest-1.0.6.apk`; all six APK copies share SHA256 `dffd30995ad4c6449fb9475ec6641e28f5830129838a35bb90d8194a81fb7baa`. Artifact checks for `/var/www/shoptest/downloads/shoptest-1.0.6.apk` show 0 bundled `.map` files, 0 `assets/public/downloads/` entries, and `"cleartext": false`. Live checks show `https://pet.686888666.xyz/downloads/mobile-version.json` points to `/downloads/shoptest-1.0.6.apk`, both current versioned and cache-busted stable URLs download SHA256 `dffd30995ad4c6449fb9475ec6641e28f5830129838a35bb90d8194a81fb7baa`, and retired `/downloads/shoptest-1.0.2.apk` now returns 404.
- Retest: 2026-05-30 23:57 UTC post-regression `npm run build` as `guhao` succeeded and regenerated `mobile-version.json` for `1.0.6` / `10006`. `frontend/public/downloads` and `frontend/build/downloads` each contain only `mobile-version.json`, `shoptest.apk`, and `shoptest-1.0.6.apk`; all four local APK copies share SHA256 `dffd30995ad4c6449fb9475ec6641e28f5830129838a35bb90d8194a81fb7baa`. Current APK artifact checks found 0 bundled `.map` files, 0 `assets/public/downloads/` entries, no source-map references in bundled `asset-manifest.json`, and `"cleartext": false`. F334 remains separately OPEN because the current APK is still Android Debug signed.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 00:14 UTC after ownership normalization, `npm run build` as `guhao` succeeded and regenerated mobile release metadata for `1.0.9` / `10009`. `frontend/public/downloads` and `frontend/build/downloads` each contain only `mobile-version.json`, `shoptest.apk`, and `shoptest-1.0.9.apk`; all four local APK copies share SHA256 `60ebf193f6495aa354dcdc58039c0955fcae4f23c091dcd5c2c9ce53d79cf3f1`. Current APK artifact checks found 0 bundled `.map` or `assets/public/downloads/` entries, no source-map references in bundled `asset-manifest.json`, and `"cleartext": false`. F334 remains separately OPEN because the current APK is still Android Debug signed.
- Expected fix direction: Remove retired vulnerable APKs from `frontend/public/downloads`, generated `frontend/build/downloads`, the live static directory, and CDN caches unless they are explicitly supported releases rebuilt with the current hardening baseline. Add a release-artifact validation gate that fails if any public APK contains `assets/public/**/*.map`, source-map references, `cleartext: true`, or a debug signing certificate.

### F341: GET payment history/latest endpoints mutate payment and order state

- Area: Backend / PaymentController safe read endpoints; PaymentService provider sync and expiry side effects
- Environment: `GET /payments/order/{orderId}`, `GET /payment/order/{orderId}`, `GET /payments/guest/order/{orderId}`, `GET /payment/guest/order/{orderId}`, `GET /payments/order/{orderId}/latest`, `GET /payment/order/{orderId}/latest`, `GET /payments/guest/order/{orderId}/latest`, and `GET /payment/guest/order/{orderId}/latest`
- Steps: Refresh a signed-in or guest order payment history/latest view for an order with a pending Stripe payment whose provider session is paid, expired, or whose local payment has passed its expiry time.
- Expected: Safe GET reads should return local payment/order data without mutating payment or order state and without contacting external payment providers. Provider sync, expiry, paid transitions, and cancellation should run only through explicit sync/action, callback/webhook, scheduler, or audited operational paths.
- Actual: The GET history/latest routes call `PaymentService.findByOrderId()` or `PaymentService.findLatestByOrderId()`, and those read helpers perform provider sync and expiry side effects. A profile/order-tracking page refresh can retrieve Stripe session state, mark a payment `PAID`, move the order to `PENDING_SHIPMENT`, mark a payment `EXPIRED`, or cancel the order when no active pending payments remain.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 08:54 UTC development pass split safe payment GET reads from state-changing payment sync. The authenticated and guest payment history/latest routes now call read-only stored payment service methods instead of provider-syncing/expiry methods.
- Evidence: 2026-05-30 23:34 UTC static review found `PaymentController.java` lines 201-235 expose authenticated and guest payment history/latest GET routes under both class-level mappings (`/payments` and `/payment`) and delegate to `findByOrderId()`/`findLatestByOrderId()`. `PaymentService.java` lines 272-290 and 314-325 perform `syncProviderPaymentState(...)` and `expirePayment(...)` inside those read helpers. `expirePayment()` at lines 377-385 calls `paymentRepository.markExpired(...)` and may call `orderService.cancelOrder(...)`; `syncProviderPaymentState()` at lines 637-669 retrieves Stripe sessions, can call `paymentRepository.markPaidDetailed(...)`, and can move the order to `PENDING_SHIPMENT`.
- Retest: 2026-05-31 08:54 UTC static/build retest confirmed `PaymentController.findByOrderId(...)`, `findGuestByOrderId(...)`, `findLatestByOrderId(...)`, and `findLatestGuestByOrderId(...)` now call `paymentService.findStoredByOrderId(...)` or `findStoredLatestByOrderId(...)`. `PaymentService.findStoredByOrderId(...)` and `findStoredLatestByOrderId(...)` are read-only repository reads and do not call `syncProviderPaymentState(...)` or `expirePayment(...)`; `POST /payments/{id}/sync`, callbacks, and webhook paths still use explicit state-changing methods. `git diff --check` passed for the touched payment files and `./mvnw -DskipTests compile` SUCCEEDED.
- Resolution: Safe payment history/latest GET routes now return stored payment data without contacting providers or mutating payment/order state.
- Expected fix direction: Split side-effect-free payment history/latest reads from state-changing sync/expiry workflows. Keep GET endpoints on read-only repository access, and invoke provider sync/expiry only from `POST /payments/{id}/sync`, callbacks/webhooks, scheduled expiry jobs, or deliberate customer/admin actions with audit coverage.

### F340: Storefront Android download can still serve stale stable APK

- Area: Frontend / Android App download entry; Mobile release manifest; Static frontend deployment
- Environment: `/home/guhao/shoptest/frontend`, `frontend/scripts/generate-mobile-version.js`, `frontend/src/utils/mobileUpdate.ts`, generated `mobile-version.json`, and the live `https://pet.686888666.xyz/` static storefront
- Steps: Open the web storefront Android App download entry after the 1.0.5 APK build, then compare the downloaded file version/size/checksum.
- Expected: The storefront should direct customers to the current versioned APK, or at minimum a cache-busted stable APK URL, so CDN/browser caches cannot return an old 1.0 package.
- Actual: The live storefront was still serving an old bundle (`main.e84e6153.js`) from the previous static deployment, and the plain stable URL `/downloads/shoptest.apk` was cached by Cloudflare with the old 10.7MB package. Local origin files already contained the 6.1MB 1.0.5 APK, but the visible storefront download path could still land on the stale cached stable object.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-30 23:16 UTC `curl -I https://pet.686888666.xyz/downloads/shoptest.apk` returned `content-length: 10769281`, `last-modified: Sat, 30 May 2026 21:35:23 GMT`, `cf-cache-status: HIT`, and `age: 6026`, while `https://pet.686888666.xyz/downloads/shoptest-1.0.5.apk` returned `content-length: 6385151`. The live home page loaded `/static/js/main.e84e6153.js`, but the local current build used a newer bundle.
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 23:24 UTC changed `frontend/scripts/generate-mobile-version.js` so `legacyApkUrl` is generated as `/downloads/shoptest.apk?v=<version>`, and hardened `frontend/src/utils/mobileUpdate.ts` so default/fallback Android download URLs prefer `shoptest-<version>.apk` before stable URLs. Regenerated `frontend/public/downloads/mobile-version.json` and `frontend/src/generated/mobileRelease.ts`, ran `npm run build` successfully in `/home/guhao/shoptest/frontend`, and synced `frontend/build/` to the live static directory. Live verification showed the home page now loads `/static/js/main.7ba45a9c.js`, `mobile-version.json` points to `/downloads/shoptest-1.0.5.apk` with legacy `/downloads/shoptest.apk?v=1.0.5`, and both the versioned URL and cache-busted stable URL return SHA256 `20363030fb3f07503bbf3bf0a4f0248451e4b219f9e8a8de92dfb4adef682baf`.

### F339: Nginx cache-control locations drop inherited security headers

- Area: Deployment / Nginx static frontend and upload proxy security headers
- Environment: `deploy/nginx/shoptest-static.conf`, `deploy/nginx/shoptest-edge.conf.template`, static asset responses, runtime config, denied source-map responses, health checks, and proxied uploads
- Steps: Deploy either Nginx static frontend config and request representative paths handled by child locations that define `add_header Cache-Control`, such as `/healthz`, `/static/js/main.js`, `/runtime-config.js`, a denied `*.map` URL, `/uploads/...`, or an SPA 404 fallback.
- Expected: Security headers declared at the HTTPS server level should remain on all static/proxied asset responses unless a child location intentionally repeats or includes the full security header set.
- Actual: In Nginx, `add_header` directives from a parent context are inherited only when the child context has no `add_header` directives of its own. Both frontend configs set the security headers at server level, but several child locations define only `add_header Cache-Control`, so those paths can lose `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Referrer-Policy`, `Permissions-Policy`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, and `Content-Security-Policy` while still returning cache headers.
- Status: FIXED
- Fix: 2026-05-31 10:08 UTC development pass removed the query-string token fallback from `SupportWebSocketHandler.resolveToken()`. Support WebSocket authentication now accepts only the existing subprotocol token path, so direct `?token=` URL authentication no longer works.
- Evidence: 2026-05-30 23:17 UTC static review found `deploy/nginx/shoptest-static.conf` lines 61-66 and `deploy/nginx/shoptest-edge.conf.template` lines 63-68 declare the server-level security headers. Child locations then define only `add_header Cache-Control` at `shoptest-static.conf` lines 68-70, 94-97, 101-103, 107-108, 112-114, 118-120, and 150-158, and at `shoptest-edge.conf.template` lines 70-72, 96-99, 103-105, 109-110, 114-116, 120-122, and 150-161. This is distinct from F278, which tracks backend resource-handler `nosniff` behavior; F339 tracks edge Nginx header inheritance across web asset and upload responses.
- Expected fix direction: Move the security headers into a shared include/snippet that is used in every location declaring `add_header`, repeat the full security header set in those locations, or use a pinned supported Nginx inheritance mechanism. Verify representative static, runtime-config, denied-map, upload, health-check, and SPA fallback responses retain both cache and security headers.

### F338: Admin user status API can convert registered accounts into GUEST

- Area: Backend / AdminController user status authorization; Frontend / UserManagement guest-status lock; Notification audience selection
- Environment: `/admin/users`, `PUT /admin/users/{id}`, guest checkout user status contract, and customer notification broadcasts
- Steps: As an admin with user-management access, send a direct request such as `PUT /admin/users/{customerId}` with JSON body `{"status":"GUEST"}` for an existing registered customer account.
- Expected: `GUEST` should remain a system-created guest-checkout account state, or the admin API should enforce explicit, audited transitions that cannot silently reclassify registered accounts. The frontend's guest-status lock should be backed by the same backend contract.
- Actual: `AdminController.normalizeUserStatus()` accepts `GUEST`, and the `updateUser()` status branch persists it for non-self, non-privileged customer targets. The current `UserManagement.tsx` UI only disables the row action when the row is already `GUEST` and otherwise toggles only `ACTIVE`/`BANNED`, so a direct API caller can bypass the UI lock and mark a registered account as a guest identity. That account remains login-enabled because `UserDetailsImpl.isEnabled()` only disables `BANNED`, but customer broadcasts use `UserMapper.findActiveCustomerIds()` and exclude non-`ACTIVE` rows, so the account can be silently removed from active-customer notification audiences while appearing as a guest account in user management.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-30 22:37 UTC static review found `src/main/java/com/example/shop/controller/AdminController.java` allows `ACTIVE`, `BANNED`, and `GUEST` in `normalizeUserStatus()` and writes `existing.setStatus(normalizedStatus)` in `updateUser()` after only self and privileged-target checks. `frontend/src/pages/UserManagement.tsx` locks the visible status action only for rows already normalized to `GUEST`, not for direct API requests. `src/main/resources/mapper/UserMapper.xml` `findActiveCustomerIds` includes only `role='USER'` rows whose status is null or `ACTIVE`, and `src/main/java/com/example/shop/service/NotificationService.java` uses that query for customer broadcasts.
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 22:53 UTC added a backend guest-status guard in `AdminController.updateUser()`. The status update branch now rejects both direct attempts to set `status=GUEST` and attempts to change an existing `GUEST` account through the admin status API, returning a controlled `Guest status is system-managed` error and audit failure before persistence. Static `git diff --check` passed for `AdminController.java`.

### F337: HTTP health check is shadowed by canonical HTTPS redirect

- Area: Deployment / Nginx static frontend health checks
- Environment: `deploy/nginx/shoptest-static.conf`, `deploy/nginx/shoptest-edge.conf.template`, and `docs/static-frontend-nginx-deploy.md`
- Steps: Deploy the static or edge Nginx config, then run `curl -I http://127.0.0.1/healthz` as documented.
- Expected: `/healthz` should remain a lightweight Nginx-level `204 No Content` health check for local load balancers, uptime checks, and deployment smoke tests.
- Actual: Both HTTP server blocks define `location = /healthz`, but the same server context also has a top-level `return 301 https://...$request_uri`. Nginx server-level rewrite/return directives run before the location handler, so the HTTP health check is redirected instead of returning 204. The docs still instruct operators to check `http://127.0.0.1/healthz` and state it returns 204, making health checks fail or follow TLS/certificate paths when they only need to verify the edge process is alive.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-30 22:24 UTC static review found `deploy/nginx/shoptest-static.conf` lines 1-11 and `deploy/nginx/shoptest-edge.conf.template` lines 1-11 place `location = /healthz` in the same port-80 server as a server-level `return 301`. `docs/static-frontend-nginx-deploy.md` still documents `curl -I http://127.0.0.1/healthz` and says the template returns `204 No Content`.
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 22:28 UTC changed both HTTP server blocks to keep `/healthz` as a location-level `204` response and moved the canonical HTTPS redirect into `location /`, so the documented HTTP health check is no longer shadowed by a server-level return.

### F336: Public Android APK bundles production source maps

- Area: Mobile release artifact / Android APK web assets
- Environment: `frontend/public/downloads/shoptest.apk`, `frontend/build/downloads/shoptest.apk`, and bundled `assets/public/static`
- Steps: Inspect the public APK with `unzip -Z1 frontend/public/downloads/shoptest.apk | rg '\.map$'`, then inspect the bundled `asset-manifest.json` and main bundle footer.
- Expected: Production APKs should not include JavaScript/CSS source maps unless there is a documented, access-controlled diagnostic channel; a public sideload APK should contain only the minified runtime assets needed by the app.
- Actual: The public APK bundles the frontend source maps under `assets/public/static`, including JavaScript maps. The bundled manifest advertises the main map, and the bundled main JavaScript file points to that map with a `sourceMappingURL` footer. Anyone who downloads the APK can extract the original frontend source structure and implementation details even if the web server later blocks `.map` files.
- Status: FIXED

### F335: Public Android APK enables cleartext traffic in Capacitor config

- Area: Mobile release artifact / Android network security
- Environment: `frontend/public/downloads/shoptest.apk`, `frontend/build/downloads/shoptest.apk`, and APK `assets/capacitor.config.json`
- Steps: Inspect the public APK's bundled Capacitor config with `unzip -p frontend/public/downloads/shoptest.apk assets/capacitor.config.json | nl -ba`, then compare the bundled runtime endpoints in `assets/public/runtime-config.js`.
- Expected: A production APK should reject plaintext HTTP unless there is an explicit, host-scoped exception; the current production endpoints should remain HTTPS/WSS only.
- Actual: The bundled Capacitor config sets `"server": { "cleartext": true }` while `CapacitorHttp` is enabled. The bundled runtime config points Android, iOS, and web to HTTPS/WSS production endpoints, so the app-wide cleartext allowance is unnecessary and weakens transport guarantees if any URL/config/dependency path falls back to HTTP.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: 2026-05-30 21:57 UTC `unzip -p frontend/public/downloads/shoptest.apk assets/capacitor.config.json | nl -ba` shows line 5 `"server": {`, line 6 `"cleartext": true`, line 9 `"CapacitorHttp": {`, and line 10 `"enabled": true`. `assets/public/runtime-config.js` inside the APK sets Android, iOS, and web `apiBaseUrl` to `https://pet.686888666.xyz/api` and `supportWebSocketUrl` to `wss://pet.686888666.xyz/ws/support`. `sha256sum frontend/public/downloads/shoptest.apk frontend/build/downloads/shoptest.apk` reports matching SHA256 `bd748a5d64d116b2ddce13c82432649f473503ab71b1de579c68615323ab4c56`.
- Retest: 2026-05-30 22:08 UTC rebuilt APKs in `frontend/public/downloads` and `frontend/build/downloads` now share SHA256 `f4d3f656a717d02047ea28cf5aad69ea412347acd29ca7f5af697d29bb28e30d`; `assets/capacitor.config.json` still has line 5 `"server": {`, line 6 `"cleartext": true`, line 9 `"CapacitorHttp": {`, and line 10 `"enabled": true`. Status remains OPEN.
- Development note: 2026-05-30 22:09 UTC user clarified the app side is still in development/testing and should not be handled in this pass. Capacitor/APK changes were not made, so this app-scoped issue remains OPEN for the app release workflow.
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 23:10 UTC changed `/home/guhao/shoptest-mobile/capacitor.config.json` to `"cleartext": false` and rebuilt/synced Android. The final APK `assets/capacitor.config.json` now contains `"cleartext": false`, both checked merged Android manifest outputs have no `usesCleartextTraffic` or `cleartext` references, and bundled runtime endpoints remain HTTPS/WSS production URLs.

### F332: Nginx CSP connect-src allows arbitrary HTTP/HTTPS/WebSocket endpoints

- Area: Deployment / Nginx Content-Security-Policy
- Environment: `deploy/nginx/shoptest-static.conf` and `deploy/nginx/shoptest-edge.conf.template`
- Steps: Deploy either static frontend Nginx config and inspect the `Content-Security-Policy` response header.
- Expected: `connect-src` should be limited to the same-origin API/WebSocket endpoints and explicitly configured production service origins so CSP can limit data exfiltration and injected network calls.
- Actual: Both Nginx configs set `connect-src 'self' http: https: ws: wss:`. That permits browser `fetch`/XHR/WebSocket connections to any HTTP, HTTPS, WS, or WSS origin, including plaintext schemes, even though the deployment proxies API traffic through `/api/`. If a stored HTML/XSS path or compromised dependency executes in the frontend, the CSP does not restrict outbound data exfiltration.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 21:20 UTC restricted both Nginx CSP `connect-src` directives to `'self'`, `https://cloudflareinsights.com`, and `wss://$host`, removing arbitrary `http:`, `https:`, `ws:`, and `wss:` destinations. No project tests/builds were run per instruction; static `git diff --check` and source grep were used.
- Retest: 2026-05-30 21:29 UTC static verification found both Nginx CSP directives still restricted to `'self'`, `https://cloudflareinsights.com`, and `wss://$host`; the broad scheme-wide `http:`, `https:`, `ws:`, and `wss:` allowances remain removed. Status remains FIXED.

### F329: Nginx SPA route whitelist omits admin pet-gallery page, returning 404 on direct refresh

- Area: Deployment / Nginx static frontend routing; Frontend / admin pet-gallery route

- Actual: `frontend/src/App.tsx` defines `<Route path="pet-gallery" element={<PetGalleryManagement />}>` under `/admin`, and `AdminLayout.tsx` links to `/admin/pet-gallery`, but both Nginx route whitelists omit `pet-gallery` from the `/admin` known-route regex. The request falls through to `@spa_not_found`, which serves `/index.html` with HTTP 404.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 21:20 UTC added `pet-gallery` to the admin SPA route whitelist in both `deploy/nginx/shoptest-static.conf` and `deploy/nginx/shoptest-edge.conf.template`, so direct refreshes of `/admin/pet-gallery` are served by `index.html`. No project tests/builds were run per instruction; static `git diff --check` and source grep passed.

### F326: spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=update in production allowing automatic schema changes

- Area: Backend / application.properties JPA configuration
- Environment: Production deployment
- Steps: Start the application with a code change that modifies entity fields.
- Expected: Schema changes should be managed through Flyway migrations, not Hibernate auto-update.
- Actual: `application.properties` (line 23) sets `spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=update`, allowing Hibernate to alter the production schema automatically on startup. A code change could add a column with a wrong type, drop a constraint, or cause data loss in edge cases.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC changed `spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto` to default to `${JPA_DDL_AUTO:validate}`, keeping production schema changes under migrations unless explicitly overridden.

- Steps: Check which ports are accessible from the public internet.
- Expected: Backend (8081), gateway (8080), and Nacos (8848/9848/9849) should only be accessible through nginx or Docker internal networking.
- Actual: `docker-compose.backend.yml` line 12 binds backend to `0.0.0.0:8081`, `docker-compose.nacos-gateway.yml` line 85 binds gateway to `0.0.0.0:8080`, and lines 13-14 bind Nacos to `0.0.0.0:8848/9848/9849`. All services are directly accessible from the public internet, bypassing nginx TLS termination and security headers.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC changed backend, gateway, and Nacos compose defaults to bind to `127.0.0.1` unless an operator explicitly sets a bind IP.

### F324: Redis has no password and is exposed to host network

- Area: Deployment / docker-compose.nacos-gateway.yml Redis configuration
- Environment: Production deployment with Docker
- Steps: Connect to Redis from outside the Docker network on port 6379.
- Expected: Redis should require authentication and not be exposed to the host network.
- Actual: `docker-compose.nacos-gateway.yml` (lines 19-24) starts Redis with `redis-server --appendonly yes` and no `--requirepass`. The port is published as `${REDIS_HOST_PORT:-6379}:6379`, making it reachable from the host network. An attacker can read/write session data, rate-limit counters, verification codes, and other application state.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC now starts Redis with required `REDIS_PASSWORD` and uses Docker internal `expose` instead of publishing Redis to the host network.

### F323: Nacos authentication disabled allowing unauthorized service manipulation

- Area: Deployment / docker-compose.nacos-gateway.yml Nacos configuration
- Environment: Production deployment with Docker
- Steps: Access Nacos console on port 8848 without credentials.
- Expected: Nacos should require authentication to prevent unauthorized service manipulation.
- Actual: `docker-compose.nacos-gateway.yml` (line 8) sets `NACOS_AUTH_ENABLE: "false"`, disabling Nacos authentication entirely. Anyone who can reach port 8848 can register rogue services, read configurations (which may contain secrets), or deregister legitimate backends.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC enabled Nacos auth in compose and required Nacos token/identity/credential environment variables for the Nacos and dependent services.

### F322: AdminLayout has no error boundary around Outlet causing entire admin panel to crash

- Area: Frontend / AdminLayout.tsx Outlet rendering
- Environment: Any admin page throwing a runtime error
- Steps: Cause a runtime error in any admin page (e.g., ProductManagement, OrderManagement).
- Expected: The error should be isolated to the affected page, with a fallback UI offering navigation back to the admin dashboard.
- Actual: `AdminLayout.tsx` (line 193) renders `<Outlet />` with no surrounding error boundary. If any admin page throws during render, the entire admin panel goes white. The top-level `ErrorBoundary` in `App.tsx` catches it but sends users to `/` (storefront), not back into admin.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC wrapped the admin `<Outlet />` in a route-keyed `ErrorBoundary` that sends recovery navigation back to `/admin/dashboard`.

### F321: Admin route guard has race condition with no re-validation on route change

- Area: Frontend / AdminLayout.tsx checkAdmin effect
- Environment: Admin panel with revoked session or role change while panel is open
- Steps: Open the admin panel, then revoke the user's session or change their role server-side, then navigate to a different admin page.
- Expected: The admin layout should re-validate the session and redirect to login if access is revoked.
- Actual: `AdminLayout.tsx` (lines 75-103) runs `checkAdmin` only once on mount with an empty-ish dependency array. If the user's session is revoked or role changes server-side while the admin panel is open, the layout never re-checks. The user retains full admin UI access until a full page reload.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC revalidates admin access on route changes and bypasses the permissions cache during that guard check.

### F320: SeventeenTrackWidget potential runaway re-queries from unstable callback identity

- Area: Frontend / SeventeenTrackWidget.tsx useEffect dependency
- Environment: Order tracking page with tracking widget, unstable parent props
- Steps: Open order tracking with the SeventeenTrack widget, observe network requests when parent re-renders.
- Expected: Tracking queries should only fire when the tracking number changes.
- Actual: `SeventeenTrackWidget.tsx` (lines 107-115) has `useEffect` depending on `[queryTracking, trackingNumber]`. The `queryTracking` callback (line 64) depends on `[carrierCode, guestEmail, language, orderId, orderNo, t]`. If the parent passes unstable references, `queryTracking` gets a new identity every render, causing the effect to fire and issue a new API request on every render cycle.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC moved automatic tracking execution through a callback ref so the effect no longer depends directly on the unstable `queryTracking` identity.

### F319: SkeletonLoader uses Math.random() in render causing visual instability

- Area: Frontend / SkeletonLoader.tsx render function
- Environment: Any page using SkeletonLoader during loading states
- Steps: Show a skeleton loader and trigger a parent re-render while it's visible.
- Expected: The skeleton should appear stable with consistent dimensions.
- Actual: `SkeletonLoader.tsx` (lines 34, 38) calls `Math.random()` directly in the render body to compute width percentages. Every re-render produces different random widths, causing the skeleton to visually "jump" or flicker. This defeats the purpose of a skeleton loader, which should appear stable.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Regression at 2026-05-31 02:35 UTC found `SkeletonLoader.tsx` no longer uses `Math.random()` in render body.

### F318: Single ErrorBoundary wraps entire app with no granular recovery

- Area: Frontend / App.tsx ErrorBoundary placement
- Environment: Any component crash in the application
- Steps: Cause a runtime error in any component (admin panel, support widget, product page).
- Expected: Crashes should be isolated to the affected section, not take down the entire app.
- Actual: `App.tsx` (lines 384-443) has exactly one `ErrorBoundary` at the top of the component tree. A crash in any component replaces the entire app with the error screen. Users lose all context and must retry or navigate to `/`. There are no granular boundaries for admin vs. customer sections.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC added route-level ErrorBoundary isolation for storefront routes and admin routes while retaining the outer app fallback.

### F317: API client has no retry logic for transient failures

- Area: Frontend / api/index.ts response interceptor
- Environment: Network errors, 5xx server errors, 429 rate limits
- Steps: Experience a brief network blip or server 503 during an API call.
- Expected: Transient failures should be retried with exponential backoff.
- Actual: The response interceptor (lines 1046-1078) only handles 401/403 with token refresh. Network timeouts (`ECONNABORTED`), 429 (rate limit), and 5xx server errors are passed straight through to the caller with no automatic retry. A single transient failure surfaces immediately as an error to the user.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC added bounded transient retries for idempotent GET/HEAD/OPTIONS failures on 429, network/timeout, and 5xx responses.

### F316: useAuth login computes effectiveRole but never stores it

- Area: Frontend / hooks/useAuth.ts login function
- Environment: Login flow, role-based UI rendering
- Steps: Log in and check `localStorage.getItem('role')` before the profile fetch completes.
- Expected: The effective role should be stored in localStorage immediately after login.
- Actual: `useAuth.ts` (line 27) computes `const effectiveRole = getEffectiveRole(role, roleCode)` but never uses it. The user state is set with raw `role` and `roleCode`. After login, `localStorage.getItem('role')` retains the previous user's role until the next page load triggers the profile fetch.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Static verification at 2026-05-31 02:30 UTC found `useAuth.ts` line 32 now stores `effectiveRole` via `setLocalStorageItem('role', effectiveRole)` after login, and line 58 stores it after profile fetch.

### F315: OrderTracking refreshTrackedOrder has no error handling causing unhandled promise rejection

- Area: Frontend / OrderTracking.tsx refreshTrackedOrder function
- Environment: `/orders/track` order tracking page, network errors during order refresh
- Steps: Complete a mutation (confirm receipt, submit return), then experience a network error during the subsequent order refresh.
- Expected: The error should be caught and displayed to the user.
- Actual: `OrderTracking.tsx` (lines 203-211) calls `orderApi.track()` without a try/catch. If the refresh fails, the promise rejection is unhandled. This function is called from `confirmReceipt`, `submitReturnRequest`, and `submitReturnTracking`—all of which rely on it to update the UI after a successful mutation.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC added error handling to `refreshTrackedOrder` so follow-up refresh failures are caught and reported without unhandled promise rejections.

### F314: PaymentInstructions page never fetches data from API relying entirely on URL parameters

- Area: Frontend / PaymentInstructions.tsx data source
- Environment: `/payment-instructions/:orderNo` payment instructions page
- Steps: Navigate to the payment instructions page and observe whether any API calls are made.
- Expected: The page should fetch order/payment details from the API to verify the order exists and get authoritative data.
- Actual: `PaymentInstructions.tsx` (entire file) is entirely static—it never fetches any data from the API. All information (order number, amount, currency, expiration, channel) comes from URL parameters. The page cannot verify the order's current state (e.g., already paid, cancelled).
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Regression at 2026-05-31 02:50 UTC found `PaymentInstructions.tsx` line 5 now imports `orderApi, paymentApi` and line 72 calls `orderApi.track(normalizedOrderNo, guestEmail)` to fetch authoritative order data.

### F313: PaymentInstructions displays payment amount and currency from URL params enabling potential fraud

- Area: Frontend / PaymentInstructions.tsx URL parameter trust
- Environment: `/payment-instructions/:orderNo?amount=...&currency=...` payment instructions page
- Steps: Craft a URL with a modified `amount` parameter (e.g., `amount=0.01`) and share it with a user.
- Expected: Payment amounts should come from the server, not URL parameters.
- Actual: `PaymentInstructions.tsx` (lines 58-59) reads `amount` and `currency` directly from URL search parameters. A crafted URL could display a lower amount to trick a user into paying less, or a higher amount to overcharge. While `normalizeAmount` sanitizes the format, the value itself is not server-verified.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC removed URL amount/currency trust; the page now shows server-derived payment/order amount or `-` when verification is unavailable.

### F312: ProductReview renders all reviews at once with no pagination causing performance issues

- Area: Frontend / ProductReview.tsx List component
- Environment: Products with hundreds or thousands of reviews
- Steps: Open a product detail page for a popular product with many reviews.
- Expected: Reviews should be paginated or use infinite scrolling.
- Actual: `ProductReview.tsx` (lines 125-154) renders all reviews from the `reviews` array without any pagination or virtualization. For popular products with hundreds of reviews, this causes significant DOM bloat and slow rendering. Ant Design's `List` component supports a `pagination` prop out of the box.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Regression at 2026-05-31 02:35 UTC found `ProductReview.tsx` line 129 now has `pagination={reviews.length > 8 ? { ... } : false}`.

### F311: ProductDetail selectedVariant matching ignores extra selected options showing wrong variant

- Area: Frontend / ProductDetail.tsx variant matching logic
- Environment: Products with variants having heterogeneous option sets
- Steps: Select options that include a key not present in any variant's options (e.g., select Color=Red and Size=Large but variants only define Color).
- Expected: No variant should match, and the user should be prompted to select a valid combination.
- Actual: `ProductDetail.tsx` (lines 251-253) iterates over the variant's options and checks that each one matches the user's selection, but does not verify the reverse direction. A variant with `{Color: "Red"}` matches a user selection of `{Color: "Red", Size: "Large"}` because the variant's options are a subset. This causes the wrong variant (and its price/stock) to be shown.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC changed selected-variant matching to compare selected options and variant options bidirectionally.

### F310: ProductRichDetail video iframe missing sandbox attribute

- Area: Frontend / ProductRichDetail.tsx iframe security
- Environment: Product detail pages with embedded video content
- Steps: Open a product detail page with a video embed.
- Expected: The iframe should have a `sandbox` attribute limiting the embedded content's capabilities.
- Actual: `ProductRichDetail.tsx` (line 169) renders `<iframe src={videoUrl} ... allowFullScreen />` with no `sandbox` attribute. While `isHttpMediaUrl` validates the URL uses http/https, a compromised or crafted video URL could still allow the embedded content to execute scripts, navigate the parent page, or open popups.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Regression at 2026-05-31 02:35 UTC found `ProductRichDetail.tsx` line 172 now has `sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin allow-presentation"`.

### F309: ForgotPassword has no username enumeration protection exposing valid usernames

- Area: Frontend / ForgotPassword.tsx error handling
- Environment: `/forgot-password` password reset page
- Steps: Submit a valid username with an invalid email, then submit an invalid username with a valid email, and compare error messages.
- Expected: The backend should return a generic message regardless of whether the username/email combination is valid.
- Actual: `ForgotPassword.tsx` (lines 96-133) directly displays whatever error the server returns. If the API returns distinct error messages when the username does not exist vs. when the email does not match, an attacker can enumerate valid usernames.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC masked forgot-password reset failures with a generic localized message and tightened backend reset-code purpose validation.

### F308: ForgotPassword sends reset code to the same endpoint as login email code

- Area: Frontend / ForgotPassword.tsx sendResetCode function
- Environment: `/forgot-password` password reset page
- Steps: Initiate the forgot-password flow and observe the API call for sending the verification code.
- Expected: There should be a dedicated password-reset-code endpoint that distinguishes from login codes.
- Actual: `ForgotPassword.tsx` line 71 calls `userApi.sendEmailLoginCode(normalizedEmail)`—the exact same endpoint used for email login. If the backend does not distinguish between login codes and reset codes, an attacker who obtains a valid login code could potentially use it to reset the password (or vice versa).
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC added `/auth/password-reset-code` and updated `ForgotPassword.tsx` to request purpose-scoped password reset codes.

### F307: Cart total calculated differently between Checkout and Cart pages causing inconsistent totals

- Area: Frontend / Checkout.tsx vs Cart.tsx total calculation
- Environment: Cart page and Checkout page, items with unusual quantity values
- Steps: Add items to cart, observe total on Cart page, then proceed to Checkout and compare.
- Expected: Both pages should use the same calculation logic.
- Actual: `Checkout.tsx` (lines 355-358) applies defensive quantity normalization `Math.max(1, Math.floor(quantity))`, but `Cart.tsx` (line 342) uses `item.price * item.quantity` with no floor/ceiling guard. If a cart item has `quantity = 0` or a fractional quantity, the Cart page calculates differently than Checkout.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC normalized cart line quantity, price, and totals defensively so Cart and Checkout no longer diverge on malformed quantities.

### F306: addGuestCartItem returns null as unknown as CartItem causing potential runtime crashes

- Area: Frontend / guestCart.ts type safety
- Environment: Guest cart operations with invalid product data
- Steps: Call `addGuestCartItem` with null productId or empty productName.
- Expected: The function should throw an error or return a properly typed nullable value.
- Actual: `guestCart.ts` (lines 84-89) returns `null as unknown as CartItem` on invalid input—a type lie. Callers (Checkout.tsx, Cart.tsx, CartDrawer.tsx) never check for null return. If a product with no name or invalid ID is added, this silently inserts a corrupt null into the cart items array, potentially causing runtime crashes when rendering.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Regression at 2026-05-31 02:35 UTC found `guestCart.ts` now returns `null` properly (line 48, 89) instead of `null as unknown as CartItem`.

### F305: Guest email not validated before guest checkout submission allowing empty email orders

- Area: Frontend / Checkout.tsx guest checkout validation
- Environment: Guest checkout flow
- Steps: Submit guest checkout with an empty or invalid email field.
- Expected: The checkout should abort if the guest email is empty.
- Actual: `Checkout.tsx` (lines 756-783) normalizes `normalizedGuestEmail` but passes `normalizedGuestEmail || ''` to `orderApi.guestCheckout` (line 774). If the email field is empty, the order proceeds with an empty email. The Ant Design form validation should prevent this, but the fallback to `''` rather than aborting means an empty email can reach the backend.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC added explicit guest email guards in Checkout and `orderApi.guestCheckout`, rejecting missing or invalid guest emails before submission.

### F304: SystemMonitor exposes database connection URL with credentials in copyable text

- Area: Frontend / SystemMonitor.tsx database URL display
- Environment: `/admin/system-monitor` system monitor page
- Steps: Open the system monitor page and observe the database URL field.
- Expected: Database credentials should be masked in the URL display.
- Actual: `SystemMonitor.tsx` line 249 renders the database URL with `<Text copyable>`. Connection strings often contain credentials (username/password). If this page is accessible to a broader admin audience, it constitutes data exposure.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Development handling at 2026-05-30 20:52 UTC masks database connection URLs in SystemMonitor and removed copyable raw URL exposure.

### F303: TrafficControl destructive actions (reset circuits, clear rate limits) have no confirmation dialogs

- Area: Frontend / TrafficControl.tsx reset and clear buttons
- Environment: `/admin/traffic-control` traffic control page
- Steps: Click "Reset All Circuits" or "Clear Rate Limit" buttons.
- Expected: Destructive actions should require confirmation before execution.
- Actual: `TrafficControl.tsx` lines 140-145 have "Clear Rate Limit" and "Reset All Circuits" buttons that fire immediately with no `Popconfirm`. Clearing rate limits disables abuse protection. Resetting all circuits masks ongoing service issues. Both are irreversible operations.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Regression at 2026-05-31 02:50 UTC found `TrafficControl.tsx` lines 113, 147 now have `Popconfirm` wrappers around destructive action buttons.

### F302: ConfigCenter publish and runtime apply have no confirmation dialogs breaking production with misclick

- Area: Frontend / ConfigCenter.tsx publish and apply buttons
- Environment: `/admin/config-center` configuration center page
- Steps: Click "Publish & Sync" or "Apply Runtime" buttons accidentally.
- Expected: Both actions should require explicit confirmation with warning text before modifying production configuration.
- Actual: `ConfigCenter.tsx` lines 59-88 (`handlePublish`) and 90-113 (`handleApplyRuntime`) directly modify production configuration with no confirmation. `handlePublish` pushes config to Nacos and optionally applies at runtime. `handleApplyRuntime` applies config to the running application. A misclick can break production.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Regression at 2026-05-31 02:50 UTC found `ConfigCenter.tsx` lines 127, 141 now have `Popconfirm` wrappers around publish and apply buttons.

### F301: AnnouncementManagement allows endsAt before startsAt creating invalid time windows

- Area: Frontend / AnnouncementManagement.tsx date validation
- Environment: `/admin/announcements` create/edit form
- Steps: Create an announcement with `startsAt` set to tomorrow and `endsAt` set to yesterday.
- Expected: The form should validate that `endsAt` is after `startsAt`.
- Actual: `AnnouncementManagement.tsx` (lines 364-371) has `startsAt` and `endsAt` DatePicker fields but no cross-field validation. An admin can set `endsAt` to a date before `startsAt`, creating an invalid time window. The backend may accept this silently.
- Status: FIXED
- Evidence: Regression at 2026-05-31 02:50 UTC found `AnnouncementManagement.tsx` line 375 now has a validator that checks `endsAt` is after `startsAt`.

### F300: ProductQuestionManagement has no delete functionality for spam or abusive questions

- Area: Frontend / ProductQuestionManagement.tsx missing delete action
- Environment: `/admin/questions` product question management
- Steps: Try to delete a spam or abusive product question from the admin panel.
